chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to the NYC Council Committee on Finance hearing with Public Safety and Fire and Emergency committees, and reminds attendees to silence phones.
chapters.title: Council staff opens hearing with housekeeping announcements

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to the New York City Council Committee on Finance with the Committee on Public Safety and Fire and Emergency.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please place your phone on silent mode or vibrate.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do not approach the days at any time during this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Linda Lee opens the fifth day of FY27 Executive Budget Hearings, introducing the NYPD portion and welcoming Commissioner Jessica Tisch, Speaker Julie Menin, and Chair Oswald Feliz.
chapters.title: Chair Linda Lee opens FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for NYPD

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Happy Monday morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to the fifth day of our FY27 Executive Budget Hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and today we will begin with the New York Police Department, followed by New York City Fire Department.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm pleased to be joined by Speaker Julie-Menin and my colleague, Chair of the Committee on Public Safety, Councilmember Oswald Feliz.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Today we have been joined by Councilmember Wong, Councilmember Marte, Councilmember Caban, Councilmember Marano, and I think that's it for now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome, Commissioner Jessica Tisch and your team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you to all for joining us today to answer our questions.

chapters.summary: Speaker Menin outlines NYPD's $6.6B planned budget, notes declines in murders and shootings but troubling increases in felony assaults and reported rapes, and raises questions about the Office of Community Safety, summer surge staffing, and recruitment.
chapters.title: Speaker Julie Menin delivers opening remarks on NYPD budget priorities

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before I go any further, I'm going to invite Speaker Menon to share her opening remarks.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: First of all, I want to thank you, Chair Lee, for conducting all of these budget hearings.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I want to thank our Public Safety Chair, Chair Feliz, for your oversight of the Committee on Public Safety.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And welcome, Commissioner Tisch and everyone who is on your team who's here.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So to keep New Yorkers safe, the NYPD...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: PD has a planned budget of $6.6 billion that supports 35,379

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: uniform and 13,386 civilian personnel in fiscal year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Today, we want to hear much more detail about whether and how the executive budget gives the police department the resources it needs to continue its efforts under your leadership.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The department has overseen a recent period of significant decline in both murders and shootings in the city,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: which we are very pleased to see, but there are still some troubling increases in other major felony crime categories, like felony assaults hitting a 25-year high, as well as increases in reported rapes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Reported hate crimes have also been increasing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The proposed executive budget included new funding for the department, as well as transfers of funding from existing departments to create the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm interested in hearing what, if any, programs are being shifted from NYPD's purview to the new OCS,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: and if there are any changes to NYPD's community affairs work in anticipation of the new office's creation.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The Council is also interested to hear how decisions about resources and staffing impact crime levels, particularly with recent NYPD announcements about the summer surge, the Division of the Bronx into two.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: to patrol boroughs and the introduction of 12-hour shifts for a majority of officers starting July 1st.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As always, recruitment and retention of police officers is also a prime concern.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: These issues are important to drill down on because, of course, the ability to proactively respond to the city's public safety needs is determined in large part by the department's budget and the decisions on how to allocate resources.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So we look forward to a very productive hearing, and we want to thank you once again for being here and for your commitment to our city.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee details NYPD's proposed FY27 budget of $6.59B, a decrease of $110.9M from the preliminary plan, and calls for greater transparency through additional units of appropriation and use of asset forfeiture dollars.
chapters.title: Chair Lee presents NYPD budget details and Council priorities

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26 to FY30 with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The New York Police Department's proposed FY27 budget, as the Speaker mentioned, of $6.59 billion

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: represents 5.3% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan, and this is a decrease of $110.9 million, or 1.7%, from the $6.7 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This decrease results from several actions, mostly from uniform and civilian PS accruals, vacancy reductions, and vehicle capitalization.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 26, NYPD's headcount was approximately 1,500 less than their FY26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, we called on the admin to add units of appropriation for greater transparency into NYPD spending.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In addition, we call on the

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: on the administration and YPD to utilize asset forfeiture dollars for eligible costs that expand your investigative resources before calling on city tax levy dollars.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Although additional units of appropriation were not added to the executive plan, we hope the adopted budget will include them and we look forward to exploring how asset forfeiture can be used in the future.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Council Member Chair Feliz, for his opening statement.

chapters.summary: Chair Feliz highlights key budget issues including headcount, overtime, subway funding, school safety, drone technology, and fan safety for upcoming World Cup and NBA Finals, while praising officers' recent heroism.
chapters.title: Chair Feliz delivers opening remarks on NYPD budget and public safety

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Oswald Feliz, Chair of the Committee on Public Safety.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I would like to welcome everyone to the Fiscal Year 2027 Executive Budget Hearing.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I want to thank Speaker Manin and Chair Lee for joining us today.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Today we'll discuss the fiscal 2027 executive budget for the New York City Police Department to understand the administration's budget proposals for a department that plays a crucial role in public safety.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I'm interested in learning about the changes in the NYPD's $6.59 billion budget and discussing issues related to headcount, overtime, state subway funding,

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: school safety agents and crossing guards, technology to deter crime such as the use of drones, and much more.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I would also like to explore the department's plans to ensure fan safety for big upcoming events like the World Cup and the NBA Finals.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: On the NYPD, we are thankful for

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: for the work and the sacrifices that police officers make every day to keep New Yorkers safe.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Public safety, as we know, is a shared responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We achieve it with good schools, good jobs, and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Police officers play an important role in this issue.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: They help ensure that residents can engage in these activities, work, school, and opportunity, without having to worry about their safety.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We're thankful for that.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Their dedication to this city does not go unnoticed.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Yesterday, for example, I saw a video of police officers in the Bronx in the 48th precinct running towards two individuals who had just engaged in gun violence.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Police officers who ran towards danger and used technology such as drones to quickly apprehend them.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: That incident serves as an example of how a strong police presence and technology, such as drones, can ensure that there is fast accountability, accountability that helps deter crime.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We also saw that same courage earlier this year when police officers ran towards an ignited homemade explosive that was thrown to harm others at a protest.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: They risked their own lives without hesitation to keep others safe.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Our communities are thankful for these police officers, and we're also better off because of their hard work and sacrifices.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: There are many important budget actions in this plan, and we hope to better understand them and the impact that they'll have on public safety.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I look forward to productive conversations on what's going well, for example, shootings and murders being at record lows.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We also look forward to discussing the areas that need

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: attention, including the skyrocketing costs related to overtime.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Skyrocketing costs that are still going as high as almost a billion dollars, or specifically $955 million projected this fiscal year, according to a recent article by Political, but of course we'll hear the official numbers from the NYPD

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: soon.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I also want to thank our Public Safety Committee staff for their hard work, our financial analysts, Owen Kotowski and Casey Leitsky, policy analysts Chad Benjamin, committee councils Jeremy Whiteman and Josh Kingsley.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I also want to thank my staff, my chief of staff, Espranza Diaz, and my legislative director and many others who have joined today.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And I would also like to welcome the NYPD leadership team.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Welcome, Commissioner and team.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We look forward to conversations today.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it back to Charity now.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee thanks Council Finance Division staff, reminds attendees that public testimony for all agencies is scheduled for Wednesday June 10th at 9:30 AM, and turns to committee counsel for witness swearing-in.
chapters.title: Chair Lee thanks staff and announces public testimony date

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before we get started, I wanted to take a moment to thank the entire Council Finance Division, especially Jack Story and Owen Kotowski for today's hearing, and of course our amazing Committee Counsel, Brian Sarfo, and Senior Advisor, Athena Say.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as a reminder, for this year's budget hearing, we will take public testimony for all the agencies on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9.30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So Wednesday, June 10th, 9.30 a.m., please sign up for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's going to be a long, fun day, so please join us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And now I will turn it over to Committee Counsel to swear in the witnesses.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Commissioner Tisch, First Deputy Commissioner Kinsella, Deputy Commissioners Gerber, Ryan, Crone, and Chief LaPetrie before testimony begins.
chapters.title: Committee counsel swears in NYPD witnesses

utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth and nothing but the truth before the council members, Commissioner Tisch?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: First Deputy Commissioner Kinsella?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Gerber?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Ryan?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Crone?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Chief LaPertri?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Tisch introduces her leadership team and begins testimony on the FY27 executive budget, stating the NYPD is delivering results with crime down across the city.
chapters.title: Commissioner Tisch begins testimony on NYPD progress and budget

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Good morning, Speaker Menon, Chair Felice, Chair Lee, and members of the City Council Committees on Public Safety and Finance.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'm Jessica Tisch, Commissioner of the New York City Police Department.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'm joined at the table today by First Deputy Commissioner Tanya Kinsella, Chief of Department Mike LaPetrie, Deputy Commissioner of Management and Budget Christine Ryan, Deputy Commissioner of Legal Matters Michael Gerber, and Deputy Commissioner for Strategic Initiatives Alex Krohn,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: as well as all other members of my executive staff seated here behind us.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today on the NYPD's fiscal year 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: When I appeared before this council earlier this year, I said very clearly that the NYPD was focused on delivering results,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: driving down crime, restoring public confidence, strengthening accountability, and making this department more effective in every part of its work.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Today, we can show real progress.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The seeds that we planted in 2025 have taken root.

chapters.summary: Tisch presents detailed crime data: shootings, homicides at record lows, major crime down 6% citywide, murders down 25%, robberies down 11%, burglaries down 19%, and nearly 2,000 guns removed from streets.
chapters.title: Tisch presents citywide crime statistics showing major declines

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I feel like the proud mother of 35,000 members of the New York City Police Department, so allow me a moment to brag about their fine work.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For the first five months of the year, shooting incidents, shooting victims, and homicides are all at record lows.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The same is true for the month of May for both shooting incidents and shooting victims.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And burglaries in New York City are at their second lowest levels ever.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Now, I am a data-driven person, so here is some more of it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Through the end of our last Comstat report on May 25th, major crime is down 6% citywide.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Murders are down 25%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Shooting incidents are down 8%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And shooting victims are down 10%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So far this year, NYPD officers have taken nearly 2,000 guns off of our streets.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Robbery is down 11%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Burglaries are down 19%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Grand larceny is down nearly 5%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Auto theft is down 11%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And retail theft is down more than 19%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And that progress is evident across the city.

chapters.summary: Tisch reports federal Homeland Security grant funding cut by 40%, with NYPD anticipated to receive only $53M. She also notes the department did not receive FY25 port security funds and had to forego 15 additional federal grants due to certification language conflicts.
chapters.title: Tisch warns of severe federal funding cuts to counterterrorism

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Annually, the department receives on average just over $192 million in federal funding, the majority of which is from the Department of Homeland Security.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Murders in the city are down 10%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Murders in the city are down 10%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch

utterances.text: Murders in the city are down 10%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Murders in the city are down 10%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: This year's federal Homeland Security grant funding has been cut by 40%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Murders in the city are down 10%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Murders in the city are down 10%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: from federal fiscal year 2024 levels, with the NYPD anticipated to receive only $53 million.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In addition, as a result of new terms and conditions placed on several uncertain federal grants,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: particularly related to civil immigration enforcement, there are numerous grants for which the department either didn't receive funding or was advised by the law department that we would be unable to comply and therefore no applications were submitted.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Specifically, the NYPD did not receive any FY25 port security funds despite the department receiving funds in prior years and submitting an application for $6.56 million.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: There were 15 additional federal grants that the NYPD had to forego based on the required certification language.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The maximum potential award amounts ranged from $200,000 to $6.25 million.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We cannot overstate the gravity of this.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It is not bureaucratic inconvenience.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It is a direct threat to the NYPD's ability to maintain and enhance critical counterterrorism and intelligence operations in New York City, the most visible terror target in the United States.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: People are alive today because of these grants.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We must never lose sight of that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The time to act is now, and I will not stop pressing this issue until the federal government restores the funding and the resources that we need to keep New Yorkers safe.

chapters.summary: Tisch closes by stating the NYPD is delivering results with crime down, violence falling, and the department building capacity to sustain progress, while emphasizing the budget must reflect the full weight of what the department is being asked to do.
chapters.title: Tisch closes testimony emphasizing results and budget needs

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The through line of this testimony is simple.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The NYPD is delivering results and we are building the capacity to sustain them.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Crime is down across the city.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Violence is falling.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Public housing, transit, schools in the Bronx are all seeing enormous progress.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: At the same time, we are strengthening training and improving accountability, managing overtime more effectively, hiring more officers, modernizing our tools, and preparing for a threat environment unlike anything the city has faced in years.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That work requires a budget that reflects the full weight of what this department is being asked to do.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The men and women of the NYPD carry that responsibility every day on patrol, in our subways, in our schools, at protests, at major events, in counterterrorism operations, and in every neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We are proud of the progress that we've made, but progress has to be protected and sustained.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: My staff and I look forward to answering any questions you may have.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to pass on to Speaker Minin.

chapters.summary: Speaker Menin asks about the connection between staffing levels and crime reduction progress, noting NYPD's uniform headcount is 1.5% below budgeted levels.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin questions Tisch on headcount and crime reduction

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Commissioner, for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I know we've got a number of different questions, so I look forward to the dialogue on this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So first of all, NYPD's current actual uniform headcount of 34,497 is 1.5% less than the budgeted headcount of 35,025.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Since the last time you appeared before us for the budget hearing, it's clear that the department has made progress in filling vacancies and hiring new officers, and we commend you for that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: How do you think about the connection between the department's staffing level and the progress NYPD has made to drive down murders and shootings?

chapters.summary: Tisch explains the department received 580 new police officer positions bringing budgeted headcount to 35,555, and details how increased officer deployment in high-shooting neighborhoods has directly driven down shooting incidents.
chapters.title: Tisch explains headcount growth and shooting reduction strategy

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So first, just some background on the headcount of the department.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Actually, in this budget, the NYPD has received 580

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: new police officer positions, bringing our budgeted headcount up to 35,555.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so that is something to be celebrated and that is something that we appreciate deeply, especially in a time of general need to cut.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I will also say we have a hiring plan this year that should get us to our budgeted headcount of 35,555 by the end of this year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So last year was our most aggressive year in hiring on record, and we are keeping our foot on the gas this year, and we plan to do a significant amount of hiring.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Last year, we actually ended the year with 800 more police officers than we had over the previous three years.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So, point is, the size of the department has been growing over the past year and will continue to grow.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: To grow this year meaningfully, and that is a very

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: good thing for all New Yorkers because to answer your specific question, the number of police officers we have is directly related to our progress driving down

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: shooting incidents and shooting victims in this city.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Shootings are one crime that you can really deploy for.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We know where the vast majority of shootings in New York City occur.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We know what times of day they occur at.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so our plan, the reason we have been able to drive down shootings so far, is we deploy officers in those neighborhoods, on those streets, at those times.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And that plan has worked to, you know, with tremendous historic success.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So most of the officers who are working on those streets in our zones, we call them, are actually the new officers that we hired last year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They spend their first 18 months in the department learning those neighborhoods, learning those streets, learning police work, and they have done an extraordinary job driving shootings down.

chapters.summary: Chair Feliz raises concerns about felony assaults hitting a 25-year high and asks about progress from the new domestic violence unit created in October.
chapters.title: Feliz questions Tisch on felony assaults and domestic violence unit

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, so we've seen the work on that note that the department is doing to decrease shootings in the city, including getting guns off the street and the corresponding drop in murders.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: However, there's also been a troubling increase in other major felony crime categories, like felony assaults hitting a 25-year high, as mentioned before.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Other major felony crimes are also above pre-pandemic levels.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: levels.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Last October, when you created the domestic violence unit, the department stated domestic violence has remained a consistent driver of overall felony assaults, accounting for nearly 40% of these crimes citywide.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So with the increased funding and creation of the new domestic violence unit, have you seen progress in driving down the concerning rates of felony assaults that are domestic incidents?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And to that end, what has worked and what hasn't worked?

chapters.summary: Tisch explains felony assault increases are driven by domestic violence (up 50%), assaults on police officers (up 100%), and assaults on public employees (up 200%), and details the DV unit reorganization including a dedicated DV warrants squad.
chapters.title: Tisch details felony assault drivers and DV unit reorganization

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So just a...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Just to level set here, as I mentioned in my testimony, we have seen extraordinary crime declines in most crime categories in New York City over the past two years, but particularly this year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You are correct that rape is one area where we have seen that continue to go up, and we can discuss the reasons for that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Felony assault, as of the last CompStat report, is basically flat compared to last year, but you are correct that that is an area where we have not seen

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: the same types of crime declines as we have seen, for example, with shootings and burglaries and robberies and grand larceny.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So the increase in felony assaults, if you go back, let's say, the last six years, because that's when it's really, that's the period where it's really exploded.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Has been driven by three main things.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The first, as you mentioned, is domestic violence.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The second,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: is assaults on police officers, and the third is assaults on other public employees, for example, MTA workers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Attacks on other public employees are up 200% in that time.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Attacks on police officers are up 100% in that time.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And attacks on, I'm sorry, and DV is up, Chief, is it 100% in that time?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Sorry, DV is up 50% in that time.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So those are the three main categories where you're seeing huge increases in felony assaults over the past six years.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For DV specifically, to address what we were seeing last year, at the end of the last year, the NYPD did a major reorganization of our domestic violence operations, basically to streamline things.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We know that domestic violence is a recidivist crime, and it's horrifying to be the victim of crime in your home, in your personal space.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so by streamlining things, we hoped we had a number of goals, but among them to create a

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: victim-centered approach where we weren't having multiple different officers asking victims the same questions, streamlining the experience for victims and hoping

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: that we can do a better job working with victims to actually deliver.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: consequences for offenders, I am confident that that reorganization was the right thing to do.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The other big change that occurred in the department as part of this DV reorganization is we created a special dedicated

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: DV warrants squad for DV misdemeanors where we have teams of people whose only job it is every day to go out and find offenders wanted for DV misdemeanors.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So the hope

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: is through all of this to drive consequences for DV crimes and in that way bring the level of DV down.

chapters.summary: Feliz notes hate crimes jumped from 128 in Q1 2025 to 148 in Q1 2026 and asks what accounts for the increase.
chapters.title: Feliz questions Tisch on hate crime increase

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, moving on to hate crimes.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: One concern I have is that hate crimes have jumped

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: this quarter from 128 from the first quarter of 2025 to 148 over the first quarter of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And Comstat shows the increase as well.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So what do you account for the increase in hate crimes?

chapters.summary: Tisch provides updated hate crime data through April 30 showing all hate crimes down 5.4%, anti-Semitic hate crimes down 8.4%, with arrests up 7.9% overall and 10.3% for anti-Semitic crimes.
chapters.title: Tisch provides updated hate crime statistics

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Let me give you the numbers, more recent numbers for

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: hate crimes.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: This is as of the end of last month, so it doesn't include May.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We'll report on May tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So as of April 30th, all hate crimes citywide are down 5.4%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: this year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Hate crimes against Jews or anti-Semitic hate crimes represent 56% of the total.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Anti-Semitic hate crimes are down this year 8.4% again as of the end of April.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In addition to that, and I think importantly, enforcement of hate crimes is up in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So arrests for hate crimes in New York City this year are up 7.9% and arrests for anti-Semitic hate crimes are up 10.3% this year.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks which metric Tisch uses for reporting anti-Semitic hate crimes. Tisch confirms NYPD uses confirmed hate crimes for reporting increases and decreases.
chapters.title: Feliz and Tisch clarify hate crime reporting metrics

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And which metric are you using for the reporting of the anti-Semitic hate crimes?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Because I know that was something we talked about.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yeah, we have always used for that type of reporting confirmed hate crimes.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So we have two types of reports that we issue, confirmed hate crimes and also reported hate crimes, which we started putting out publicly in March.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And it has always been when we talk about increases or decreases, we have always talked in New York City about confirmed hate crimes.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks whether any programs have shifted from NYPD to the new Office of Community Safety. Tisch responds no programs have shifted and no conversations have commenced with NYPD about OCS.
chapters.title: Feliz questions Tisch on Office of Community Safety program transfers

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, moving to the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: The executive plan included transfers of funding from existing departments and new funding to create the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Have any programs shifted from the NYPD's purview to OCS?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: To what extent is NYPD engaged with the administration and what specific roles and coordination?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Will occur between NYPD and OCS?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Those conversations have not yet commenced.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So the administration's announcement of approximately $260 million for the creation of OCS, there have been no conversations with NYPD about the agency and who will be doing what?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Not at this time.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I believe, I mean, I expect that those conversations will begin to occur.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But as I mentioned before, in response to the last question, no program, no programmatic responsibility

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: is being shifted from the NYPD to OCS at this time and none that I know of going forward.

chapters.summary: Feliz presses on what OCS's purview will be. Tisch explains OCS consolidates existing offices from other agencies, and NYPD's work with those offices continues, but no strategic conversations about shifting NYPD responsibilities have occurred.
chapters.title: Feliz presses Tisch on OCS purview and NYPD coordination

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So in your view, so then what will the purview of OCS be?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The OCS has many different offices, some of which have existed in this city for a while, in other agencies that have all been consolidated.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I want to be clear, our work with those offices continues.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so that work certainly has not.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Stopped to the extent that the NYPD has worked with those offices in the past.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That continues to this day.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But we haven't had strategic conversations about what's going to shift from the NYPD to OCS because nothing is planned at this time to be shifted.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks about mental health calls and OCS. Tisch explains the city's Be Heard program, where mental health professionals and EMTs respond to nonviolent EDP 911 calls, has existed for at least five years and continues unchanged.
chapters.title: Feliz and Tisch discuss mental health calls and Be Heard program

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So what about mental health calls?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So on mental health calls, as you know, the city has long had, I believe since the de Blasio administration, a program by the name of Be Heard,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: where you have mental health professionals working with EMTs to respond to

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: 911 calls for service where you have one, an emotionally disturbed person who is both nonviolent and does not have any weapons.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That program has existed for at least five years and that work continues.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, so I know my other colleagues are probably going to have questions on this.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I assume Chair Lee will as well.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: But it still remains unclear then what the relationship between NYPD and OCS is around mental health calls and some of the other programming.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And as I mentioned, the city has long had a program by the name of Be Heard, where 911 calls for service

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: are diverted away from police officers to EMTs and mental health professionals for them to respond.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That program continues.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I am not aware at this time of any changes to that program, but to the extent that there are, we'll certainly let you know.

chapters.summary: Feliz raises overtime concerns: FY25 uniform overtime budgeted at $477.5M but actual spending was $959.7M, and FY26 projected at $955M against a budget of $511.6M, asking for year-to-date spending comparisons.
chapters.title: Feliz questions Tisch on overtime spending vs. budget

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, got it.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, overtime.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I know you mentioned overtime in your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So overtime at the department has been a concern, obviously, for a significant number of years.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: In fiscal year 25, uniform overtime was budgeted at $477.5 million, but the NYPD spent $959.7 million.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: In fiscal year 2026, NYPD expects to spend $955 million on overtime pay for officers, according to recent reporting.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: The budgeted amount for fiscal year 2026 was initially $500.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: 500 and a half.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: 11.6 million, meaning the department is on pace to exceed its allotted amount by nearly 100%.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: The council for many years has continuously pushed the department and the administration to adjust the overtime budget to more accurately reflect spending, and the mayor's prelim plan included a baseline increase in overtime.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: For the coming fiscal year, but nowhere near the average $1 billion spend per year.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So could you provide us with year-to-date spending on uniform overtime and how this compares to last year's?

chapters.summary: Tisch argues NYPD overtime has never been realistically budgeted, notes the administration took steps to correct this, and presents data showing overtime down 12.4% in CY2025 and 10% in FY26 first three quarters, with FY26 projected 6% below the 10-year historical average in today's dollars.
chapters.title: Tisch defends overtime management progress

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I have a few points on overtime, and then our Deputy Commissioner of Management and Budget, who really knows this inside and out, will add anything I missed.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: First, I want to, first, NYPD overtime has never been realistically or appropriately accounted for

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: in a preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Meaning, the numbers that we get or that you see reflected in the first budget of the year

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We know when that budget comes out that they are wildly unrealistic.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: This administration took steps in their first budget to correct that by adding a significant amount of funding in the last budget for

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: NYPD overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That was a meaningful chunk of the problem.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It did not address the entire problem, but is the first time we saw a meaningful attempt to reflect more realistic NYPD overtime numbers in the past four years.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Second, if we take out the transit overtime program, which is net new and which the governor

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: pays for.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And we put overtime over the past nine or 10 years in today's contract dollars, meaning cops are paid more, so overtime costs go up.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In FY26, we are projected to be below average for the past 10 years.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so I just want to make sure that that's clear.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Other information on overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In calendar year 2025, as I testified, overtime spend was down 12.4%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It was $143.6 million less than calendar year 2024.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In FY26, the first three quarters, overtime spend is down 10% compared to the same.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Period in FY25.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In FY26, our full year projection is $1.013 billion, which is $91 million less than FY20.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: five, correct, Chris?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the FY26 overtime spend is projected to be 6%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I said that's 6% less than the historical average.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: A couple extra questions on that.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks how hiring to full headcount would offset overtime costs. Tisch uses the summer 12-hour tours as an example of where increased headcount could reduce overtime needs.
chapters.title: Feliz and Tisch discuss hiring impact on overtime

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So with increased initiatives to recruitment, can you explain how hiring to your full-time headcount would offset some of the overtime costs?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Well, look at what's going to happen this summer as an example, where we're going to have to move to...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: 12-hour tours for at least one week.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We expect it to be from July 1st to July 7th, and we may need to add additional days closer to the World Cup finals.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We are doing that because we will take everyone, we have three different tours each day.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We're going to take the first platoon, the first tour and the third tour will cover all patrol operations.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Everything in the, meaning responding to 911 calls is an example.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Everyone who works in that middle tour will have to be deployed for, you know, the SAIL 250 and the World's Cup, etc.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So those 12-hour tours that we are forced to work July 1st to July 7th have a significant overtime impact on this department.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: An example is an increased headcount would, I guess, offset that type of thing.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee asks for clarification on whether the 35,555 officer headcount is for FY27 or FY28. Tisch confirms it's in the FY28 budget but expects to achieve it by end of calendar year 2026.
chapters.title: Lee clarifies headcount timeline with Tisch

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I have more questions, but I'm going to pass it over to Chair Lee, and I'll come back on second round.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, just a clarification question.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the 35,555 officers that you were referring to, was that for FY27 or FY28?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It's in the budget for FY28, but we expect to achieve that hiring at the end of this year, this calendar year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so in the middle of FY27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about state subway funding for NYPD transit deployments. Tisch and Deputy Commissioner Ryan explain the state provides $154M total, covering 5,000 hours of daily transit overtime.
chapters.title: Lee questions Tisch and Ryan on state subway funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You sort of went into this a little bit just now, but the plan for this, this is about overtime funding with the state subway funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The plan includes some funding from the state for subway deployments, but not the full amount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We do 5,000 hours a day of transit overtime, and we have been doing that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: This is our second year of doing that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And my understanding is that the governor, the funds that the governor has provided pays for it, but Christine can add to that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and if you could just note how much was received or how much is expected to be received.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yeah, Christine will let you know.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Yes, so we expect in total to receive $154 million from the state.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Half of that is already reflected in the budget, and we're working very closely with the state for the other half.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: But as the commissioner said, it's currently covering an additional 5,000 hours.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: per day in the transit system.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how the department utilizes state, city, and federal funding to offset city funding for overtime. Ryan explains funding cannot supplant but is coordinated with deployment plans.
chapters.title: Lee and Ryan discuss overtime funding offsets

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so that portion for the 5,000 hours will be covered by this funding for overtime?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and the department has implemented overtime reduction plans in the last fiscal year, which kept FY25 uniform overtime spending relatively flat compared to FY24.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how do you utilize

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how do you utilize the state and city federal funding to offset the city funding for overtime, and how are reduction targets changed by changes the state and federal funding have made?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So it actually depends.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: We can't, you know, if we're funded for the overtime, we can't supplant with federal or state funding.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: But generally, for example, with the subway overtime, it's a great example of the fact that we worked in concert with the state to determine this funding is going to be available to us.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: What are key things we can do with it?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So the funding was sort of in concert with the increased deployments.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks for an update on federal funding losses. Tisch states no areas have been affected yet but warns of an existential threat two years out, and Ryan details the 40% Homeland Security grant cut and $6.5M port security grant loss.
chapters.title: Lee questions Tisch and Ryan on federal funding cuts update

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And at the preliminary budget hearing, you mentioned the city may not receive some of the federal funding this fiscal year, and can you update us on some of those numbers and what areas have been affected by the loss of funding?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I'll just say today, no areas have been affected.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: As I testified in the preliminary budget, this will be a problem two years from now because we are still today spending grant dollars from previous fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I want to be clear with the council and with New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: that this funding cut doesn't create an immediate threat, certainly not for the World Cup or this summer, but it does pose sort of an existential threat two years from now, and it does need to be corrected.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But as to the rest of that question, Christine?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So the cut for the Homeland Security Grant funding is 40%, so we had expected we would get about 89, and we're getting only 53 of that.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: As the Commissioner mentioned, the Port Security Grant, which we've traditionally received, we did apply for that.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: We did not receive it.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: That's usually about $6.5 million.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: And then there were over a dozen smaller grants that we didn't apply for.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: We wouldn't have necessarily received funding for all of them, but they range from $200,000 to over $6 million.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Cumulatively, had we gotten the maximum for all of them, it would have been about $19 million.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: And because of the existing terms of conditions and guidance from our legal and the law department, a determination was made that we could not make those certifications, therefore we did not apply.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So as the Commissioner said, we're able to work within our existing resources now to make sure there's no impact on security and safety, but if this continues, and we hope that it doesn't, there will be an impact further down the line.

chapters.summary: Tisch emphasizes the Homeland Security Grant Program, slashed to $53M, funds intelligence analysts, counterterrorism deployments, heavy weapons teams, and the domain awareness system, calling the cuts a direct threat to NYC's counterterrorism capabilities.
chapters.title: Tisch warns of counterterrorism grant cuts impact

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I'll add our bread and butter, where we get like the biggest amount of dollars for

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: from federal Homeland Security grants is called the Homeland Security Grant Program.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That was slashed to what we

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: to be about $53 million.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That is a 40% cut on that bread and butter

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: grant for Homeland Security grant programs, things that fund our intelligence analysts, our counterterrorism deployments, our heavy weapons teams, our

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: technology, our domain awareness system.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That is the grant that we are most focused on.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Earlier in the year, when then Secretary Noem announced these cuts, we were hopeful, based on some of the things that the president had said, that that funding would be restored.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Unfortunately, now that it's time to get the funding, we have not seen that restoration.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And our hope is that either somehow that money will find its way to New York City.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about contingency plans if federal funding isn't restored. Tisch states either the federal government pays or the city will have to pick up the tab, calling cuts to intelligence and counterterrorism capabilities non-starters.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss contingency plans for federal funding loss

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what is plan B if it doesn't and what are contingency plans?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: My opinion, I mean, again, I said this is a problem not for this fiscal year, but for like one or two fiscal years out.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I don't see how New York City does without the capabilities that these grants fund.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So either we get this corrected with the federal government, which we should.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I think needs to happen, or the city is going to have to pick up the tab.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But from my perspective, cutting the intelligence analysts, cutting the domain awareness system, cutting the

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: responsados that do shows of force in front of, for example, houses of worship.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Cutting those things are non-starters, so either the federal government pays up or the city's going to have to pick up the tab.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how executive plan savings of $9.3M in FY26 work with overtime additions. Ryan explains the savings include $19M in uniform overtime savings plus PS accruals, vehicle capitalization, and civilian headcount reductions.
chapters.title: Lee and Ryan discuss executive plan savings breakdown

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The executive plan includes savings of $9.3 million in FY26, and how does the savings work with overtime additions that were included in the preliminary plan?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So part of that savings actually did include uniform overtime savings of $19 million on the city side.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: The overall budget did include some additional federal funding.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So we are still, as the commissioner indicated earlier, achieving savings year over year in overtime through our overtime management plan.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: The additional other savings were just PS accruals as well as capitalization of the vehicles, as the commissioner mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: And we had the civilian savings that were taken that resulted in headcount reductions as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about vehicle capitalization savings of $103.7M across FY27-FY30. Tisch expresses enthusiasm, explaining the change in state law allows police emergency response vehicles to be capitalized over five years, protecting fleet purchases from expense budget cuts.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss vehicle capitalization changes

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For vehicle capitalization, exec plan includes savings of $103.7 million across FY27 to FY30 from utilizing capital funds instead of expense funds for vehicle costs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And could you explain what changes that are

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: allowed these vehicles to be capital eligible?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes, we are ecstatic about this because the NYPD's fleet over the past few years, the state of it, has been disgraceful.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: With an unacceptable portion of the fleet being either out of service or beyond its useful life or close to beyond its useful life.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: These vehicles are crucial parts of our operation, and in my opinion, it is incumbent on the city and on this department to keep them refreshed.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: When previously they had not been capitally eligible, so we could only buy new vehicles with expense funding.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And over the past four years, I think with the exception of last year, the city continually cut expense funding as part of expense reductions for NYPD fleets.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So the NYPD bought like shockingly few.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Vehicles several years in a row, which really takes its toll.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We needed a change in state law to allow our fleet to be capitally eligible, both on the marked and unmarked side.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We saw it as a no-brainer.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We believed that our fleet

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: met all of the requirements for capital eligibility in terms of, for example, the five-year useful life.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And we were thrilled to see in the governor's budget the change to that state law that allows our fleet to be capitally eligible.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I just want to be clear, this is not just a good thing for this year, this is a good thing for future years, right?

chapters.summary: Lee asks how capitalization changes the procurement process. Ryan confirms the process remains unchanged, just requiring certificate to proceed with OMB, and they've already commenced the process for next year.
chapters.title: Lee and Ryan discuss procurement process for capitalized vehicles

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then how does that change the procurement process, if at all, with the $82.6 million now for the purchase of the vehicles?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: The procurement process will remain unchanged.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: We just obviously have to go through the certificate to proceed process with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: We've already commenced that process for next year so we can get the orders in as quickly as possible to continue to replenish the fleet.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I'll say when I speak to our officers about things that management can improve for them, the fleet.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Comes up almost every time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about IT license capitalization changes. Ryan explains certain licenses were re-evaluated and determined to be capital eligible, resulting in expense savings.
chapters.title: Lee and Ryan discuss IT license capitalization

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The plan included a similar change for IT license costs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you explain the change there?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Capital eligibility versus expense.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Yeah, so there are just certain licenses.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Licenses for IT tend to be an area where, depending on how long the license is going to last and actually what we're getting, there have been at certain times questions on whether or not certain licenses were capital eligible or had to be used operating funds.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So working with our IT.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Information Technology Bureau, we took a closer look.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: look at the licenses and determine that there were more that could actually be part of the capitalization process and that resulted in expense savings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks if other cost areas could be similarly capitalized. Ryan says they continually look for opportunities, noting inflation sometimes pushes items above minimum dollar thresholds.
chapters.title: Lee and Ryan explore additional capitalization opportunities

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And are there any other areas of cost that similar changes can be made to swap expense funding for capital or?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: We always look at it.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Sometimes, unfortunately, because of inflation, things

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: The silver lining is they become more expensive, so they may have met all the other criteria but not met the minimum dollar value.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So we're always looking to see if we can capitalize.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Obviously, we want to be efficient and responsible to capital funds as well, but you don't want, you know, there's a reason why there's capital funding to cover things that have a long, useful life to pay them over time.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So we do maximize capitalization as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about using technology, IT, and AI to improve timesheet systems and lower overtime. Tisch notes ongoing IT modernization including a new 311 system and digitized command logs, and commits to looking at administrative system updates.
chapters.title: Lee asks Tisch about technology to manage overtime

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And have you also looked at, I guess this is generally for overtime and other areas within NYPD, because I think I brought this up in the preliminary hearing, but looking at technology, IT, AI, those types of platforms to

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: figure out timesheet updates, for example, that could help lower overtime or sort of improving the systems within NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So, you know, I spent a lot of my career building IT systems at the department, and we are always interested in modernizing both our operational and administrative systems.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I will say that we have been doing a lot of operational work on IT.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In terms of IT development, we have a new 311 system for the department that's going to come out.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It's coming out now-ish.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We're digitizing the command log.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We will look seriously at where we can put IT resources to update administrative systems as well.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That's not a guarantee that it will be done, but I take the feedback and we will look for opportunities to do that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about uniform and civilian PS accruals savings of $110.8M in FY27 and whether hiring will be delayed. Tisch and Ryan explain the accruals reflect salary mix adjustments, not hiring delays, with plans to hire nearly 4,000 officers this year.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss PS accruals and hiring plans

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in terms of the uniform civilian PS accruals, executive plan includes savings of $110.8 million in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Our understanding is that these savings are usually taken from vacant salaries, and are you planning on not hiring additional?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know that you said the number of hires has increased, so wanted to know when you expect the next NYPD class and will be similar size as recent classes.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Oh, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: As I mentioned earlier, we are...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Trying to end the year at our new authorized headcount.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So we're going to be hiring approximately, I think just shy of 4,000 officers this year to get to that number based on our expected attrition.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: I can speak to you.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: And those accruals, those are not reflecting any delay in hiring.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Essentially,

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: As you can imagine, when you have a workforce of over 35,000 people, we make assumptions about looking at attrition, who's going to leave what salary.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: But over time, if more people with higher salaries leave for a certain period of time or whatever the mix is, in addition to

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: differentials and things like that, it can add up quickly in terms of just adjustments to this is what we assumed we were going to need to cover all the salaries for X number of people.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Those same number of people can remain employed in terms of the assumption of who's coming on, but you have people at maybe higher levels leaving.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: It's a mix.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: And you're off a little bit, and it allows for adjustment.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Sometimes it unfortunately goes the other way, right?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So this is just reflecting where we are right now.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yeah, so I'll add that if our assumptions are correct,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: about how many resignations we'll see and how many retirements we will see this year than we should end the year at the authorized headcount based on the hiring plan.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And of course, with the council's help, raising the maximum age for which someone can take on the noble job of becoming a New York City police officer.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about coordination with the Office to End Gender-Based Violence now under OCS. Tisch confirms she recently met with the NGBV commissioner and all work continues seamlessly.
chapters.title: Lee questions Tisch on NGBV coordination under OCS

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The speaker had alluded to this question earlier, but I just kind of wanted to go a little bit further and to say, because as we know, the NGBV and a lot of the domestic violence work is now under OCS.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I know that you had said that previously, you know, the work that you have done with those departments will continue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just like to know.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yeah, like I just had a meeting with the commissioner of NGBV.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: gender-based violence last week or the week before.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about language access for domestic violence reporting in immigrant communities. NYPD staff explain they have language line services, crime victim advocates in precincts, and coordinate with Family Justice Centers for culturally specific organizations.
chapters.title: Lee and NYPD staff discuss language access for DV reporting

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I want to be clear that all of that work with all of those officers, with all of those offices, including on the executive level, commissioner to commissioner, that continues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also, because I know that in order to report that, they have to go through, a lot of it is underreported because especially in communities of color, immigrant communities where there's different languages, cultural barriers, those types of things,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: are there folks within the precincts that would be able to appropriately help a lot of those folks that are more language specific?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Aside language line, because I thought about that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Alex, do you want to answer that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So part of creating the...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Domestic violence investigators is really to have investigators whose sole job is investigating domestic violence.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that wasn't the case before.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: When you've got a detective investigating a domestic violence crime, they could be investigating lots and lots of different crimes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there was no specialization there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We do our best that we can to put people in communities who are able to speak the languages.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And if we need to, of course, we can borrow from one precinct to another.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If there is a real need there, it does come up from time to time.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the inspector who is doing a brilliant job running our new domestic violence unit can add to that answer.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So in addition to just the officers there, we have the language line, but we also have crime victim advocates that work inside of the individual precincts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we work closely with Family Justice Center, who has, you know, gender-based, not gender-based, but culturally specific organizations that we can always link our survivors to.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just want to make sure that you guys are all in communication.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know some of the precincts I overlap with are very great about working with the community groups.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: One of the offices that we have the closest coordination to is the Office to End Gender-Based Violence.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They are involved in the development of our training in the selection of and oversight of some of our closest partners, even people who work within our precincts.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so the coordination there has been and continues to be seamless.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about Be Heard program coordination and 988 mental health hotline numbers. Tisch reports 204 911 calls qualified for 988 referral in the first 16 days, and provides Be Heard data showing 17,961 calls requested but only 1,411 responded to in the first four months of 2026.
chapters.title: Lee questions Tisch on Be Heard and 988 call data

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And my last question, I'll pause.
