utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I have more, but I want to pass it off to the chair and other members.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But in terms of the Be Heard, and it's more of a comment, but do you have lots of thoughts on Be Heard, as I know Commissioner, Councilmember Caban has.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But in terms of the coordination, I think that's always been the frustration, as well as the fact that it is hard to hire up, staff up for that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And it's more of FDNY EMS, which we'll go into later this afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But just wanted to know, in general, for folks that are experiencing mental health crisis, I know that we have 988 now, which hopefully more people are utilizing.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes, people are using it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and just wanted to know what the numbers look like there in terms of if a call comes in to you all in 911, and then how does that get transferred, referred, how successful are those transfers?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So between, you're talking specifically for 988 or you're talking about for Be Heard?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For both, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay, so let's start with 988 because I'm there on that page.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Between program inception on April 14th of this year through April 30th.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: All of my data here ends April 30th.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I guess for the first 16 days, 204 911 calls qualified for referral to 988 mental health resources.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And then on Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Let's see.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: All right, so at 911, we get about 4.3 million calls.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: for service each year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Of those, the emotionally disturbed persons, nonviolent, no weapons, 85,793.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So of the overall calls for service, approximately 2% are divertible to

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Be heard.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay, now I'll give you the numbers for this year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: There were 17, so between January and April, first four months of the year,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: There were 17,961 calls where Be Heard was requested.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Is that right, was requested?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Where Be Heard was requested.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They responded to 1,411 of them

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So that is 7.9% of the calls that they were requested to respond to.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that a lack of the teams that they have?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You have to ask FDNY.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I'll definitely ask them, but I...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Point is, the 911, NYPD's role in this is the 911 call taker, who is an NYPD employee,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: determines that the call is eligible to be transferred, to be heard, and then it goes outside of the NYPD's

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: universe.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether Be Heard and 988 have led to more successful diversions. Tisch states NYPD doesn't see outcomes of those calls and notes diversion numbers are tiny compared to overall volume, with significant room for improvement.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss Be Heard outcomes and improvement opportunities

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then just let me ask it a different way.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have you seen that with the addition of Be Heard as well as 988 that there are more successful incidences that are being deferred out that have been successfully?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We don't see the outcomes of those calls.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Right now, the diversion numbers are tiny compared to the overall volume and the overall need.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So there are lots of opportunities to improve, even with

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: without changing the criteria.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Meaning if the criteria remains emotionally disturbed person, nonviolent, no weapons, there is still a huge amount of room for Be Heard to respond to more calls.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I will pause there and pass it over to Chair Felice.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, sorry, before I do that, I just want to say we've been joined by Council Members Areola, Hanks, Brooks-Powers, Deputy Speaker Williams, Council Members Dinowitz, Felder, Aviles, Majority Leader Abreu, Council Members Brewer, Ressler, Joseph J.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sanchez, and Council Member Mealy.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks about the 12-hour officer shifts planned for July 1-7 during the World Cup. Tisch explains officers will work five days with four hours added to beginning or end of tours, with training canceled and no planned RDO cancellations.
chapters.title: Feliz questions Tisch on World Cup 12-hour shift plan

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Wow, we have a full house today.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We have almost enough members to hold a stated meeting and have quorum.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I'll start my questions.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You attract a crowd.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I'll start my questions on the topic of large-scale events.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: As we know, we're one month away from the World Cup in July.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: With increased tourism coming to New York City for the FIFA World Cup, you issued a memo stating that officer shifts will be 12 hours from July 1st to July 7th.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: How exactly will this work?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Are they expected to work three days per week for a total of 36 hours and then a half of a shift for 40 or 42 hours?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They will either work pre-tour or post-tour overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So importantly for our officers, during that week, we are not throwing out their chart.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They will continue to work their standard five days a week, just with four hours added either to the beginning or to the end of their tour.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the chief of department is going to soon issue guidance to each bureau about how their 12-hour tours are going to work, meaning are they doing pre-tour or post-tour.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Chief, do you have anything to add to that?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: All right, so five days a week, 12-hour tours.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Five days a week, 12-hour tours, but in that time, we are doing things.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: like canceling training.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We are canceling all things that could otherwise be a drain on NYPD resources.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We do not expect to be canceling their RDOs, their regular days off, as a practice.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It may have to happen from time to time.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks what percentage of officers the 12-hour shifts will apply to. Tisch says it applies to the majority from police officer to chief, and the chief of department will issue bureau-specific guidance.
chapters.title: Feliz and Tisch discuss scope of 12-hour shifts

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Is this going to apply to every police officer?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We're not going to cancel, for example, like if they have holiday picks, they're not going to have to work, you know, when they were planning to be away.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But it is going to apply to the vast majority of...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'd say the majority of police officers in the New York City Police Department for that week and perhaps for a few days closer to

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: the finals will be asked to work 12-hour tours, and that is from the rank of police officer on up to chief.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So it really affects everyone.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It is not a decision that we took.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Lightly or that I took lightly, but it is an absolute necessity for that week given the number of things going on in the city.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks about projected overtime for the World Cup week. Tisch and Ryan project $35-42M for July 1-7, approximately $73M total for all overlapping events, with $12.4M for equipment and $6.5M for drones, totaling $92M against $71M currently budgeted.
chapters.title: Feliz questions Tisch and Ryan on World Cup overtime costs

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Approximately how many police officers would this apply to?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: You said a vast majority.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Would you say more than 80%?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I don't want to give an actual...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: percentage, what I can say is that the chief of department is going to issue guidance.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: to each bureau and once we have that guidance, we will let your office know what percentage it applied to.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: How much overtime is projected for that week of the World Cup?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Can you give the numbers?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Well, for the World Cup or the July 1st to July 7th?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Because like, remember, we've got

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: July 4th, Sail 250, which is like the president's military parade in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: NBA Finals, World Cup, all as potentially Taylor Swift's wedding.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'm kidding.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But all as...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Overlapping events in New York City between June 11th and July 19th.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Any one of those on their own would be a big deal in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: All of them taken together are unprecedented and are going to place

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: unusual and I would say historic demands on the New York City Police Department.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So Christine, can you go through the July 1st to July 7th, what that dollar value projection is, and then also for the World Cup specifically?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So these projections are preliminary, but for the July 1st through July 7th, our projection order of magnitude is that will cost between $35 to $42 million for that period.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: overall for FIFA and the and sale 250 so this entire time of all of these overlapping events

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: we believe over time is projected about 73 million on top of that obviously there's equipment that the department needs to make sure that we're able to keep everything safe secure and also manage the crowds that's 12.4 million

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: and then there's 6.5 million for drone equipment so in total the estimate right now is 92 million dollars the good news is that the budget that you've all looked at already really accounts for on the city side

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: about $50 million, and then there's another $21 million in federal funding.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So there's $71 million that we're accounting for.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: All the federal funding's not quite reflected yet, but that does leave a shortfall of about $20 to $21 million based on projections.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I will just add that the federal government slash FIFA did not give us nearly enough money to cover the overtime bill on the New York City Police Department.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Christine, how much did people give us?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: $14 million?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So they gave us $14.6 for the overtime, toward overtime, and $6.5 for counter-drone equipment.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: We are having conversations about potentially getting more, but that's not determined yet.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks about fan safety plans for transit hubs and watch parties. Tisch describes extensive planning for watch parties, transit security, and the need to view all FIFA events through a counterterrorism lens.
chapters.title: Feliz questions Tisch on fan safety at transit hubs and watch parties

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It's going to be a rough start to the fiscal year for our overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Just remember that for next year's overtime hearings.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We'll keep that in mind.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Talk to us about the plan for fan safety at transit hubs and also watch parties.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The plans, okay.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So with all of these major events that are going on in New York City, there will be other joyful events going on around them in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: All of those events will require a police presence.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For example, NBA finals watch parties, FIFA watch parties, people taking the subway, using mass transit, going to and from those watch parties.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: People going to the events themselves, meaning getting people from New York City to the World Cup as a whole to do in and of itself that requires security.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They're going mostly by bus or by rail, and there's a big screening component and security component there.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So the...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It would be an understatement to say that we have been planning.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We have been like aggressively planning for so many different things that are occurring this summer, not just the main events themselves, but all of the festivities surrounding those events.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And remember, FIFA is an international name and therefore a target.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so we do not look at FIFA as just a normal event in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Both the FIFA tournament and the events surrounding FIFA in New York City, the fan zones, the watch parties, those we all have to look at seriously through the counterterrorism lens.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks how NYPD will coordinate with federal and state authorities for event safety. Chief LaPetrie details bus screening operations in New Jersey, Penn Station rail screening for 15,000 people, and coordination with Amtrak and MTA.
chapters.title: Feliz and LaPetrie discuss federal and state coordination for World Cup

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Speaking about event, how will the NYPD work with federal and state authorities to ensure participants are safe getting to and from the events?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Hand in hand.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The chief can, do you want to talk through the buses and the trains and how you're coordinating with partners on that?

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: On the eight game days at MetLife, we have identified four separate locations that over 200 buses will be starting in New Jersey,

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: swept in New Jersey, with an NYPD present to make sure that they are swept properly.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Those buses then come into New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Obviously, we're not able to park 200 buses.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: In and around the four identified areas.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: So we will start with 45 buses at a time.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: That footprint at those locations start 12 hours prior to those buses being parked there.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Obviously those locations have to be swept by our counterterrorism team.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: They then have to be, the footprint has to stay there until the buses actually arrive.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Those buses, each one of those buses.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: I've assigned two uniformed officers to, again, to continue to observe those buses, to make sure that, you know, they're safe to go back into New Jersey.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Again, very dynamic.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: The 42nd Street will be the street that we will be using that will be closed to just bus and emergency vehicles going through the tunnel into New Jersey.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: As far as the rails, we have identified approximately, we think, upwards of 15,000 people.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: To go through Penn Station and then ultimately have to get screened by the NYPD to go again into New Jersey.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: So it's a huge undertaking.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: On top of doing all that on the eight game days, there will be numerous watch parties throughout New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: And again, we've gone from 32 teams to 48 teams.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: We've gone from approximately 150 games to 200 games.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Every game that is being played will be front and center in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: As far as the rails working with our federal partners and Amtrak, so actually next week I will be chairing a meeting with the Northeast Corridor from Washington, D.C.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Up to and through New York City with Amtrak, MTA.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: And all our rail partners to make sure that, again, it's seamless, it's safe.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: And the federal government, the state government, and obviously the NYPD are working very, very close.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Thank you, Chief.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks whether public watch parties will be scaled back due to NYPD concerns. Tisch says the mayor will announce robust watch parties across all five boroughs, but notes the department must view FIFA events through a counterterrorism lens while managing standard summer events simultaneously.
chapters.title: Feliz and Tisch discuss World Cup watch party plans

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Speaking about the World Cup and also watch parties, there's been numerous articles which stated that public events, including World Cup watch parties on open streets and plazas and parks, will be scaled back due to NYPD concerns.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Is it true?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And if yes, can you talk to us about some of the concerns?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And also, is the plan or the hope to have police presence at every single watch party?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: My understanding is that the mayor is going to announce a very robust set of watch parties for FIFA around all...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Five boroughs, which will run the length of the tournament.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We do have, that we know of, already announced the five FIFA fan zones that will be

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: five FIFA fan zones, one in each borough.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So my hope and my expectation is that we are going to have a lot of festivities in New York City around the World Cup.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Obviously, we want to celebrate.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We need to celebrate safely.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I already told you about the need to look at all things FIFA through a counterterrorism lens.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That's what this department is doing.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: don't have unlimited resources, and we also have a lot of other things that are occurring in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You know, when a country hosts the World Cup or the Olympics, usually they cancel everything else going on in that city at that time.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We didn't in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We're still having the Puerto Rican Day Parade.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We're having lots of the Pride Parade.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We are having many or nearly all of the standard large-scale events in New York City this summer that we would have in a summer with, like, none of these.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Events going on and so I would appreciate it if you could think about the demands on this department in in that context.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We would like to facilitate a lot.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Perhaps we won't be able to facilitate everything but there will be a lot of celebrating in New York City.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks what factors NYPD considers when reviewing watch party permits. Tisch explains they assess whether events can be secured with blocker cars, available resources, and concurrent events, reviewing each application based on specific details.
chapters.title: Feliz and Tisch discuss watch party permit review factors

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So, absolutely, yes.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And, of course, we're expecting there to be a lot of requests for permits for watch parties in communities and et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And on that issue.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For each one, for each city-sponsored one, the chief and Commissioner Weiner have to put together a full counterterrorism package because, again, that name.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: FIFA comes with elevated threat.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Right, yes.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And of course, there's a lot of agencies that play a role when it comes to issuing permits.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: NYPD plays a big role in reviewing and issuing them.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: What are factors that the NYPD will consider?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: When determining whether to issue a, let's say a watch party on Fordham Road.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So whether we can do it safely, which means does the footprint make sense?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Can we secure it appropriately with blocker cars?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Like we don't want cars driving into these watch parties either on purpose or by accident.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We don't want to be, we've seen vehicle ramming attacks across the city.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Do we have the resources available to it?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Like, for example, are there several other watch parties going on in the same precinct at the same time?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So, I mean, the main thing is.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Are we able to ensure a responsible amount of security at the event?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: What are areas that are more likely to receive an approval for a watch party?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I'm sure doing a watch party at a park would be much easier to secure.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: You know, it's more difficult to...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Usually it is, that is true.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Each permit application we review based on the specific details.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So how big is it?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Where is it?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Is it in a heavily trafficked area?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Is it right near a subway station?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Does it run right alongside a busy corridor where a vehicle can just come right up?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: into it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Like there are so many different factors that we look at.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: What else is going on in the city at the same time?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I mean, everyone needs to remember, we have a day in New York City where we both have a, hopefully or potentially, an NBA finals game in New York City and a World Cup game in

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: the New York, New Jersey area.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So we also have to look at what else is going on in the city that day.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: All right, thank you for the information.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks for overtime totals by fiscal year since 2020. Ryan provides: FY20 $1B, FY22 $918M, FY23 $1.047B, FY24 $1.155B, FY25 $1.12B, with FY26 projected at $1.013B. Tisch notes FY26 is 6% below the 10-year historical average when adjusted for contract dollars and excluding transit overtime.
chapters.title: Feliz and Ryan review overtime spending by fiscal year since 2020

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So I'll ask some questions about the overtime issue, and then I know my colleagues have a lot of questions, so then I'll pause there.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So can you repeat the numbers related to overtime, let's say for the last seven years, the last seven fiscal years?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And for these questions, let's just focus on the fiscal years, because of course, calendar years are very different from fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: This, the fiscal, okay, fiscal year 26, the first three quarters, we are projected to be down 10% in overtime spend.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: What about total, not just the first three quarters?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Total fiscal year 26.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Well, we're not, we're not.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We're in the last quarter now.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Right, but projected for the last quarter included.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Oh, the projection is $1.013 billion, which is $91 million less than fiscal year 25, the same first three quarters.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For the first three quarters, sorry, which is 91.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Million dollars less than fiscal year 25.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So the full year projection is 1.013 billion, 91 million dollars less than fiscal year 25.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For the first three quarters that have been completed in fiscal year 26, we are down 10% in overtime compared to the first three quarters of fiscal year 25.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And if you take out the transit overtime,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: which the governor pays for, and you put overtime spend in today's dollars, meaning in today's contract dollars, how much the cops are paid,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: the FY26 overtime spend is projected to be 6% lower than that historical average going back to 2016.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And what has been the total overtime budget since, let's say, 2020, all of the fiscal years since 2020?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I have it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It's this chart here, Christine, right?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Yep, those are the adjusted values.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So going back to 2020, so if you look at, again, excluding the transit, it was a billion.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: In fiscal year 2020.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Well, including the entire thing, transit the entire over time.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Well, with transit, there wasn't really much transit that year.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So that was a billion for like billion.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: It was basically a billion in fiscal year 20.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Fiscal year 22 was 576, but that was basically because of the pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So that's really an outlier.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Fiscal year 22 was $918 million.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Fiscal year 23 was $1,047,000,000.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Fiscal year 24 was $1,155,000,000.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: And fiscal year 25 was $1,120,000,000.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Fiscal year 25 is when transit...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Started.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so if you take transit out, which the governor pays for, then fiscal year 25 is 954.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the projection for FY26 without transit is 862.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I know it's a big spend for overtime, but I just want to make sure that the

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: the members of this council understand that if you look at historical spending totals, the numbers here seem more reasonable than they otherwise would.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks why overtime remains near $1B despite 4,000 new hires. Tisch explains the hires offset attrition from 20 years ago, the department ended up only several hundred officers above prior years, and overtime is driven by special events, protests, and targeted crime responses.
chapters.title: Feliz presses Tisch on why $1B overtime persists

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Yeah, and that is my next question.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Why are we still spending a billion dollars in overtime?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I mean, you know, I know all of you continue to mention that the previous administration, there was an anything-goes system.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: There were significant changes.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Yeah, there was.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: If I could just finish.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And there were also about 4,000 new police officers added last year, which exceeded attrition rates.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So I'm just curious, why are we still in the ballpark of $1 billion?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: This is something that we don't see with any other agency.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yeah, so the officers that were added last year, the reason we hired 4,000 of them is because 20 years ago,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: we had hired a huge number of officers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So we hired over 4,000 officers last year to make sure that we kept up with attrition.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In fact, we beat attrition by a few hundred officers, which was a minor miracle.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But I don't want to give the impression that we ended the year

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: up 4,000 officers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We ended the year up several hundred officers, which is the first time we'd ended the year up for many years.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: At the same time, it is the case that before,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I got here.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The department did not manage overtime in any meaningful way.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And it, to me, looked like when I showed up an anything goes system.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the executive team here under the first step and the deputy commissioner of management and budgets leadership have put in, I think, a very responsible

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: overtime plan that has delivered meaningful results, a 10% decline in overtime in fiscal year 26 and a 12.4%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Decline in overtime in calendar year 25 and we will continue to do that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: As to the question about why overtime is required, I think we need not look farther than what's going on this summer and the previous questions you just asked.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Are we going to be able to have all of these watch parties?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Are New Yorkers going to be able to celebrate?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the answer is like, yeah, we're going to do a lot of celebrating, but that celebrating needs to be safe.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And in order for it to be safe, we need to make sure that we have officers there and that we've thought through all of the security.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: requirements.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So for things, for example, like special events, that requires overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For protests, things that are like unexpected, that requires overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Sometimes, for example, like if we're seeing a gang war,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: in the floor.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And we say, oh, the officers on Saturday night, they usually end at, I'm making this up, at 1 in the morning or midnight, and we want them to stay on post until 2 in the morning because we want to make sure that we curb the gun violence.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Those are examples of things that we would use overtime for.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Right, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And yes, this is, as we know, this is, is and will always be a very busy city.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: New York City will always have large-scale events, also sports events, parades, and et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: But I think the big, and of course, we need and appreciate the police presence at these events to make sure that people are safe.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks whether increasing headcount could decrease overtime. Tisch says it would help for special events like this summer but wouldn't make a dent in other overtime categories, and notes the mayor added 580 additional officers in his first month.
chapters.title: Feliz and Tisch discuss headcount increase vs. overtime reduction

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: But the big question or the big point here is whether there's any measures that we can take so that we could have that police presence, let's say, for example, by increasing headcount and not have to continuously rely on a billion dollars of overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I do not believe having more officers this summer would definitely

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: decrease the overtime, the need for overtime this summer for all of these special events, and in my opinion, could have an overall general effect on overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But those are budget decisions that the council needs to make in coordination with the mayor and his team.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I am doing the best I can with the officers that we have, and I am very pleased that so far this year in his first month,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: the mayor has given us over 500 additional officers budgeted for the New York City Police Department, 580.

chapters.summary: Lee asks for the breakdown of patrol officers versus community affairs and other units. Tisch reports patrol staffing at 15,749 as of April 30, up 888 officers from last year, with the Bronx up 250 officers.
chapters.title: Lee questions Tisch on patrol staffing breakdown

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Brief question on headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the headcount is approximately 35,000.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What percent of this 35,000 is patrol officers versus community affairs and any other units?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Oh, I have that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Hold on one second.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Oh, here it is.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Our patrol staffing, I'm very pleased to tell you, is 888 officers, as of April 30th this year, 888

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: officers over what it was the same time in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So we have nearly 1,000 additional officers on patrol now if you compare April 30th of this year to April 30th of...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: last year and that our number right now I believe is 15,749.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I want to be clear, those numbers include our evidence collection teams and our domestic violence units.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I'll just explain why.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Historically, so last year and the year before and the year before that, forever, those two teams were part of our patrol services.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Bureau so they were included as part of the patrol headcount They have been moved now to be under the detective Bureau But they perform the same functions that they performed when they were under

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: patrol services So I'm giving you an apples-to-apples comparison

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the Bronx is up 250 officers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I know you care about that since last year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much for that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We'll take more if there's...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You're about to get more.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That's the great thing about all the hiring we did last year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Now we do 18 months of training for these officers on the street.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And as they hit that 18 months, they go into precincts where they're responding to 911 jobs.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So every few months now, precincts across the city are going to see an influx of new police officers on patrol.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And it is something now that we can enjoy for the next year and so long as our great hiring continues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much for that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're absolutely seeing the results.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I've been around a few incidents and the police response has been at the speed of light.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So that's something that we appreciate and we welcome.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the top three things leading to overtime. Ryan identifies events and investigations as the two largest categories, with arrests, operational needs, crime reduction, and transit safety also contributing.
chapters.title: Lee and Ryan discuss top overtime drivers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: A few more questions about the issue of overtime.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What are the top three things that lead to overtime?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sure large-scale events is going to be something big this year for protests.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Do you have that, Christine?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Yeah, so it's events, it's investigations, and then basically we have the other categories are roughly even with arrests, operational,

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: crime reduction, and then of course we have our transit safety as well.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: But the two largest areas are investigations and the events.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about officer deployment numbers for large protests versus smaller events. Tisch and LaPetrie explain it varies greatly, citing 3,000+ officers assigned to a recent parade, with deployment based on security risks and expected participation.
chapters.title: Lee questions Tisch and LaPetrie on officer deployment for events

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, and just two more questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know we have a lot of...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: colleagues who have signed up for questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: A few questions about officer deployment.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Approximately how many officers do we have for a large protest versus a smaller protest, also large-scale events, and also for parades, a smaller parade versus a larger parade?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What's the ballpark of a lot of officers?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Really runs the gamut.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It just, it varies so much that it is hard to answer that question.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Do you want to?

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Sure, I can answer it.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: Let's just look at yesterday's parade.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: So yesterday's parade, and this is total uniform members, so I'm not separating our CT teams, I'm not separating our emergency service.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: We had over 3,000 offices that were assigned.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: To that detail.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: And again, we look at security risks, we look at past incidents, not just in New York City, but across the world, and we also look at how many participants we expect at those events.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: So a lot of times it could be hundreds of officers,

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: but many times it could be thousands of officers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the same is true for protests.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It just really depends on how many people are expected to go to the protests, where is the protest, what time of day, what else is going on, all of those factors.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If I may also just, the tiered system that we have in the protest element speaks to this as well, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it's everything that the chief of department was just saying, the police commissioner was saying.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then one thing that has been a product of the protest settlement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: is thinking through, okay, how many officers do we actually need to do this responsibly, appropriately, consistent with the settlement?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think that's actually been a real benefit of the protest settlement.

chapters.summary: Lee asks what percentage of overtime is worked during shifts versus regular days off. Tisch says they don't have that data available but will provide it.
chapters.title: Lee asks about overtime during shifts vs. days off

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, thank you for the information.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Final question before I pass it on to colleagues and on the issue of overtime.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sure there's a lot of things that, a lot of ways that we could get to overtime.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Let's say a police officer has to work a few additional hours after their shift.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They can also do overtime during their, as they say, RDLs, during the regular day off.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They're off and they're called to work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What percentage of overtime hours are worked by officers already working versus those that are during their RDO's?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We're going to have to get that to you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We don't have that data right here, but I think that we can.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Figure that get that number for you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm gonna pause here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We could get that I know you have your team here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We could get that through my next round.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That would be I don't think we're gonna be able to get that in the next round the deputy commissioner management and budget will get it over to your office, but she's got to do a little work to get that number out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we have Deputy Speaker followed by Councilmember Cabon.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Williams asks about discrepancies in hate crime data between Tisch's testimony and other reports showing an 11.8% jump. Tisch explains the reports referenced Q1 data while her numbers include April, and small numbers can shift percentages significantly.
chapters.title: Williams questions Tisch on hate crime data discrepancy

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Hello, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just a quick question about something you said in your testimony around hate crimes.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: You had mentioned that it was down 5.4%, but...

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We saw some reports that show a jump to 11.8% and that rapes are also up.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So just wanted to know if you can explain like the differences between like your testimony and some reports that we saw that says.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes, the reports that you saw referenced the first quarter of the numbers at the end of the first quarter of the year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So January, February, March.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The numbers that I gave account for April as well.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so it changed.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You know, these are, hate crime is a terrible thing, but these are small-ish numbers compared to overall crime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So a few crimes can make a big difference in terms of up or down.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about NYPD's racial equity plan goal to enhance hiring by removing barriers, and what funding supports this work. Staff explain a hiring panel of experts is convening over 100 days, with no specific funding allocated for the study.
chapters.title: Williams questions NYPD on racial equity plan hiring goals

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just turning questions to the racial equity plans that you put forward.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I've been asking all city agencies about their racial equity plans, especially during budget hearings, because we hope that budget allocations are being appropriated to fulfill some of the goals.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So one of the goals of the NYPD is to enhance the hiring process by removing unnecessary barriers, bringing greater transparency to decisions, and ensuring qualified candidates are not excluded by the system.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: This is listed as a short-term goal to be completed by 2026.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Has the department done a review in the past of the hiring process?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: What was the outcome of this review?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And is any funding in this budget going towards recruitment and hiring?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there have been many reviews of the hiring process, but not one that particularly looks at equity.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the hiring panel is convening over a period of about 100 days.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It met earlier last month in the initial meeting.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's a collection of experts, academic experts, community members, really across the board to give us their best thoughts on how to improve that process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we had our initial kickoff.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We should have our next meeting, I think, in just a couple of weeks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then we'll have a final meeting at the end, and those are what the recommendations will come out of.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's no funding allocated just for the study.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's just being done with in-house staff.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And just to answer the last part of the question, a lot of the success that we have had in hiring over the past year and a half has been

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: driven by our fraternal organizations.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So those fraternal organizations going out into their communities, educating people about the work of police officers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We, a year ago at this time,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We couldn't find enough candidates to take the job.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You know, we had an actual, our actual headcount was well below the budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I give the work of those fraternal organizations a lot of credit for the work that they have done getting people interested in the job.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Williams asks which units have the lowest diversity levels for the goal of increasing representation in leadership by 2028. Tisch says they will get back with diversity by unit data.
chapters.title: Williams asks about diversity in NYPD specialized units

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Another goal is to increase representation in leadership positions and specialized units by 2028.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Which units currently have the lowest levels of diversity?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I have to get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We can get back to you with the diversity by unit.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just turning over to some district-specific questions.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So I appreciate the Q teams.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I visited each Q team in my district.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We love them.

chapters.summary: Williams raises concerns about truck parking in residential neighborhoods and asks about the Transportation Bureau budget. Tisch proposes assigning tow trucks directly to precincts with heavy towing needs, and Ryan reports the Transportation Bureau budget at $212M.
chapters.title: Williams and Tisch discuss truck parking enforcement and towing

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: But some of the things that I see Chief Obie here, specifically around truck parking

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: and making sure that these precincts have the resources to remove trucks and address a lot of the overnight commercial vehicle parking in residential neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So just wanted to know.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Specifically about her division and the budget there and how you...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Which division?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: The Chief Obey.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Oh, Chief Obey.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So what I want to say on that is I think the paradigm that this department has used forever on towing makes no sense.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the precincts that have heavy towing needs should probably have tow trucks assigned to them and people trained in how to

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: use them assigned there as well.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That is what makes sense for me.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You know, council members always want to give us funding for strategic things.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I think that if we can reorganize appropriately, that might be a great new way to address some of the acute towing problems that we are seeing in

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: specific precincts, including in your district.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yes, I love that idea.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And I know like all of us in Southeast Queens would definitely allocate, I hear, sound effects to our precincts because we do try to support them.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Let's have conversations about how we can make that a reality.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I think that that will be more meaningful than talking about how much money is assigned to the Transportation Bureau.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But Christine has that for you.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: $212 million.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just on a final note, just to add in your district, since we've met with you, the work still continues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: On 10 dates, we wrote 259 summonses, 51 toes so far, and 51 boots also.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the work will continue.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Williams advocates for increased Community Affairs Division funding, noting officers raise their own money for community events. Tisch reports she reassigned community affairs officers back to their precincts in January, restoring the function under patrol command.
chapters.title: Williams advocates for more Community Affairs Division funding

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just one more question about my favorite division in the police department, which is the Community Affairs Division.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I would love to see every year I ask for an increase because there are amazing community affairs officers.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: One precinct, they're doing a whole dance recital, you know, and they do the best that they can.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: They raise money inside of the police department.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: They externally ask for resources.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: But I feel like when I think of like community policing and what we all want to see from the police department, I feel like this is like the unit that does that work.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And I feel like they just don't, I would love to see them have more money.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: You know, the game truck breaks down, the rock wall, there's only like one rock wall.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We'd love to see a rock wall in each bureau.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I mean, this is just my yearly, please give more money to the community affairs division.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I hear you on that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I will say that one thing that we have done, and this was at the request and advice on the advice of.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Virtually every precinct CO that I've spoken to.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For four years, the community affairs officers were not assigned to their precincts.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They were reassigned to a centralized community affairs bureau, which basically meant that the precinct CO

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: lost his or her basically right-hand person, but particularly definitely the right-hand person for dealing directly with the community because that person no longer reported to him and her.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: As of January this year, I was very pleased to reassign community affairs officers back to their specific

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: precincts and I think that that has and will continue to go a long way.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: There are still many community affairs officers that are centrally assigned under the Community Affairs Bureau, but I think restoring that function under

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: patrol has led to some very positive outcomes and I am getting positive feedback on that both from many of your colleagues but just as importantly many precinct CEOs that I speak to.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember Banks, and we'll go with Councilmember Caban, followed by Wong.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Caban asks for FY26-to-date spending on the Quality of Life Division, broken down by Q teams and Q stat, plus FY27 projections. Ryan says she'll need to get back with the breakdown.
chapters.title: Caban questions NYPD on Quality of Life Division spending

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: As always, I have lots of questions, so I'm going to ask that you all be as succinct as possible.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to start with just some money numbers.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can you tell me, FY26 to date, the money spent on the quality of life division, and if you could break that down to money spent on the Q teams, Q stat, and then just the projected budget for FY27 for those?

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: Christine has that for you.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So I'm actually have to get back to you because I don't have a broken back by each bureau by spend to date.

utterances.speaker: Christine Ryan
utterances.text: So I will get back to you on that.

chapters.summary: Caban asks whether NYPD tracks complaints and civil suits based on Quality of Life Division enforcement. Staff say they can pull the data but don't track it day-to-day by division. Caban requests the data be provided to the Council.
chapters.title: Caban asks about tracking complaints from Quality of Life enforcement

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, that would be really helpful.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Are you tracking complaints in civil suits based on that particular division's enforcement?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Like CCRB complaints?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And civil suits.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And what?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And civil suits, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Civil suits.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the question is, do we sort of have like a separate tracking system by division?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, we certainly can pull that data.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's data that we can get, but I don't think that we're tracking it sort of day to day, sort of by division.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, so I want to move on to different questions.

chapters.summary: Caban asks what evidence NYPD has that increased arrests for low-level offenses improve public safety. Tisch states the Quality of Life Division was not created to increase low-level arrests but to address the explosion of 311 complaints, and rejects broken windows policing.
chapters.title: Caban and Tisch debate evidence for low-level arrest effectiveness

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think the request is, can you pull the data on that and provide it to the council in terms of the complaints and civil suits coming out of quality of life enforcement?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For sure.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Out of the quality of life division.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Quality of life division, yes.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Um,

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What, Commissioner, what evidence does the NYPD have that increased arrests for low-level offenses improve public safety outcomes?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: To be clear, we are not looking to make increased arrests for low-level offenses.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We give our officers a huge amount of discretion.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Discretion starts with engagement.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The quality of life division was certainly not created to gin the number of low-level arrests.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Well, Commissioner, that's not my question.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: No, but that's not my question, so I just want to redirect for a second.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What evidence is the NYP?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Because we have in prior hearings where you have testified and others.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I don't have evidence of it because that is not a stated goal, so I wouldn't look at that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The stated goal of the quality of life division.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: is to address the explosion of quality of life complaints that we have seen across every precinct and every district in this city.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And as a partner in public safety, what evidence do you have that addressing broken windows offenses, low-level offenses, improves public safety outcomes?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Told you I am not interested in going back to broken windows policing where you look to make a low, you look for a low level thing to find a higher level thing.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, so I will say that the theory...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: What I'm looking to do is address, can I just finish?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You can have more time.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: What I'm looking to do is...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Can I, Chair?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Is that cool?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think the commission is offering more time.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Maybe like another minute and then we'll go around.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We'll definitely put you for round two.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: What I am looking to do is address the explosion of 311 complaints that we have seen across every community in the city.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And those complaints differ community to community.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So what I want to say about this here is that either our goal has to be improved public safety outcomes or be the one to answer certain 311 complaints simply because you get them.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And what I think is important for us to analyze here is that there has been a 20-year meta-study analysis that debunks the relationship between disorder crime and using resources for broken windows policing.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Generally, it was disproved.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And even though you're saying it's not a stated goal, we have seen...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: the Data Collaborative for Justice over a four-year span from 21 to 24, so a sharp upsurge in arrests for level-level charges.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: A Gothamist article talked about a 3,000% increase in arrests for lying down or taking up more than one seat on the subway, and that at this point in the year, court cases for that offense in particular are already exceeding the pace

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: of last year.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I just, those things don't make sense to me in terms of either you're improving public safety, you're doing the things, if you're data driven, to improve public safety, or just

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: going to answer 311 calls simply to answer them and not improve public safety.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But I want to ask this, and then I'll go to a second round with my questions.

chapters.summary: Caban asks for the total annual cost to taxpayers of processing low-level arrests compared to summonses or diversion programs. Tisch says they can provide Criminal Justice Bureau budget data, and explains most arrests for summonsable offenses occur when the person is unidentifiable or has an open warrant.
chapters.title: Caban questions cost of low-level arrests vs. summonses

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What is the total annual cost to taxpayers of processing low-level arrests?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Through the criminal legal system compared to issuing summonses or utilizing diversion programs, while pointing out that maybe one way to reduce overtime is to stop dragging people through the system for taking up two seats on the subway.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We can get you information about the budget for our Criminal Justice Bureau, which is where we would spend money on processing arrests.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Is that fair, Chris?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Can you do that?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, so specifically the ask would be the cost to taxpayers of processing low-level arrests compared to the cost if those were through issuing summonses or utilizing some other diversion program.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Councilmember, I would say if you are...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: If we're talking about...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Talking about low-level offenses, the vast majority of the time, if we make an arrest for something that is otherwise summonsable, it will be for one of two reasons.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Either we cannot properly identify the person making

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: them ineligible for summons because then the summons means nothing or there is another warrant that is outstanding that they haven't responded to for

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: some other crime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I will say that the

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: unidentifiable is about 6.6% of those.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So the vast majority of the time, it is that person is wanted on an open warrant for some other crime.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So vast majority, can you give a percentage to that?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: How many of those are, if we're talking vast majority is well over 50%, which I,

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I don't know that that's accurate.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Alex can tell you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So in transit, when we do have to make an arrest, again, as the commissioner stated, it's 6.6% of the time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's because we were not able to properly identify.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The remainder of all the arrests are either because the person has a warrant and we have a legal obligation to bring them back to court, or they're being arrested for a higher level offense in addition to that low level offense.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You're saying that those are the only two other reasons?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, can I follow up on this?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Unidentifiable, wanted on a warrant, and third, being...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If you are being arrested for a higher level crime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In addition to the lower level crime.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, one more follow-up and then we've got to move on.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Well, it's not one.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I have a line of questions.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Just responding to that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Council member, we can sit together as long as you want and go through any questions that you have on this.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I know we have to, but I actually think that there's a lot of value in not just doing it sitting in a meeting, but doing it in an open hearing with the public so that they know how their money is being spent and what outcomes they're getting or not getting and what we should be able to expect.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I have, I'm...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I will pass it off to you, but I have a second round just specifically on that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Councilmember Areola.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And thank you to the Commissioner and the men and women of the NYPD for testifying today.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Before my questions, I want to recognize some excellent work by the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Last week, my office with Councilmember Areola's office and community members became aware of social media posts promoting a planned takeover at the Atlas Mall.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: We immediately shared that information with the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Queen's Borough Chief Russell Green and Captain Rios took the intelligence seriously and acted quickly, deploying a significant police presence around the mall.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: For a few hours, the Atlas Mall looked very well secure.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That deterrence worked and the takeover never materialized.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: When organizers began directing participants towards Middle Village Playground in my district, my office again alerted the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
