utterances.text: Officers responded quickly and prevented any disruption before it could occur.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And because of proactive policing, nothing happened.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's exactly what residents expect and deserve.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I also want to thank Inspector Wong at the 110th Precinct for his work addressing quality of life concerns, including issues surrounding the Amadeus nightclub, illegal car meetups,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: loud music, and other persistent neighborhood complaints.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I also want to thank Captain Fennessey of the 112th Precinct for addressing recent anti-Semitic vandalism affecting the communities in my district and Councilmember Shulman's district.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I appreciate the work these commanders are doing to keep our neighborhoods safe, address quality of life concerns before they become larger problems, and maintain confidence in public safety.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: With that, I have a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is there a department policy against publicly disclosing prison by prison staffing levels?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And if so, what is the rationale?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because my constituents regularly ask about these figures and they look and look and there's nowhere to be published.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the way the city council set up the law, we have various disclosure obligations, various wide range of staffing data that we are required to disclose, except to the extent

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that it will endanger public safety or endanger our officers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so, as you know, we provide a very wide range of data regarding staffing and deployment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: However, as it gets more and more granular, more and more specific, we do get more and more concerned about the safety aspect of this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If bad actors know precisely how we have staffed at a very granular level, that allows them to make strategic or tactical decisions that can be incredibly dangerous for the public or for our officers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that's the balance that we're constantly striking.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I do want to emphasize that the city council set up the law in this way.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is maximum transparency consistent with public safety and the safety of the officers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Which is why we tend, when we talk publicly about staffing numbers or report public and staffing numbers, to do it on the borough level.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But when you have asked in the past and other your colleagues have asked in the past about your staffing numbers, about concerns about staffing, we are very transparent with you in those discussions, take the feedback and then assign officers accordingly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about the legality of operating dirt bikes and ATVs on public streets and in parks. Chief LaPetrie confirms unregistered dirt bikes cannot be operated on NYC streets.
chapters.title: Wong asks about dirt bike and ATV legality on streets

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Recently, as the temperature gets warmer, we have a big problem about dirt bikes and ATVs driven on the streets and in parks in my district.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Question, is it legal to operate a dirt bike or ATV on a public street or in a park?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It is not.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Even if it's for minors or adults?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: An ATV, no, it is not.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What about for dirt bikes?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: If it's an unregistered dirt bike, no, it cannot.

utterances.speaker: Mike LaPetrie
utterances.text: It cannot be on a New York City street.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I see.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Oh, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Chair, I would like to have more questions for round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Ariola asks for crime data broken down by council district. Tisch calls it a good idea and says they can try to provide it, noting it would be high enough level to not reveal precinct-specific deployment.
chapters.title: Ariola requests crime data by council district

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Areola, followed by Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you for always coming fully prepared and for testifying with full transparency and clarity.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We really do appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Many of us here want to see the NYPD get more resources than I'm hearing you saying we're getting, so that's what we're here for.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: As far as numbers, you had them categorized by borough and how crime has decreased by borough, especially retail crimes in specific boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Do you have that?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Drill down as far as council districts because oftentimes we get calls in our council offices that says well it's we can't even live here anymore you know we're ready to go but we want to be able to tell them like you know you're our

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Numbers by council district.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: How good a...

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That's a good idea.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I don't think we have that available now, but that would...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: be, I think, high enough level that it wouldn't be at the precinct level.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That's actually a really good idea about how to get more granular than just borough.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And we can try to provide that.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yeah, so this way we can fight back any narrative that says, you know, crime is out of control because I do, I love the Q teams, I love our community affairs officers, I agree that we need more heavy toes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I'm so happy that you're open to that, you know, in some of our areas.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The other thing is, you said that a lot of

chapters.summary: Ariola asks how NYPD will navigate conflicts between federal grant requirements and sanctuary city laws. Tisch explains the smaller grants conflict with sanctuary law, but the larger Homeland Security grant cut is about allocation levels, not sanctuary city conflicts.
chapters.title: Ariola and Tisch discuss federal grants and sanctuary city conflicts

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: of your funding come from federal grants and you're going to continue to fight to get those federal grants but there's some language that precludes you from being able to apply for those federal grants and i can only imagine that that includes working fully with federal law enforcement

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: agencies A lot of it is that a lot of the problematic language for us conflicts with the sanctuary city law.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So how will you manage that or navigate that when there is a conflict there?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Because you certainly need that funding.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So there's two separate things going on.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The first are the grants, which tend to be the much smaller grants.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They do not represent a majority of the...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Counterterrorism grant funding we get where we have that conflict with sanctuary city law and we are

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Not eligible to apply.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The more meaningful chunk of money is not about a conflict between sanctuary city law and eligibility requirements.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It's the Department of Homeland Security's allocation levels to New York State, which were dramatically cut

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: under Secretary Noem, and we were hopeful that the president would overrule.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Unfortunately, we have not seen those dollars yet for federal fiscal year 25.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Joseph, followed by Councilmember Hanks.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Joseph asks about youth arrests for major felonies and collaboration with other agencies. Staff report school arrests are down 4.8% this year, and describe monthly meetings with DOE partners and quarterly advocate meetings.
chapters.title: Joseph questions NYPD on youth arrests and school collaboration

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Hi, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Always good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I'm right here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Correct me if I'm wrong.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Youth arrests for major felonies have increased in each of the last fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: At the same time, major felony crimes in schools relatively flat over the past year.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: period.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How can we improve those stats?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You know how I feel about that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And what other agencies and programs can NYPD reach I believe would be great partners?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Hold on, I have two people.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: He's explaining to me the first part of your question.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: What's the second part of the question?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How are you working with other agencies to make sure we're bringing down the numbers among young people that we're seeing in these cases?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Alex is going to answer the first part of the question.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: After he explained the question, all right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I can answer the first part.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry, there's lots of different things going on at once.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All right, so arrests in schools are actually down.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So this year they're down 4.8%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're seeing both less crime and less arrests in schools at the same time.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: But how are you also working in collaboration with schools?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: For example, I knew before there was a division working on school.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: There was a drone for subway surfing.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We lost a young person at 14 years old to subway surfing.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The last hearing that I had here with transportation and YPD and New York City Public Schools as interagency, these are conversations that all agencies should be having.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So where are we on that in terms of collaboration and working?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: On all of things.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Collaboration with DOE.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I hope if you ask them, they will say the same thing.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But from our perspective, the collaboration with DOE specifically has never been better.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'll let Tanya speak about the work that we're doing with DOE.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And then I want to speak a little bit about the work we're doing with them with Subway Surfing.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How are you doing?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I'm good.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We take arrests.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We work with our DOE partners really well.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Oh, you facilitated that whole thing.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You had the first meeting.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 65% of incidents that occur in schools now are mitigated by DOE.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We meet with our DOE partners on a monthly basis.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We also meet with the advocates on a quarterly basis to try to work with the advocates in DOE to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we don't have to arrest and have DOE take on more of the incidents.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So you're working interagency, so you're working with Project Pivot, who's on the ground, which is the cure violence groups they have in schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, 351 schools, Project Pivot is it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We provide support in those schools during the times that Project Pivot is going on.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about crisis intervention team training numbers. Staff report approximately 3,384 personnel trained in de-escalation and restorative justice. Joseph also raises concerns about broken metal detectors causing student delays, and Tisch announces 40 new scanners have been ordered.
chapters.title: Joseph asks about crisis intervention training and school metal detectors

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, in your crisis intervention team training, the numbers I was for uniform members in communication skills, de-escalation, and behavior and restorative justice, of course.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we have approximately...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 3,384 personnel trained.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: In de-escalation, restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We received a couple of calls around your metal detectors are not working at schools and students are being late.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How are you addressing that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we know of three incidents that occurred this year thus far.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The school safety executive leadership responded and spoke to all three principals.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have now have a in-house training of our agents to help mitigate if a scanner breaks within 30 minutes, we have agents

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: rectifying the issue and as well as now there's a direct notification and investigation done once we are aware that a school is late because of scanning issues or personnel.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Based on the feedback that you've given me about this specific thing in the past, I am pleased to tell you that we have ordered 40 new scanners

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: to replace some of the old equipment that we have because it is not acceptable to be late for a school because of scanning.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we received 10 of those scanners on Friday, last Friday.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, good.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And what's the timeline to put them in place for them, or are they already in place?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're working on that now with a deal we're working with Mark and our executives and school safety to procure where exactly they're going to go or replace.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: This council had passed the bill for 988 to be in New York City public schools.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about 988 calls from young people in schools. Tisch says NYPD doesn't collect demographic data on 988 transfers and suggests asking 988 operators. Joseph also requests mental health 911 call numbers by council district.
chapters.title: Joseph asks about 988 usage in NYC public schools

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How many calls do you get from young people?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Do you track that?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We would not have that data.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That data, you should need to ask 988, the people who run that, because all we do is transfer the call so we don't collect the demographic.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And how many mental health-related calls were this training would be applicable if you were to do it?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You can get back to me on that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Mental health related 911 calls?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We can get you the number of mental health 911 calls.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I know that in the first 15 days, 204 calls were transferred to 988, but that is nowhere near the total number.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You're asking for the total number of any EDP call we get.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee announces a brief five-minute recess at 12:10 PM, with the hearing to resume at 12:15 PM.
chapters.title: Chair Lee calls brief recess

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And as my colleague had mentioned earlier, it would be great to look at the breakdowns.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Council District in order for us to work better with you, probably provide more resources and provide more support.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We will get that to you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Chair, I'll come around my second round.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it is 1210.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're going to take a brief five-minute recess.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 1210, so we'll see everybody at 1215.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And please, just five minutes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It is 1210.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'll see everybody at 1215.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The low-level transit bus, they're 50% up to 50,000, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I have all that data.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'll make it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Let me get it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'll hand it to you right now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to see the council here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's perfect.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Barbara
utterances.text: My name is Barbara.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Barbara
utterances.text: You almost explained the job.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Barbara
utterances.text: That's just one of the things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Barbara
utterances.text: My coach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Barbara
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Barbara
utterances.text: I think they were happy with the place.

chapters.summary: Chair Feliz resumes the hearing, announcing Councilmember Hanks will begin followed by Councilmember Brooks-Powers.
chapters.title: Hearing resumes after recess

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, I think we're gonna get started.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Everyone, please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We're getting ready to resume the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Hanks asks about the Office of Community Safety and whether NYPD has identified programs or gaps for partnership. Tisch says no programs are shifting and highlights the mental health crisis as an area for potential OCS collaboration.
chapters.title: Hanks questions Tisch on Office of Community Safety

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, so we're going to resume with Councilmember Hanks and then Councilmember Brooks-Powers.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: It's good to see everyone.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Commissioner Tisch.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So I'd just like to circle back to the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: And just for the record, can we kind of...

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Whatever you know, I give a list of existing programs under OCS if there is.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I do not have that list.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: You don't have the list, okay.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So as a holistic structure, it's still quite vague what this is.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Does NYPD have any idea of what they would want to partner with or need support with or any gaps that would help define this OCS?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: As I mentioned, we do a tremendous amount of work with a lot of the offices that have been moved under OCS.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The Office to End Gender-Based Violence is a shining example of that partnership, and that work continues.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Look, I think the mental health crisis in New York City is definitely an issue that affects the New York City Police Department profoundly.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so I would look forward to working with the Office of Community Safety to understand ideas that they have for addressing it.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Hanks asks why Staten Island Borough Patrol budget dropped $7.9M from FY26. Tisch explains the decrease reflects the reorganization moving evidence collection and DV units from patrol to detective bureau, not actual resource reductions.
chapters.title: Hanks questions Tisch on Staten Island patrol budget decrease

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: I'll have a line of questioning that's a bit more local.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: So the Borough Patrol of Staten Island is funded at $103.2 million in the FY27 executive plan, down by $7.9 million from the $111 million FY26 adopted level,

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: primarily in the 20th precinct headquarters at 78 Richmond Terrace and St.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: George with parts in the 121st.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Why does the Borough Patrol of Staten Island fall 7.9 million below FY26 adopted level in the executive plan, and what does it mean for authorized officer counts at the 120 and the 121?

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: precincts.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So as I mentioned, we did a reorganization of department resources last year to better

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: to streamline, for example, our response to domestic violence, and also to make our evidence collection teams work more closely with our crime scene unit.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so in doing that, we move those two units from under the patrol services

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Bureau to the detective bureau.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Those movements happened across the entire department and they certainly did not take resources away from community members.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It just looks on paper like we've taken resources out of patrol, but those functions still remain just assigned under a different command code.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Brooks-Powers thanks NYPD for recovery efforts for a drowning victim, raises concerns about gun violence surge in Southeast Queens, and asks about social media monitoring for teen takeovers. She also asks about NYPD coordination with DOC on Rikers population.
chapters.title: Brooks-Powers questions Tisch on gun violence, social media, and Rikers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Brooks-Powers?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, and thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I first want to thank you, Patrick Gordon, Ryan, for bringing the team back out when we unfortunately lost 18-year-old Brandon Figueroa at sea in Rockaway.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: It was meaningful.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The mother was definitely appreciative.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm hopeful that the body that came ashore yesterday is Brandon's body.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But we know that PD is still maintaining regular communication with us.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I also want to thank Chief Obey and her division for the work that they've been doing, including Police Officer Marion Manick, who has been amazing since your visit to Southeast Queens.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Regarding the illegal truck parking in Southeast Queens, it has been tremendous.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That makes me so happy to hear.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And then I'm going to just ask you three questions.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: If I need to repeat anything, let me know.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So first, Southeast Queens has seen a surge in gun violence in the last few months, including the tragic shooting of 15-year-old Jaden Pierre in the Deputy Speaker's District, which is a neighboring district to mine.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As summer approaches and our youth are out of school, how is NYPD prepared to prevent these types of tragic shootings from happening in the first place?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Often these violent outbursts are the result of organized takeovers that spread on social media.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm curious to know how the NYPD is detecting these social media trends and getting ahead of potentially violent incidents and how

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: NYPD is communicating to the local precincts when viral social media trends pop up in specific geographies.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'm not smart enough to remember all of these.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Can we do them one at a time?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Well, because of the interest of time.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Okay, okay.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So say them and then you'll help me.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I'll repeat whatever I need to.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So from the end of December 24 to October 25, NYC admitted on average 49 more people to jail each month and it discharged.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: the jail population by around 500 people.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As we approach the transition to borough-based jails, our city must work to reduce the incarcerated population.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I wanted to know how NYPD specifically is working with Department of Corrections, MOCJ, the DAs, and other agencies to work towards a common ground and ensure we can successfully close Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So just wanting to know that coordination is happening there.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about truck route enforcement training for officers. Tisch commits to providing specific refresher training for precincts with truck enforcement issues. Brooks-Powers also asks about social media-driven takeovers, with LaPetrie detailing field intelligence officer monitoring.
chapters.title: Brooks-Powers and Tisch discuss truck enforcement and social media monitoring

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And lastly, we have consistently heard from community members and local precincts that police officers are not aware of local laws regarding the truck routes, truck island, and other park-related...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Trucking related matters.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The result is that there are no, there is no consistent enforcement against trucks breaking the laws.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: What steps is NYPD taking to ensure that all police officers are trained on this issue and able to enforce traffic laws against trucks that are not compliant?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I'll give you an example.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: We did a walkthrough with DOT.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And there was a truck that was idling on Springfield Boulevard.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And we had a local officer there as well, but it was clear that it was not clear in terms of how long that truck was able to legally idle in that location.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay, I'll start with that one first, and then you have to remind me about the other one.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Sorry, Coach.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: If there is a precinct where we have to do specific refresher training on specific laws like truck idling,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We are very happy to do that and to do that quickly.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So my team can connect with you to understand what the very specific issue is.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And you have my commitment that those officers in that command will be refreshed in.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Those issues.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You asked, I think your second to last question, working backwards here, was about Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I'll just be blunt.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: NYPD does not think in terms of the size of the correction population or the Rikers population.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That is a matter specifically for the Department of Correction.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We don't enjoy arresting people, but we will take action when there is a crime committed.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so my hope is...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: My expectation is that the city will account for that in their planning for correction capacity.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And just to clarify, because I think we're in alignment and not necessarily wanting people who should be in custody to be in custody, it's more in terms of looking at the low-level offenses that I think you mentioned earlier.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: that could be a summons, for example, but understanding that coordination because we are seeing an increase in the arrests that are happening and we're not seeing that revolving door for those that may be

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: instances where there could be summonses or at low level.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We allow our officers to use discretion.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Where you are seeing increases in arrests, like where you're seeing arrests

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: for something that would otherwise be summonsable is generally in three areas.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: One, the person cannot be properly identified.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Which is out of the officer's control.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: If the person cannot provide ID or the department cannot properly identify them, they are not eligible for a summons.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Doesn't matter how low the offense is.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And if they are able to provide it, oftentimes they are.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Second is if they are wanted on an open warrant that they themselves have not

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: responded to and are required by law to respond to.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And we come across them for a low level thing, we will arrest them and return them to, they need to be returned to court to deal with that open warrant.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I just don't want their

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I reject the narrative that the NYPD is on a spree of arresting people.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: people for low-level offenses when the actual data shows otherwise.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Just going back to the first one, it was more centered around the takeovers and the social media

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: efforts that I know PD has technology and they are viewing different social medias to try to stay up to speed with what's happening with the young people,

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: but wanting to know what that coordination with the local precinct looks like so that we can be more proactive and responding to it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The chief is going to take this one, but I just want to say social media is driving right now.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Two big things that I know are of particular concern to many of you here.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Car meets, and teen takeovers, which are exploding across the country, and I expect only to get more intense as school lets out.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We monitor social media for both of those

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: things, but your question is specifically about team takeovers and the chief will let you know how we approach those and how we coordinate with the local precincts on them.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So actually, the local precinct is the first line of defense when it comes to monitoring social media.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: We have field intelligence officers assigned to

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: all 78 precincts across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Our housing PSAs and also our transit PSAs.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: We do have a centralized social media team out of our detective bureau, but also out of our

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: intelligence bureau, but it starts with the local precinct.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So if a field intelligence officer is monitoring social media in the 101 in Far Rockaway, he or she, through our

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: ECMS system, will share that intelligence, not just locally, but citywide.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: And we, you know, again, we're very good at knowing the platforms that we need to look at, but that's real time.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Like, that's real time.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: We don't wait.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: That gets sent out immediately, and everybody is always working together, and we've gotten very, very good with the car meets and also the team takeovers with sharing that intelligence, right?

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: One of the four principles of, actually two out of the four principles of CompStat is timely and accurate intelligence and rapid deployment, and that's a staple of the New York City Police Department.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I just want to add on your last question.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I lost the forest through the trees, threw me off for a second.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Low-level arrests don't end up in Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You end up in Rikers for a felony.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Low-level arrests are not driving the Rikers population.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So not everyone at Rikers are there for a felony.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I know Councilmember Caban is going to probably dig more into this later, so I'm not going to take up more of the committee's time on it.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I do want to thank you for your...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: responses and just make a plug for the 101 precinct in terms of capital needs because that precinct right now is highly dilapidated and under-resourced as well.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, we have Councilmember Felder versus Councilmember, and next we have Councilmember Brewer followed by Ressler.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Felder asks about community policing contacts, past programs using Orthodox Jewish officers, traffic cameras, and bicycle licensing. He requests written responses due to a dental appointment. Tisch offers to answer immediately but Felder insists on written responses.
chapters.title: Felder asks about community policing, cameras, and bicycle licensing

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Felder, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I apologize because I'm going to be leaving for an implant, not in the brain, in the teeth.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: But I don't want you to answer my questions first, but if somebody could call or let me know afterwards, I'd be very happy.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: So first of all, I want to thank your colleague here who spent hours at the hearings on anti-Semitism, listening to questions and answering them.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I've never, ever been at a hearing when someone...

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: didn't say once, at least when I was awake, didn't say once, I'll get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: He answered every single question.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I never saw that in my life.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I never saw that in my life.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I think there's something wrong with him.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I think you should check it out.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Yeah, okay.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: In terms of my questions, if you could just, if somebody could let me know.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Number one, I'd like to know in terms of the community policing, if somebody could let me know if there's a particular person that...

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: It's assigned to the district.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I don't know how exactly it works.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I'd like to know who that person is so that I could call.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We'll get you in touch with the community affairs officers in your precincts.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Yeah, if somebody could.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Yeah, but I asked you not to answer my question.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Oh, sorry, I missed that.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Yeah, so if you could please listen.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: Yeah, please.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: The other thing is that for years ago, they used to have a program where they had

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: officers, for example, that looked not great, but looked like me, for example,

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: policing, you know, were actually cops walking around that looked like Orthodox Jews.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And, you know, I think that the concept, when they publicized it, that alone was helpful.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I'm curious why that has not been used in terms of fighting anti-Semitism.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: That's two, three years ago.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I don't know how many.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I think it's about 15 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: They installed certain cameras on top of the lights to fight crime.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I don't know if those cameras are operational or not.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And finally, when I was in the Senate about four years, five years ago, I sponsored a bill to actually have insurance.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: and licenses and other things, you know, on certain bicycles, even regular bicycles.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And I've seen some talk about it, and I'd like to know what the, you know, whether the police department

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: you know, has any opinions on it.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: And I thank you very, very much for everything that you're doing.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: At your request, we will get back to you in writing on all of those things.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But I don't want that held against us in terms of our live response rate.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Because I could have answered all of that right here and now.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I'm not listening to you because I make the jokes.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: You're not.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: It's my show, you know.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Fair enough.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Great, thank you, Councilmember Felder.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Councilmember Ressler.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Brewer asks about retail theft trends and prevention efforts. Tisch reports retail theft down 19% citywide, with clearance rates up from 41% to 51%, crediting aggregate theft law changes and recidivism-focused enforcement.
chapters.title: Brewer questions Tisch on retail theft reduction efforts

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I wish I had Simcha Felder's sense of humor.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I do want to thank Central Park Precinct, 2-4 and 2-0, Simca.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Those three I want to thank.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Retail theft is an issue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In one situation, the Duane Reade put in full-time security, and they have zero theft.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But the nearby Duane Reade is now still overwhelmed with security problems because they don't have one, and then thefts.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I didn't know how.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How much time or effort or money you put into the idea of retail theft and if you think the numbers are going down or up?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Retail theft numbers in New York City are going down, like way down, and it is wonderful to see.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It's been happening over the past two years, and Chief LaPetrie will tell you all about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But specifically Manhattan North.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So as far as retail theft, as the commissioner said, this year we've seen a 19% reduction in retail theft in New York City across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: When you look at the five boroughs, geographic boroughs, it's led by Staten Island at 41%, followed by Queens at about 21%, and Manhattan at about 20%.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: What I'm really proud of is the men and women both on the patrol side and the investigative side.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: when it comes to clearance rate, which means you commit a shoplift and you get arrested.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So just four years ago, actually three years ago, that clearance rate was about 41% of the time we made an arrest with a shoplifting complaint.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: This year, it's over 51%.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: And that includes low-level shoplifting crimes.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So again, I thank the detectives that have a very high caseload to take on those petty larceny cases.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: They've done a phenomenal job.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: We've used the tweak in the state law as our friend, meaning the aggregate amount of merchandise that somebody steals from a particular store, whether it be a CVS on 51st Street or a CVS on 55th Street, we're able to aggregate that.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: And again, it's built off of recidivism.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: And we've done very, very well with that.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: We still have a lot of work to do, but we're doing well.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about NYPD warehouse consolidation and costs. Ryan says they're actively working with DCAS to consolidate warehouses including the cannabis warehouse, and will provide an update on consolidation efforts.
chapters.title: Brewer and Ryan discuss NYPD warehouse consolidation

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Warehouses.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I went to the decast warehouse in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's large.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It has lots of extra space.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, how many warehouses does NYPD have?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've been in the cannabis warehouse.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There's a lot of cannabis in there.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I just was wondering how many warehouses you have, what the cost is, can you reduce?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So we're actually actively working right now on ideally consolidating some of the warehouses, including the warehouse you're referencing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The cannabis one.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: There are other things there as well, but yes, there is cannabis.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: At that facility.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: Unfortunately, we did have funding.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: In 20 before 2020 to do a full consolidated property clerk which would have taken care of this that funding doesn't do this so we're trying to be creative now and look at

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: properties in some of the outer boroughs where we think we may be able to

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: get out of some of the leases that are a problem.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can you update us on that?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know you had a fire in one, too, in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So can you update us on warehouses?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I do believe that around the city, all agencies can be consolidating, not just PD, and I think it would save some money.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: I think that's a great idea, and we are working closely with DCAS, so I think the best thing would be for us to put together for you an update on what we're doing with DCAS to address the warehouse needs, with exactly the goal you're saying, to have it be more state-of-the-art so we don't have issues,

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: and then also to consolidate so that we reduce the footprint.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about the lack of a tow pound in Manhattan since Cuomo closed Pier 76. Ryan says real estate costs remain challenging but they continue looking with DCAS. Brewer also asks about NYCHA PSA funding, with Tisch reporting record crime reductions in NYCHA.
chapters.title: Brewer asks about Manhattan tow pound and NYCHA safety

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Toll pound, always my question.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No one in Manhattan, is that a problem?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are you ever going to find a toll pound in the borough of Manhattan?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: It continues to be, unfortunately, the same challenge that we had in the past.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: It's just since, it's unfortunately just the cost of real estate in Manhattan is really challenging.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: We've had a few sites where we thought we might be able to have a somewhat smaller footprint but still have something so that people didn't have to travel quite as far if they were booted or other things like that with their car.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: Unfortunately, those have fallen through, but we do continue to look with DCAS to see if we can restore a footprint to Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, just because I had the same question.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Since Cuomo closed Pier 76.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That was quite a few years ago.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then what, NYCHA, Bureau getting any more money, less money in terms of PSA, that is a really important division of NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The reason I ask is I believe, I love NYCHA, I love the residents, I strongly believe that if we were to focus on NYCHA, not just you,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But in a holistic way, you'd have far fewer issues.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I am very proud to tell you that in NYCHA specifically, New York City is having its safest start to the year on record.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: With major crime there, down 10%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Murders, down 43%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Shooting incidents, down 24%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Shooting victims, down 30%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Robberies, down 24%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And burglaries, down 19%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The crime story in NYCHA this year, or the crime picture in NYCHA this year, is vastly improved from last year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: NYCHA and the Bronx are the two places where we are seeing the largest crime reductions.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I give a tremendous amount of credit to the chief of department and the chief of crime control strategies on their precision

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: policing, which is focusing additional resources where the violent crime occurs.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And sometimes you can set a strategy.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: put a strategy in place and don't see results.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: In this one, they put the strategy in place and they are driving real results.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Restler expresses concern about NYPD's response to incarceration questions, noting Brooklyn Jail will open in three years with half the capacity needed. He also raises deeply troubling Be Heard data showing only 0.01% of 911 calls being responded to.
chapters.title: Restler challenges Tisch on incarceration and Be Heard response rates

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What else is needed for NYCHA?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Oh, we could have a very long conversation about that, but I think in terms of policing,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: resources, you are seeing very good outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: There's obviously a lot more work that can be done.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I think a lot of it is on the social services side, but from policing at this time, we feel like we have the right strategy in place and the right resources too.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Deliver results.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Ressler, followed by Councilmember Banks.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee and Chair Felice.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Appreciate both of your leadership.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And Commissioner, it's always good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I disagree with you on a number of issues, but I appreciate your competence and the integrity that you've returned to the department and think that a number of things are moving in a positive direction, and I want to credit you for that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I also just want to take a moment to shout out Chief Obey and her XO, Chief O'Sullivan, who have been really responsive to some of the issues that we face in the 8-4, and I'm really grateful for that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They've made a real effort, and it's been very much appreciated, so I just want to take a moment to say something nice before I give you a hard time.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Troubled by your response to Councilmember Brooks-Powers'comments, questions,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: around incarceration.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Just to put a finer point on it, we're about three years away from the opening of the Brooklyn Jail.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have twice as many people incarcerated in Rikers right now from Brooklyn as what that jail can accommodate.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We need an all-hands approach from the police department, from the Department of Correction, from the courts, and from a whole lot of other stakeholders, including the council, to work together to drive down incarceration in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I'd hope that you'd be a partner in that effort so that we can reduce unnecessary incarceration, reduce the amount of time that people are incarcerated.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: All working together as much as possible to achieve the smaller and safer vision of what Close Rikers is about.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I was also deeply troubled by the data you shared around Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I'll just walk through the data that I think I heard, and you can correct me if I said something.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Hold on, I've got to get it out.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You get it out.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'll talk while you get it out.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So 4.3 million calls to 911 annually.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You said that in the first four months of this year,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Only 17,961 of the calls you received were appropriate for Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Hold on.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Yeah, Alex says yes.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes, that is correct.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So that's about 54,000 calls annually.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So 54,000 calls out of 4.3 million is that 1.2% of calls into 911 are appropriate for Be Heard, according to the police department's view.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The total number of nonviolent, nonweapon calls from emotionally disturbed persons.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So we believe that if you say that that's going to be constant every year or, you know, the percentage is about 2% of 911 calls for service under the current criteria.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I had you at 1.2 based on the first four months of this year.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You're saying in 2004 it was a little higher, but in that range.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So firstly, I just think for this administration, it's taking a different approach that we want to invest in alternative responses for nonviolent situations.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Saying that 1% to 2% of our calls is appropriate for Be Heard, I think is unfortunate.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But even more troubling, which is not on your plate, is that for 97% of the calls year to date, just 1,411 out of the 17,961 calls that were appropriate for Be Heard, according to you, just...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 2.6% of those calls were actually responded to by Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I have 1,411 out of 17,961.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: 7.9% from January 1st to April 30th.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So to put a finer point on all of that,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That is 0.01% of calls that Be Heard is responding to.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 0.01% of calls.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That is so small, it's a rounding error, it's negligible.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Does the program even exist?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're spending gosh knows how many tens, hundreds of millions of dollars on this program.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It was functioning at some point.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It doesn't seem like it's even functioning today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we have some questions for the fire department.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: and health and hospitals for why be heard is essentially irrelevant.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think that of the lowest of low-hanging fruit in the vision of what the Office of Community Safety can be and eventually the Department of Community Safety, to my colleagues, is that nicely done?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Nan.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We got a co-sponsor in the room.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This is the lowest of low-hanging fruit, and it's an embarrassment.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Well, I'll do round two because I talk too much.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Brooklyn Central Booking has been a serious problem.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm grateful that your team has finally confirmed for us this morning that we'll get a tour.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We've seen too many fatalities and serious issues there.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And in round two, I'd like to dig in more on response time.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We'll adjourn to round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we, oh no, not yet.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then now we have Councilmember Banks.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Go ahead.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Banks raises concerns about the Penn Track prostitution issue in his district. Chief LaPetrie details vice enforcement operations and says it's the number one location for enforcement. Banks also asks about splitting the 75th precinct, with Ryan noting a new precinct costs $108M.
chapters.title: Banks questions NYPD on Penn Track and 75th precinct split

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and good to see you, Commissioner, to your team.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Pentrack, the Pentrack.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We still have concerns and frustrations around the Pentrack.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: If we can get an update for the 42nd Council District as to what's going on, and obviously with the warmer weather that is approaching, well, it's here already, residents constantly complain about the Pentrack.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I know there's been ongoing stuff, operations back there, but it would be a great idea if you kept the community updated as to what was being done.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Obviously not to censor.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Their stuff, but at least let the community know the progress that is being made, or if any progress is being made, as opposed to continually see the visual we always see, and that's sex workers walking the streets back there.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And also, let me finish, the issue, you know, there's been multiple social media videos

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: that have been made about the Penn Track ongoing, and, you know, this has continued to put a blight or attempts to put a blight on the 42nd Council District.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: But if residents are hearing and seeing PD is working on their behalf as far as to try to solve this issue, I think that would go a long way with helping out as far as on the PR side or communicating to the community.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So if we can get an update, I greatly appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The chief of department will answer the question.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So obviously, Chris, you know, many, many years in New York.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: To dealing with the Pennsylvania track, in and around that Linden Boulevard area.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: You're right, it is stubborn, and it is somewhat frustrating in that with the change in the law,

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: and a class action lawsuit that happened years ago, it has hurt our local enforcement, our local enforcement, meaning the precincts aren't really able to deal with the actual prostitution,

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: John situation, but our vice enforcement unit

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: consistently, consistently hits the pen track, as does our human trafficking team in and around some of those stubborn hotels, motels, 100 Jersey, things of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: I've also tasked the precinct commander to put a plan in place to deal with

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: low-level crime in and around those hotels along the Penn Track, meaning the quality of life issues that we do see.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: That facilitate some of the violence when it comes to, you know, the prostitution.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: But I will tell you, it is the number one location in the city of New York when it comes to enforcement by our specialty units, namely VICE and the human trafficking side on VICE.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I know there was efforts up in Roosevelt also.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So if we can get the same type of attention, that would definitely, I know it made an impact in that particular district, I believe, from what I've heard.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So if we can get that same type of attention, I'll greatly appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And the last thing pertaining to the 75th precinct, and I represent close to four commands for the city council, but particularly for the 75th precinct, and it's been a long-held

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: complaint or frustration, knowing that the 75th precinct is probably one of the largest commands throughout the entire city.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: When will we be able to get an opportunity to split that command?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: You know, on the southern part of the district, we had a major increase in population with the housing that's being built on that side.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I believe it's long time needed for the 75th to be split.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I mean, it goes all the way from Starrett City all the way into on Jamaica, up in Sandy's district.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: The residents want that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You don't need to tell the chief of department that he is a former CEO of the 75.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I had a great relationship with him when he was the former.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So he understands that.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We just need to see action.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We need to see something done pertaining to that particular issue.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Because this goes back over 20, 30 years, the need to have the command split.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Even if it's some type of satellite that was put on the southern side, like in the Gateway area, it will go a long way.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: I'll just answer very, very quickly.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Just like the police commissioner talks about the Bronx getting the most resources as far as now two patrol boroughs, by far the 7-5 gets the most resources in all of Brooklyn North other than some precincts in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: And again...

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: You know, I had the most RMPs, I had the most cops, I had the most cameras, I had the most August cameras.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So, pointed, it is a large command with a lot going on, but that's why we put, you know, D.I.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Hines there has a lot of experience.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: D.I.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Hines is doing a pretty good job.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Being in East New York, and he's going to do a phenomenal job.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I will just ask Christine to speak about the process to build a new, to create a new

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: New precinct.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So when we look at that, we take into account, obviously, all the factors in the precinct, the crime, the population, everything you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: But there's also, obviously, the real estate element of it, the most recent newly built.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: precinct, the 116, was $108 million.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So right now we don't have funding for a new precinct, but if that were to move forward, we would need those resources and, you know, work.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'm so happy to continue the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We look forward to having that side of our conversation.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee asks about the front-loaded capital plan with $432.9M in FY26 dropping to $16.4M in FY29. Ryan explains the front-loading is driven by vehicle capitalization and the Rodman's Neck project, with projects shifting as construction progresses.
chapters.title: Lee and Ryan discuss capital plan front-loading

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Actually, talking about capital and going into that theme, so looking at the capital plan, it appears to be heavily front-loaded with $432.9 million planned in the current fiscal year, and then it drops to about $16.4 million in FY29.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we know that capital plans are generally front-loaded, and there are some large capital projects wrapping up in the next few years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Could you generally explain some of the big projects happening in the next few years that drive some of these changes and also give some context on the planning of projects?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So part of the reason that it's so front-loaded is, as you mentioned, large projects.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So one is, as we mentioned earlier, the infusion of capital funding now for vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So there's a significant amount of funding early in the plan so that we can jumpstart continuing to replace those vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: And also our largest single project is also in the early years of the plan, which is Rodman's Neck.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So we're working on that, which should be done by the summer of 28.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So those are the two biggest.
