utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: and then the others are spread out more throughout the plan.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: What we do when we plan our capital budget, it changes with each financial plan because we take a look at where we are in the construction process and whether or not we have CPs yet, and obviously with the procurement of contracts.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: The drawing's been done.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So things tend to shift.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: Certain things we will push out, but then at the same time, OMB works very well with us, and they're very flexible.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: If something's moving more quickly or we have a need earlier than it's reflected in the capital plan, we will look to accelerate those funds.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about asset forfeiture funding usage. Ryan reports a $52M balance with $38M already accounted for, leaving $14M available, used for supplies, equipment, critical training, and investigation-related initiatives.
chapters.title: Lee and Ryan discuss asset forfeiture fund usage

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then for asset forfeiture funding, which I mentioned earlier in the statement, preliminary budget response, council called upon NYPD to utilize the asset forfeiture dollars for eligible costs that expand the investigative

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: resources before calling on city tax levy dollars.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how much asset forfeiture has NYPD used over the course of this fiscal year, and what are the costs associated with it?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So right now we have a balance of about $52 million in asset forfeiture.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: Of that, and sometimes it's just the timing of how it's drawn down, of that, $28 million, apologies, $38 million has already been accounted for, so there's $14 million balance.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So I completely understand the perspective of utilize this first instead of using city funds.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: We work very closely to establish what is eligible for asset forfeiture.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: There are very strict guidelines on the uses of asset forfeiture, and the things we utilize it for are things that are really critical to the department.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: We use it for...

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: supplies and equipment.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: We use it for critical training for our officers and travel to and from that training, as well as supplies and equipment and initiatives tied to investigations.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So those are the types of things where if we weren't using this money, we would then have to use city funds.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So most of it is actually already accounted for, and then we'll go forward and replenish those items I just listed.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So if not for using it there, we are in fact not just trying to hold it to do something, it's just supplementing the normal things that we would utilize our city funds for as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the racial equity plan goal to track racial disparities in stops and the How Many Stops Act data. Tisch reports Level 3 stops dropped from 680,000 in 2011 to 26,405 in 2025, with staff detailing the Racial Disparities Review Committee working with the monitor.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss stop-and-frisk data and racial equity tracking

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then the deputy speaker had to leave, but she wanted me to ask this question.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Another goal of going back to the, I think, the racial equity plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Another goal of NYPD's in the preliminary racial equity plan is to quantify, track, and analyze racial disparities in stops, risks, and searches in an effort to reduce those disparities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is listed as a medium-term goal to be completed by 2028.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you have any data on stop and frisk from data collected from the How Many Stops Act that the council passed?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So with regard to the How Many Stops Act, as part of that law, we put out data with regard to level one, level two, and level three, and level three encounters.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So that data is put out.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I believe it's quarterly.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I have the data.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So investigative encounters, 2025, full year.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Level one.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: 2 million plus.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For context perspective, that number was 680,000 in 2011.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Which went level three?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, 680.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So we went from 680,000 level three stops in 2011 to 26,405 level three stops in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And this year, so far, we have about 16% less or fewer stops, level three stops.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, across, okay, level three, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and how will the training initiative goals affect this aspect of the department's work, and how will this review inform you of the relevant units and commands for retraining?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we've created something called the Racial Disparities Review Committee.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's something, it's part of the plan, it's also something we're doing with the monitor, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So there's actually, the monitor team are quite involved and obviously don't want to speak for the monitor.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But what we're doing is we are looking at data, doing that in a very deliberate way and thoughtful way, working off of a model that the monitor's team used and sort of further developing that out ourselves.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And in looking at that, to the extent you have outlier commands, we want to understand what's happening, what's driving disparities, and just really in part to understand it, but then

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: if there are disparities that we can't account for, making sure we can address them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we're looking at department level data, the department as a whole and trends over time, but then also we want to understand what's happening in any outlier commands.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then how do you track that data?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is it by borough?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is it by precinct?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So again, we're sort of developing kind of an internal model, building off of the monitors team's work.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we have

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: both sort of in-house talent, people we've hired to work on this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We also have some external help as well.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And then again, that model is still being built out.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're not there yet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is a very complicated process, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's going to play out over time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But in doing that, we're looking at department-level data.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We can look, ultimately, we'll be able to look at borough-level, command-level data as well.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're not, again, but this is very much a process that's going to play out over many months.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, and I'll pass it to Chair Felice.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks again whether increasing headcount could decrease overtime. Tisch says it would help for special events but not all categories, and notes she's already made a dent with 10% overtime reductions. Feliz asks for the top three things needed to make a bigger dent.
chapters.title: Feliz presses Tisch on overtime reduction strategies

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: A few more questions on overtime, and then we'll move on to other issues.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And trust me, I wish I did not have to spend this much time talking about overtime, but obviously a big part of the budget.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Overtime.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: To what extent do you think increasing headcount can potentially decrease overtime?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Again, there are trade-offs, so I can't answer that question specifically.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It certainly, if we had a larger headcount, it certainly would bring down the overtime budget this summer for the special events, meaning we wouldn't have to go to the 12-hour tours.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But in other cases, like it wouldn't make a dent.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So, for example, if we needed an officer to work two extra hours for

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You know, in a zone, larger headcount wouldn't get rid of it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And oftentimes it said that more headcount, more overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I don't necessarily believe that to be true because I believe if you responsibly manage overtime, you can avoid that.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: What would you say are the top three things that would have to happen in an ideal world for us to be able to make the dent on the issue of overtime that I think we all want to make?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I think we have made a dent on the issue of overtime.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I think the data that I provided earlier, calendar year 2025, overtime down 12.4%.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Fiscal year 2026, the first three quarters, overtime down.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: 10% and the fact that the fiscal year 26 overtime spend is projected to be 6% lower than the historical

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: average going back to 2016 if you put it in today's dollars and if you exclude the transit program that the governor is funding.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: When we think about overtime, yes, the numbers go up and that's because the cops rightfully get paid more.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And so that increases overtime spend.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks for current budgeted headcount for school safety agents and crossing guards. Tisch reports SSAs cut by 264 positions to 3,651, and crossing guards at 2,290 employed against 2,309 budgeted. Tisch expresses disappointment about the SSA cut but notes positions were vacant.
chapters.title: Feliz and Tisch discuss school safety agent and crossing guard headcount

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: All right, moving on to school safety agents and school crossing guards.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Can you give us the new budgeted headcount for school safety agents and crossing guards?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes, I can.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay, school safety agents were cut by 264 positions to 3,651.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Does someone have school crossing guards in front of them?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: If not, I can get it here.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Hold on, I have it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Do you have it?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: School, I don't see it here.

utterances.speaker: Christine
utterances.text: So we just recently hired a 94 in May.

utterances.speaker: Christine
utterances.text: So that leaves us with 31 vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Christine
utterances.text: The allocated budget is $2,309.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Christine, do you have the specific numbers for school safety agents?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Sorry, sorry, for school crossing guards.

utterances.speaker: Christine
utterances.text: So I have 2,309 for the allocation.

utterances.speaker: Christine
utterances.text: We just hired a class of 94 in May, so that leaves us with 31 vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Thank you, Chief.

utterances.speaker: Christine
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Can you tell us how the current...

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Head count compares to the head count for the last five years for school safety agents and school crossing guards?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Down, but we will get you those specific numbers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Do you?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I don't have it by year here.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I just have this year.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And I know we're reducing the school safety agents by 264.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Do you think what we have right now is enough?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And if not, why the cut?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I was disappointed about that cut, although I understood it because those were vacant positions.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: My hope had been to hire up to our previous authorized headcount, and we were looking at a number of different ways.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: To gin interest in taking that test and to have basically more people interested in becoming school safety agents.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the reason we weren't up to headcount is because we didn't have enough, quote, qualified candidates.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So we were looking at a number of factors, including looking at some of the physical requirements to become school safety agents, which seemed to be, to my mind, out of line with what the job

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: requires.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I was hoping to make progress increasing the number of school safety agents before this cut occurred, but I do understand that it occurred because those positions were vacant at the time.

chapters.summary: Feliz asks about ghost car enforcement and interagency roles. Chief Obey details 19 joint operations year-to-date resulting in 291 arrests, 12,000+ summonses, and 1,278 vehicles seized, working with state police, MTA, and PAPD along bridges and tunnels.
chapters.title: Feliz and Chief Obey discuss ghost car enforcement operations

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: All right, moving on to ghost cars.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So as we know, there's a lot of different agencies that are involved when it comes to enforcing rules related to ghost cars, fake paper plates, and et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And I know there's been some changes.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: The sanitation department is playing a bigger role.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Can you talk to us about the different agencies and the different processes and roles that each play?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Including the NYPD?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The sanitation department addresses vehicles that are derelict.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So they are below some certain dollar value, some dollar value.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And the sanitation department, and they're just sitting on the streets, and the sanitation department has the authority to get rid of them themselves.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The police department has a lot going on with

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: ghost cars and non-derelict vehicles and Chief Obey can talk to you about some of our enforcement there.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: Okay, so with Ghost Cart, Ghost Blade Operations, it's an interagency initiative.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: So it's not just done in a silo, just the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: We work very closely with our law enforcement partners, typically along bridges and tunnels.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: So New York State Police, MTA, the NYPD, PAPD.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: Year to date, we've had about 19 joint operations and resulted in 291 arrests.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: 12,000 plus summonses issued and 1,278 vehicles seized.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: In your district, I know that there were some concerns.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: You know, that work continues along Fordham Road and some of the allocations that you gave us.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: I don't have that specifically.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: I'll get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: But the work we do is totally separate from what sanitation does.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: We work along bridges and tunnels, and it's a multi-agency effort.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Right, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So about two years ago, a while back, had a hearing on the issue of ghost cars, and we were told that the NYPD didn't have enough space to tow or to be able to properly enforce the rules on the issue of ghost cars.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Is that still the case?

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I know you've been working on getting new towing space.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: So it's totally separate.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: I know that's what a council member always talks about, but this is totally separate.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: So as a result of the multi-agency efforts that we have with the other law enforcement agencies, the storage is not an issue for us at all because we tap into the MTA and the TBTA.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: They have ample space when it comes to storage, so we don't suffer from that problem.

utterances.speaker: Chief Obey
utterances.text: It's separate from...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: that other type of work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, good.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about NYPD drone usage. Tisch says she wants to expand drones to every precinct and believes NYPD should control its own drone program rather than DoITT. LaPetrie reports 5,000 flights this year with 2.5-minute response times, operating from five locations.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss NYPD drone program expansion

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, drones.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So as I mentioned in my opening statement, I saw a fascinating video yesterday.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Individuals in the Bronx had engaged in gun violence.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The local precinct used the drone to basically make sure that they were able to

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: to make sure they were apprehended quickly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we're thankful for those officers who were running towards two individuals who had just engaged in gun violence and also still had loaded weapons.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm so curious, and I know if you can't share this information for public safety related reasons, all fine, understood.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But can you talk to us about the use of drones?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does every precinct have one or is it some of the cars?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So the chief of department is going to answer a lot of the specifics to your question.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And our use of drones is amazing.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I would just like to say up front that I would like to expand the department's use of drones.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We do not have drones at every precinct, and I believe we should have drones at every precinct.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: As the drone management in the city is currently set up, the funding and the overall management of drone expansion is currently at the Department of Information Technology and Telecommunications.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I believe, given that the NYPD is the...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Biggest user of drones and there are such incredible opportunities to use this to that technology to drive public safety that we should be

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: in control of our own destiny in terms of expanding our drone program.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But the chief will tell you about how great it is.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So obviously I'm not going to get into specific precincts on where our drones are being launched.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So we have three areas in Brooklyn, one area in the Bronx, and one area in northern Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Each contains two docks.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Which can obviously fly two drones.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So the drone can only go out for a specific amount of time, comes back, and we put up the second drone.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: We've done approximately 5,000 flights this year alone.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: About 4,000 of those 5,000 are drones as first responders.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Our response time when we put up a drone to the actual 911 call is less than three minutes.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: It's about two and a half minutes.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: The success stories that you're talking about, I can specifically talk about the one you're talking about on May 24th, when, yes, there was a response to a shots fired, and, yes, we did grab two different individuals with pistols.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So it's highly successful.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: I agree with the police commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: We're looking to obviously explore and expand our drones.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: In addition to the 4,000 first responder flights that we've done this year, there's another 1,000 that we used for either missings, you know, beach drone activity, parades, things of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So we are up and running, and it has been a great tool for us so far.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Chair, I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I just found the...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: information you're looking for on school crossing guards.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I just want to make sure that it's in line with what the chief had previously said.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So as of May 13th, we employ 2,290 school crossing guards, including 2,211 level ones and 79 level twos.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The budgeted headcount for school crossing guards is 2,309, which breaks out to 2,219 level ones and 90 level twos.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, thank you for that data.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just curious on the issue of technology and drones.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In what cases do we use drones?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They're more effective for many reasons.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They're cheaper, which means you could purchase more.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They're faster.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you have more, you could have them in different locations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In what cases do we use drones versus helicopters?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sure every New Yorker has heard of those helicopters when there's a serious incident.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So, you know, we obviously know the price difference, right, between the fuel and things of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: I will say this.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: When we can put up a drone, that drone's going up.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: You know, there are restrictions and also the infrastructure sometimes, or I should say,

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: you know, tight locations, things of that nature, yeah, we're able to use that drone in between buildings.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: You know, our emergency service unit uses it tactically.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: for barricaded perpetrators and you know we have approximately 80 drones and I'm telling you we use it as much as we can.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $6.5M drone mitigation grant and new authority to take down drones. Tisch reports the equipment has been purchased, counterterrorism personnel trained with the FBI, and NYPD will deploy drone mitigation for the first time at the World Cup.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss drone mitigation capabilities for World Cup

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The executive plan included 6.5 million for drone-based grant.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How do you plan on utilizing this funding and also can you talk to us about the new development?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know you've been working with the federal government to have more authority to be able to take down drone switches.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes, we got $6.5 million from the federal government to use to buy drone mitigation equipment.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We have spent that money and purchased the equipment.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We also have members of our counterterrorism team trained on using that equipment and trained with the FBI specifically.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: On drone mitigation.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So for the first time at the World Cup this year and events in New York City surrounding the World Cup, the

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: NYPD will be working directly with our federal partners, with our own equipment and our own personnel on drone mitigation.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And I will say,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: That is a very important first step.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: As for doing it beyond the World Cup, beyond that special event, it is still hazy and I am not at this time certain how it will work.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We are looking forward.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: To the Department of Justice.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: putting out guidelines for state and local agencies about how they will be setting up our authority to mitigate drones generally.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the TASER 10 upgrade timeline. Tisch reports ESU has been fully trained and all other officers should complete training by end of 2027.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss TASER 10 upgrade timeline

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, so the Taser 10 upgrade.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the plan includes increased funding to move to the newer Taser 10.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's an upgraded and safer Taser.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: To my understanding, it has more range, reduced volt capacity, and much more.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: When will all officers have this new taser?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And actually, will all officers have the new taser?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So ESU has already been completely trained, which is wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And we anticipate completing the training for all other officers by the end of 2027, although those plans are preliminary at this time.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about a woman who gave birth while in NYPD custody. Staff clarify she was arrested for trespass with an open warrant, was uncuffed before giving birth, and was taken to the hospital per policy. Tisch declines to discuss specifics in open session.
chapters.title: Lee questions Tisch on woman giving birth in NYPD custody

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: A few questions about recent incidents.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So recently, I think it was the Gothamist reported a woman who was in NYPD custody, and you told me if the reporting is incorrect in any way, a woman in NYPD custody gave birth while in handcuffs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And according to the Gothamist article, she was arrested on a low-level charge.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Something that could have potentially been a desk appearance ticket.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: She was nine months pregnant and, yeah, gave birth while in custody.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you comment and provide any details on this?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That young woman, she was arrested for trespass, and we were unable to give her a disappearance ticket because she was wanted.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: She had a warrant, an open warrant, and she did not give birth in cuffs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Her cup, she was uncuffed by the time she gave birth.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, yeah, and I know there was a dispute on that on the article, but she gave birth in the courthouse.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes, hold on.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I will say, if I recall correctly, and I,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Wish that you that I knew that this was going to come up out of brought the specifics of that incident But my recollection is that she was brought to the hospital and was

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: released from the hospital and put back in our custody.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what was the open warrant for?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also, what's the policy when it comes to people or...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Individual

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Who might be pregnant or have medical needs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So, Chair, what we will do is we will follow up with you in writing about both the particulars of what occurred, and we can also provide you with any applicable policies.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We did not come prepared today to talk about the specifics of this.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The Chief of Department can speak about the policies, but what I don't want to do is talk in an open forum about a specific individual and a specific case.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So go ahead with the policy.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So the policy is to bring that individual to the hospital, which the policy was followed.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: The hospital then released that individual back to our custody.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and what was the open warrant for?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Again, I don't want to discuss a specific individual's case in an open setting like this.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So a few questions, a few final questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know earlier you mentioned there's a new precinct in the borough of Queens.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What are factors that you take into consideration when determining whether to build a new precinct?

chapters.summary: Lee asks about factors for determining new precinct construction. Tisch lists geographical size, response times, 911/311 calls, and crime conditions. Lee also asks about the top three factors driving crime reduction, with Tisch citing precision policing, technology, and officer work.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss new precinct criteria and crime reduction factors

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm only asking because I know a few precincts in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Lots of things.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: The geographical size of the precinct, response times, 911 calls, 311 calls, crime conditions.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: There is a lot that goes into it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, and last but not least, you know, we've obviously made a lot of progress on the issue of safety.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As we know, crime is down for the most part, shootings, murders, and et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What would you say are the top three things that have helped us get there?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Police presence, technology, I'm sure, also.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In your opinion, what would be three things that stand out on that?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Could have listed one more, and you would have answered the question yourself.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: You were doing great.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I give a lot of credit to the chief of department and the chief of crime controls precision policing strategies, not just the zones.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: What happens is when we see a crime problem, we look at the data and we come up with a very specific data-driven response to it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We have, for example, one response based on the data that we saw to retail theft.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Retail theft is down.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Another data-driven strategy to burglaries.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Burglaries we then saw come down.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Same thing for shooting incidents and shooting victims.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I would say largely it has been precision policing, which to my mind is data-driven strategies to address specific types of crime that we see.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Second, I agree with you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Technology has been extremely helpful in our

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: efforts.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And third, and I think most importantly, our cops are doing incredible work.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They are out there every day.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They are engaged.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: They are carrying out the strategies that we have given them with a lot of nobility and a lot of professionalism.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And without their work, we would not see any of the reductions that we are enjoying today.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about enforcement of unsafe mobility devices like e-bikes and scooters. Tisch says it's a problem, criminal summonses are still issued for reckless driving, but the department has shifted from C summonses to B summonses for lesser violations based on City Hall conversations.
chapters.title: Lee and Tisch discuss enforcement of unsafe mobility devices

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the final question is on the unsafe use of mobility devices.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As we know, mobility devices such as e-bikes, scooters, mopeds, they're good.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You could easily move around.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They reduce traffic.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They also reduce air pollution versus, for example, using cars.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But we have seen people use them in an unsafe way.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And, of course, very complex issue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It could be super difficult to enforce the rules.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You stop them, they could easily take off, and it's difficult to, you know, engage in a high vehicle pursuit.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also they could obviously move much faster than other cars and enter areas that cars can't enter.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you think the reckless use of these items are a problem?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if yes,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What's the policy on this?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So yes, I think it's a problem and I think that the data bears it out.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I want to be clear that for reckless driving of these smaller mobility devices, we still today

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: can issue criminal summonses for that, and we continue to issue criminal summonses for reckless use of e-bikes and scooters.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: But for other offenses, like for example, speeding or running a red light, things that come just short of

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: recklessness, we, or the legal definition of recklessness,

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: we have reverted from issuing C summonses to issuing B summonses based on conversations with City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for the information.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know my colleagues have a second round of questions, so I'll pause here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Caban corrects the record on the birth-in-custody incident, noting cuffs were removed only when the baby was crowning, and on the Rikers population, noting 397 people are held on misdemeanors and 7 on violations, not all felonies.
chapters.title: Caban corrects record on birth-in-custody and Rikers population

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Councilmember Cabon, followed by Wong.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: For my questions, I just want to address a couple things.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The woman who gave birth in cuffs in the courthouse in Brooklyn, and I'm going to say that because the cuffs were taken off when the baby was crowning.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And any mother will tell you that labor and birth is much more and much longer than those final few moments of when your baby crowns and is actually born.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I would suggest that whoever's giving you all that line, whoever's working on the PR spin, rethink that one because that is a terrible answer.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm emotional just like even I can't believe that that was the answer that was given.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I did want to correct the record.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I know that there was a, it was testified to that the jail population on Rikers consisted of everybody being there on felonies.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I just want to say that as of yesterday, the population was 6,652 people, 5,652 of which are pretrial, meaning that they are not proven guilty of anything and they are simply there because they cannot afford their bail.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: 397 of them are there on misdemeanors.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: 154 of them are there on technical holds.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Seven are there on violations, not crimes.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Those are not felonies.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I think that's important to have on the record.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Just if I can just add.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Well, no, that's not a question.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm stating that for the record.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to ask my question now.

chapters.summary: Caban asks what percentage of arrests in the open warrant category involve felony warrants versus other summonses. Staff explain they have a legal obligation to return people on open warrants. Caban cites CPL 150-20 stating DATs are discretionary for open warrants.
chapters.title: Caban questions NYPD on open warrant arrest practices

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I also wanted to follow up on my first round of questioning around sort of the summonses and the arrests.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It was stated that there were three categories, right?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The no ID, which was 6% of those processed arrests.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then the other two categories were if they had open warrants.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then the third category was if they were potentially going to be charged with a higher offense.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I wanted to hone in on those second and third categories.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That second one, if they had open warrants, how much of the time were those open warrants for other summonses or low-level offenses?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I don't have that data with me, but if someone is wanted on an open warrant, it is our responsibility to turn them on it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Well, okay, so I'm going to say that under the law, under CPL 150-20, actually, it says that a DAT is discretionary where there is an open warrant.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It's actually the patrol guide, not the law, that suggests it's mandatory to take people through a custodial arrest.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: In fact, under the law, it would have to be a felony open warrant.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: To make custodial appearance mandatory.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So that's why I want to know how, what the percentage of non-ID arrests include felony charges.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Council member, just one point on that, it's important.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Of course, you're right about 150-20, but here's the thing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If we have someone who we encounter, we know the person has an open warrant, and we just let that person go, we do not return them on that warrant,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That person then goes and commits another crime, hurts somebody.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: After we let the person go on an open warrant, right, we are responsible for what happened.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's a massive liability for the department and for the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We cannot do that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I will tell you that in my experience as a public defender representing people in court, oftentimes when somebody came through, when they got a summons for

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: sleeping on the subway, for an open container, their open warrant was overwhelmingly usually for another sleeping on the subway or an open container.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Not that they were.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We're some crazy threat to our safety.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I just, I think that context is important when people are trying to understand actually what it means when somebody has a warrant, actually what it means when we're spending millions of dollars of resources to police people over these issues

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: while not increasing safety but exacerbating existing problems.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I want to go into that third area of the higher offenses that may be charged.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So let's say somebody is under CPL 15020 eligible for an appearance ticket or a desk appearance ticket.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Of those higher offenses that you're using the discretion to process, how many of those are for still under the category of low-level offense?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, for example, a theft of service.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So, like, how many or the percentage of those that you're tracking in that third category are actually other low-level offenses?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We will get you that information.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can I ask a few more questions?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can we go to round three?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What's up?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I promise, Tiffany.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'll come back to you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What's up?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to come back to you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I can't hear you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to come back.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: As long as you promise to come back.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Wong, followed by Council...

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: This is going on a long time.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I'm going to excuse the executive staff and the people testifying will stay, but we have a lot going on this summer, and I just can't have them staying here all afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Go ahead.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about auxiliary police officer bullet-resistant vest funding. Ryan reports the request was included in executive budget new needs but was not funded, and they will continue making the request.
chapters.title: Wong asks about auxiliary police vest funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Wong, followed by Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Commissioner, auxiliary police officers were previously promised bullet-resistant vests after the 2007 murders of auxiliary officers Pacoero and

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Marshallick, with the city committing $3.3 million for vest purchases and recurring support for new members.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My question is, is there a recurring baseline budget item in FY27 for auxiliary police equipment?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because I looked and looked, I just don't see it.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So my question is, is there such an item that includes vests, radios, and other safety gear?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Ryan will answer that question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: So we did include the request for the equipment for the auxiliary officers as part of our executive budget new needs.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: It was not funded.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Ryan
utterances.text: We will continue to make that request of the Office of Defense.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I see.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong commends NYPD security at the Israel Day Parade and asks whether the deployment model should be used more frequently at large protests. Tisch says protest response is governed by a settlement agreement but each event is assessed individually.
chapters.title: Wong and Tisch discuss Israel Day Parade security model

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My next question is, Commissioner, I marched in the Israel Day Parade yesterday, and I want to commend the NYPD for the strong security presence along the route.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The police were visible, proactive, and engaged, and the event proceeded without any significant issues.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I saw substantial deployment that included patrol officers, the strategic response group, counterterrorism personnel, and other special...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: specialized resources work together to maintain order and ensure public safety.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Given some of the large demonstrations we have seen in recent years that have resulted in disorder, property damage, blocked roadways, or arrests,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: does the success of Sunday's deployment demonstrate the value of a stronger, preventive posture, and should the department be utilizing that model more frequently at large-scale protests and demonstrations?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Well, our response to protests and First Amendment activity is governed generally by a settlement agreement entered into a few years ago in the Adams administration, but we look at each

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: event based on the unique circumstances of it, and we design appropriate security packages accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Councilmember Joseph Thunbuer.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Joseph asks about Youth Coordination Officers (YCO), with LaPetrie reporting 177 YCOs. Joseph asks about pending investigations against YCOs and arrest/summons data, with Tisch committing to provide the data.
chapters.title: Joseph questions NYPD on Youth Coordination Officers

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I have a question around YCO.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How many youth coordinators do you have within the NYPD?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Here we go.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I got it.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Can you just answer?

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: It's approximately 200.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: No, we have the exact number.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: 177, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How many officers that are now YCO have pending investigation of abuse and use of force?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We will get you that number.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How many young people have YCO arrested or given summons to in the current and last year?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And what are the majority charges filed or summons types given to these young people?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We will have to get that specific enforcement data to you because I don't have enforcement broken out by type of officer.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: If I could just give a general statement.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Go ahead, give me general, and then I would love to see a breakdown.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Yes, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: The arrests of juveniles is at its highest level for serious crime, and we are at our lowest level for low-level crime.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Mm-hmm.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, and you'll send me a breakdown of what that looks like.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And what are we doing as...

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: NYPD and other agencies to combat young people.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Followed by Ressler.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about coordination between youth officers and DOE regarding student smoking around schools. LaPetrie details a revamped juvenile crime strategies desk and an all-day stakeholder meeting with DOE, ACS, prosecutors, and probation.
chapters.title: Brewer and LaPetrie discuss youth officer coordination with DOE

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Also on the youth officers, it's more for me coordination because the young people leave the high school.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Unfortunately, there's a lot of morning smoking, lunch smoking, after school smoking, et cetera, in the neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And the youth officers get stuck with this responsibility, although I think it's more the Department of Education that should be out there talking to the young people.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I've actually tried to get some officers, some people from DOB to go out, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question to you is, because people in the neighborhood complain, understandably, on their stoop, in their community gardens, and so on.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I guess my question is, what is the coordination between, even though we work at it at the precinct level, I think DOB should be doing more.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering at what point do you say to DOE enough?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, this is more of a policy issue than a numbers issue, but it is creating a lot of havoc unnecessarily.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm just wondering what you think should be the policy there.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You have a thought on this?

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So I'll just give you general terms again.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's pretty constant.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So we had revamped our juvenile crime strategies desk to take that, what you're talking about, and make it front and center.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: And we just had a meeting chaired by the director that oversees the juvenile crime strategies desk, who is an ex-prosecutor.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: With

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: All the stakeholders when it comes to juvenile crime, DOE, ACS.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Obviously, the prosecutors, not only on the family court side, court counsel side, but on the DA's side, on the youth part side.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Juvenile probation.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: It was an outstanding meeting.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: It was an all-day meeting.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: I actually went and spoke.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So we are really, really trying to...

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Focus on the two sides, the at-risk side, that is not what we're looking to do as far as arrests.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: We're looking to visit that family, we call them youths at risk.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: And then unfortunately on the other side, when it comes to violent crime, there has to be consequences.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: There has to be consequences.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just thought we kept up to date for how we can work on it together because it's constant.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Ressler, followed by Banks.

chapters.summary: Restler raises concerns about 911 response times, noting end-to-end times increased from 6:44 pre-pandemic to 9:10 now for critical crimes. Tisch says hiring up to headcount will help, with new officers moving to precincts starting in June. LaPetrie attributes increases to 25% higher crime volume requiring longer report times.
chapters.title: Restler questions Tisch on 911 response time deterioration

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I timed that well.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you very much to the chairs.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you again, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So the last time I raised this with you, I appreciated that unlike your predecessors, you acknowledged that response times are not where you'd like them to be and are a serious issue and you're working to improve them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we continue to see

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think in the most recent MMR, end-to-end response times were 14 minutes and 6 seconds, up from 10 minutes prior to the pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And most importantly to me, the critical crimes in progress, end-to-end, we're looking at 9 minutes and 10 seconds prior to the pandemic, 6 minutes and 44 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And the times prior to the pandemic had been very consistently in that 6-minute-plus range.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're now looking at something that's much higher.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There has been some improvement, but we still have a ways to go.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Commissioner, what's the plan to address this?

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: I think it's hiring up to headcount.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: We need to get the number of people.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: For too many years, the department had authorized headcount and then did nothing with it, meaning didn't hire people to fill those vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And those same people say that.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: It's terrible that we haven't hired 5,000 additional officers.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Separately, I think we are, I am confident that we are going to see the fruits of our hiring of these 4,000.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And additional officers come to bear starting next fiscal year when they will be in precincts responding to 911 calls for service.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Right now, all of these new rookie officers are doing their first year, 18 months of training on foot posts, but they are not in precincts responding to 911 calls for service.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: And starting in June, we are going to be moving the first tranche of them into those assignments.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: So I hope to start to see 911 response time come down as a result.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I, you know, you've expressed this to me before that you think that will make a difference.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We saw the end-to-end response times increase by 40 plus percent from pre-pandemic to now.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They're slightly down from the worst points in the Adams administration, but still not in a good place.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We didn't see a 40% modification in headcount.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We saw a single digit percentage modification in headcount.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So could you just help me understand why have things

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: change so dramatically?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Chief, you want to jump in here?

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So unfortunately, you know, the commissioner hit it when she said lowest level of shootings, murders.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: But unfortunately, crime is still up 25% from pre-pandemic, which means we're responding to a lot more violent crime than we had in the past.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Meaning those complainants, you know, the reports that the officers take, they take a lot of time.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: They take a lot, you know, it all starts with a field investigation.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: And we want good customer service.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: I want the best of the best customer service.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: That's what I demand.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: That's what the police commissioner demands.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: So a 25% increase in...

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Crime from pre-pandemic means that we are responding to a lot more serious crime, which takes more time to deal with that complaint.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I would love to dig in with this with you further to better understand those trends and what you're seeing and how that is impacting response times.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: If I could just make one real example.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Please, I have 25 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Let's take one last one.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Up 175% from pre-pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: That takes a lot of time.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: Alarm on a car.

utterances.speaker: Chief LaPetrie
utterances.text: You know, a lot of paperwork.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I understand what you're saying.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I get it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I would love to dig in with you, though, and understand where those trends are happening, what types of issues, and what we can do to mitigate them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The last thing I just want to ask, and this is a...

chapters.summary: Restler raises concerns about two officers stationed outside a retail store all day for CompStat purposes. Staff acknowledge officers should not be stationary in front of a store and should be on foot posts, and state the message from leadership is not to run commands for CompStat.
chapters.title: Restler raises concern about officers stationed at retail store for CompStat

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Pet peeve of mine.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have a, I think, global international retailer.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I won't shout them out here, but they're from Canada.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They're based in our district.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They kind of famously have policies that around no chase, no intervention policy when people steal from them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so in one of my precincts,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: three or four commanding officers in a row have made the same staffing decision where they stationed two cops outside of this business all day, every day.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And as I understand it, it's for CompStat purposes that it's to try to reduce the

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: that's coming from that business to drive down.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And it's just like for me, I would much rather have those two cops in the heart of downtown Brooklyn rather than standing outside of this business, keeping the whole neighborhood safe, rather than doing the bidding, you know, basically working for free.

utterances.speaker: Jessica Tisch
utterances.text: Well, officers should not be doing, if you're implying that they're doing like private detail.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So they should not be stationary in front of a store.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They more than tell early for, you know, an amount of time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All day, every day.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And you know the business.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know exactly.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know who I'm talking about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know exactly who you're talking about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Some people here might have, I thought it was closer.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It should be at least a block foot post where those officers walk up and down the block.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There are times that they're going to stop.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Inside a mall.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'll be square more.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Very well versed on it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With, you know, the CompStat message that you sent, I will tell you this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The message from myself, the chief of department, and all the executives is, you do not run your command for CompStat.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If you run your command for CompStat, you will no longer be a commander.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's a process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the process is at the right place, but I understand your concerns.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I love CompStat.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm not here to criticize CompStat.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I just think that when we're managing toward it, we have problems.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate your comments, and I hope you look into this issue involving this corporation that you know well across the city, because I can't imagine I'm the only one who sees it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee opens the second FY27 Executive Budget Hearing of the day for the Fire Department of New York, joined by Chair Joann Ariola of the Committee on Fire and Emergency Management, and welcomes Commissioner Bonsignore and her team.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for FDNY

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so good afternoon and welcome to the second FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for the day, focusing on the Fire Department of New York.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Linda-Lee, and I am pleased to be joined by my colleague, Councilmember and Chair of the Committee on Fire and Emergency Management, Joann-Ariola.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Today we have been joined by Councilmember Brewer, Councilmember Wong, Councilmember Palladino, Councilmember Banks, and I believe that's it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so welcome to our amazing FDNY Commissioner and you and your team, and thank you all for joining us today to answer any questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY27 to FY30 with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The FDNY's proposed FY27 budget of $2.67 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Represents 2.1% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget and the executive plan, and this is an increase of 23.8 million or 9.9%

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: from the 2.65 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions, increased fire prevention staffing funding, 14 new fire inspectors, 84 additional civilian staffing, and an increase of approximately $92 million in EMS projected revenue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 26, FDNY's headcount was 562 positions less than their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, we called on the Mayor to add $28 million to FDNY in revenue re-estimates in two areas, the foreign fire insurance tax and fire prevention liens.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In addition, we called on the administration to address the chronic pay disparity for emergency medical service workers, chronic overtime spending, and the ongoing EMS staffing crisis impairing the city's response time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We were pleased to see that our request for the revenue re-estimates were included in the plan, but the issue surrounding pay parity remains to be solved.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: EMS workers respond to the same life-threatening emergencies as their firefighting colleagues, yet continue to earn significantly less, not just compared to FDNY firefighters, but compared to EMS personnel in virtually every other major city in the country.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The result is an attrition crisis averaging nearly

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 8.3% annually, leaving the department perpetually understaffed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The council proposed $50 million in baseline funding to begin closing this gap, and the executive budget does not include this investment.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing from the commissioner on when New York City's EMS workforce can expect to be compensated in a manner that reflects the value of the work that they do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And now I want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Joann-Ariola, for her opening statement.

chapters.summary: Chair Ariola delivers opening remarks focusing on EMS pay increases, the fifth firefighter initiative, and critical infrastructure improvements, noting deferred maintenance is a moral and public safety issue. She demands clear answers and timelines from the administration.
chapters.title: Chair Ariola delivers opening remarks on FDNY budget priorities

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Good afternoon and welcome to the Fiscal Year 2027 Executive Budget Hearing for the New York City Fire Department.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I am Councilmember Joann-Ariola and I am Chair of the Committee on Fire and Emergency Management.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Today, this committee is holding a hearing for one reason, and that is to get answers.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We are here to understand whether this budget truly reflects the needs of the brave men and women of the FDNY and whether the promises that have been made to firefighters, EMS personnel, and the people of the City of New York are being fulfilled.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
