utterances.text: Among the issues we'll examine today are EMS pay increases, the long overdue implementation of the fifth firefighter, and the status of critical infrastructure improvements throughout the department.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: For years, our EMS members have been asked to do more with less.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They answer hundreds of thousands of calls annually, provide live

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: saving medical care under extraordinary circumstances and serve in an indispensable part of our emergency response system.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yet they continue to be compensated at levels that fail to reflect the value of their work.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We need answers about what steps are being taken to achieve fair pay for these workers and how this administration intends to recruit and retain the next generation of EMS professionals.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We will also once again discuss the need for the fifth firefighter on engine companies.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: which, as we all know, is not a luxury.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It is a safety issue.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Every second matters in a fire, and every firefighter deserves the staffing levels necessary to perform their duties safely and effectively.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The question before us is simple.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: What concrete steps are being taken to move this initiative forward?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And finally, we must address the condition of the department's facilities.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: In 2025, the FCMY publicly stated that approximately $940 million had been allocated for capital improvement to firehouses and related infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yet, as we review the city's financial plans, many of these projects appear to have been pushed further and further into the out years, in some cases extending through 2035.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: New Yorkers deserve

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: to know why.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Our firefighters are willingly putting their lives on the line every day.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They run into burning buildings while others are running out.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They work through natural disasters, emergencies, and tragedies without hesitation.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They never ask for anything and rarely complain about the conditions they are left with at the firehouse.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: At the very least, the city can do is ensure they return to firehouses that are safe, modern, and fit for the men and women who serve there.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: No firefighter should be forced to eat, sleep, work in facilities plagued by leaking roofs, apparatus floors ready to collapse, and deteriorating conditions because of long-delayed repairs.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Deferred maintenance is not merely a budget issue.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It is a quality of life issue, a moral issue, and ultimately a public safety issue.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Today, this committee expects clear answers, clear timelines, and clear commitments.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The FDNY is the most respected department in the world.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The fire department, EMTs, paramedics, and civilian personnel who serve this city deserve an administration that keeps their promises.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I would like to thank our committee staff for their hard work, financial analyst Tanvir Singh, assistant director Jack Story, committee counsel Josh Kingsley,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: policy analyst Will Hongash, and my amazing chief of staff Phyllis Insarillo.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I would like to welcome and thank Commissioner Bonsignor and our firefighters, EMTs, paramedics, and the department's civilian staff for the work that you do.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I look forward to a candid and productive discussion, and I thank the Commissioner and the Department for the leadership and for appearing here before us today.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Commissioner Bonsignore, First Deputy Commissioner Flynn, Chief Esposito, Chief Miano, Deputy Commissioner Kristoff, Chief Woods, Chief Corral, and Assistant Commissioners DeRacus and Nunan.
chapters.title: Committee counsel swears in FDNY witnesses

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I would now turn back to Committee Counsel for swearing in.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, just really quickly before we get started, I just wanted to remind folks that we have the executive, all of the hearings for public testimony on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 930.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So Wednesday, beginning June 10th at 930, we are going to have an all-day long festival for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So if you have folks that you know want to sign up, please make sure that they do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, I will send it over to our committee counsel for swearing in.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee, and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Commissioner Bonsignor?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: First Deputy Commissioner Flynn?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chief Esposito?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chief Miano?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Kristoff?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chief Woods.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chief Corral.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Assistant Commissioner DeCotris.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Assistant Commissioner Nunan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Bonsignore introduces her leadership team and highlights upcoming FDNY Medal Day, beginning with the story of Firefighter John Palmer from Ladder 144 who rescued a 65-year-old man from a burning building in College Point.
chapters.title: Bonsignore opens testimony highlighting FDNY Medal Day heroes

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Areola and members, and Chair Lee, and members of the Fire and Emergency Management Committee.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: My name is Lillian Bonsignor, and I'm pleased to appear before you today to discuss the executive budget for the fire department.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I am joined by First Deputy Commissioner Daniel Flynn, Chief of Department John Esposito, Chief of EMS Paul Miano, Deputy Commissioner of Management and Budget Lizette Kristoff,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Chief of Fire Operations Kevin Woods, Chief of Fire Prevention Thomas Correo, Associate Commissioner of Work Force Development Nafisa Noonan, and Assistant Commissioner for Facilities

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Paul DeRacus.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: This coming Wednesday is FDNY Medal Day, which is an event that we hold each year on the steps of City Hall to recognize the life-saving efforts of the FDNY members.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Before we get into the dollars and cents of the fiscal year 2027 budget, I'd like to briefly highlight

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: the way the fire department funding translates into a tangible, real-world services.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Among this year's medal recipients will be firefighter John Palmer from Ladder 144.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: In the early mornings of September 12, 2025, Ladder 144 recognized

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: responded second due to a reported fire in a two-story flat roof private dwelling in College Point.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: When they arrived at the fire, a woman said her father was trapped inside.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Firefighter Palmer and the inside team masked up and made their way into the second floor where they were met with high heat and zero visibility.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: At the top of the stairs, they encountered a locked apartment door, which was difficult to force, given the fire conditions and the size of the landing.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Working with a fellow member of Ladder 144, Firefighter Palmer, was able to force the door open and begin a search inside the apartment.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: He located an inward opening door and found an unconscious 65-year-old man laying on his back on the floor of what firefighter Palmer determined was a bathroom.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Working with firefighter Rafael Hernandez,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: They were able to remove the unresponsive man to the exterior landing, where he was passed off to others and received emergency medical care outside that building.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: For saving a life under extraordinarily challenging circumstances, Firefighter Palmer will be receiving the Probationary Firefighter Thomas A.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Wiley Medal.

chapters.summary: Bonsignore shares the story of EMS Deputy Chief Joseph Nocerino, who diverted from his commute to investigate a house fire in Coney Island, entered the burning building to alert occupants, and coordinated an 18-unit EMS response.
chapters.title: Bonsignore recounts EMS Chief Nocerino's rescue heroics

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: A second medal recipient this year is EMS Deputy Chief Joseph Nocerino.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Chief Nocerino was on his way

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: to work one early morning last December when he spotted smoke and diverted his route to investigate.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: In the Coney Island section of Brooklyn, he discovered a home engulfed in flames.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: After calling in the incident and requesting resources to the scene, he entered the building, braving the smoke and the flames to alert building occupants.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: After clearing the first and second floor, he closed the interior door to slow the spread of fire.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Upon arrival of fire units, Chief Nocerino provided information to the members to assist in attacking the fire and then coordinated an 18-unit EMS response.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Organizing a variety of specialized personnel and ensuring the civilians he had evacuated received appropriate care.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: For risking his own safety to rescue members of the public, he will be receiving the Chief Ulysses Grant Leadership Medal.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: In addition to Firefighter Palmer and Chief Nocerino, 50 of their firefighting and EMS colleagues will be receiving medals for their heroic acts of bravery.

chapters.summary: Bonsignore reports the executive budget provides civilian headcount restoration of 84 positions ($9M in FY27), 14 new fire inspectors, Be Heard program restoration, and capital funding for roofs, vehicles, and facility renovations.
chapters.title: Bonsignore presents executive budget additions for FDNY

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: As to the numbers themselves,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I'm pleased to report that Mayor Memdani's executive budget provides for the fire department, provides the fire department with an expansion of resources in several areas.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: It calls for significant civilian headcount restoration, providing

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: $748,000 in fiscal year 26, and approximately $9 million in fiscal year 27.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: This funding amounts to 84 individuals, and we anticipate that it will go towards bolstering several units, including facilities maintenance, information technology, and procurement.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The fire department will also receive an increase in headcount within the Bureau of Fire Prevention.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: As part of the streamlining procedures to expedite equitable development speed task force, the department will receive $1.3 million

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: in fiscal year 26 and out years to hire 11 staff members for the fire alarm plan review and inspection unit, and three staff members for the emergency planning and preparedness group.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: These additions will help the units process plan applications, speed up inspections, and help the Bureau more efficiently process

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: requests from members of the public who are looking to open a new business, renovate an existing business, or build new facilities.

chapters.summary: Bonsignore announces the executive budget reverses the previous administration's transfer of Be Heard funding, restoring nearly $2M in FY26 growing to $6.7M in FY29, with headcount expanding from 44 to 84 positions.
chapters.title: Bonsignore announces Be Heard program restoration to FDNY

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The budget also includes a restoration to the fire department and the expansion of the behavioral health emergency assistance response division, known as Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: This program is part of New York City's commitment to use a health-centered approach to individuals experiencing mental health crisis.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The budget reverses the previous

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: administration's decision to transfer funding away from the fire department and provides funding of nearly $2 million in fiscal year 26 and approximately $4.8 million in fiscal year 27,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: $6 million in fiscal year 28, and $6.7 million in fiscal year 29 and out.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The mayor's executive budget also includes numerous smaller adjustments to technical initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: This includes additional funding for motor fuel, heating fuel, utilities, and lease adjustments.

chapters.summary: Bonsignore reports capital funding including $24.3M for six roof and waterproofing projects, $6.75M for 15 emergency response vehicles, and accelerations of out-year capital for vehicle replacements, fleet maintenance, and station renovations.
chapters.title: Bonsignore details FDNY capital funding additions

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: On the capital side,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We also received a new need of $24.3 million to support six roofs and waterproofing projects, and $6.75 million

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: in fiscal year 27 to support the purchase of 15 emergency response vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: In addition to new capital funding,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We also received accelerations of out-year capital in the upcoming two years of the capital plan to enable the department to move forward on critical projects and purchases, including $16 million

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: across fiscal year 27 and fiscal year 28 to replace emergency support vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: 11.7 million in fiscal year 27 to expand fleet maintenance capacity.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: 1.7 million in fiscal year 2020.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: and 10.1 million in fiscal year 28 for multiple or multi-component renovations at EMS Station 4,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: 10.5 million in fiscal year 27 to replace the apparatus floor at Engine Company 163,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: and $6.8 million in fiscal year 27 to replace our air compressor at the Mass Services Unit.

chapters.summary: Bonsignore outlines three savings initiatives: EMS treatment-in-place revenue ($10.1M), ambulance fee increases ($24.6M), and telecom contract renegotiation ($2.2M), achieving savings without harming operations.
chapters.title: Bonsignore presents FDNY savings plan

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: When I was here for the department's preliminary budget hearing, we spoke about the department's savings plan and our determination to save taxpayer money without negatively affecting fire or EMS operations.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I am gratified that the Mayor's Executive Budget accomplishes this by creating three areas of savings that do not affect operations or negatively impact fire department members.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The first initiative increases EMS revenue by enabling the department to collect payment for treatment in place.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Under existing practice, if an ambulance responded to a medical call, provided treatment, stabilized the patient, but the patient ultimately decided to refuse transportation to the hospital,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: the department was entirely unable to bill for that care.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: However, Medicaid will soon begin reimbursing emergency medical service providers for the provision of care without transport, and the fire department will begin collecting payment

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: for treatment in place and seeing the benefits of this practice.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The second component of the department savings plan is a periodic increase in EMS ambulance fees.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Ambulance rates are adjusted every few years based on the actual cost of operating EMS.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: These rates were last adjusted about three years ago.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The current rate proposal reflects cost updates experienced since that time.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: This includes a variety of increases in costs borne by the department, including but not limited to spending on auto parts, maintenance staff,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: IT support staff, and maintenance agreements, costs associated with purchase of vehicles, and EMS labor costs.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We understand that these costs may present a challenge for some patients, and we have programs for self-pay patients who are unable to pay their bill.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: This includes a charity care program as well as a settlement program.

chapters.summary: Bonsignore closes by thanking Chair Ariola, noting she's met with many committee members in their districts, and expressing confidence in achieving gains through partnership, particularly given recent fatal fire trends in the Bronx.
chapters.title: Bonsignore closes testimony emphasizing partnership and Bronx fire concerns

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Finally, the third savings initiative identified achieved savings of approximately $800,000 in fiscal year 26 and $2.2 million in fiscal year 27.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And out through renegotiation of our telecommunication contracts.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We achieved this savings by finding efficiencies and reducing

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: As a mayoral agency, we strive to be good stewards of municipal funding, providing the best possible service to the public and not spending unnecessarily.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: With this savings plan, we believe that we've achieved the appropriate balance.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I want to thank Chair Areola for your ongoing assistance and support.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I've now met with many of the members of the Fire and Emergency Management Committee, and I've had a chance to see many of you in your own districts and learn about the different needs and priorities of the neighborhoods you represent.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Our efforts to protect New Yorkers are strengthened when we coordinate with our government partners

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: and we have the recent upward trend as we have the recent upward trends of fatal fires in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I am encouraged by the passion and enthusiasm of this committee, and I have confidence that we will achieve great gains by working together.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I am pleased that this executive budget provides funding that expands our operations, restores critical personnel who support and carry out those operations.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: This budget will enhance the fire department service of the public, both from a regulatory and preventative standpoint, as well as from a critical life-saving emergency standpoint.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We have achieved savings without harming our ability to carry out our mission of protecting life and property.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And we strive to provide the best possible service to the people of New York.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I would be happy to answer any questions you have at this time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember, did I say Banks already?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'll say it again just in case.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: De La Rosa, Councilmember Vilas, Jay Sanchez, Ressler, and Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Did I miss anyone?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, I already mentioned Councilmember Palladino.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, all right.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee asks what prompted the Be Heard transfer back to FDNY and whether there were performance or management issues. Bonsignore says the previous administration planned to transfer it to H+H, and she was able to bring it back to FDNY.
chapters.title: Lee questions Bonsignore on Be Heard transfer rationale

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to focus mostly on three areas.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: First one being Be Heard as former chair of the Mental Health Committee and as a social worker myself, this is a very, very important program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that there seemed to be some back and forth, obviously, with it being with you all and then with DOHMH and now back to FDNY.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wondering a few questions around the budget with this.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I remember when the program first came out, they had budgeted about $50 million, and then in the first year, I think it was about $22.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 22 million or so that they had actually utilized.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so wanting to get a sense of where things are at with the executive budget and be heard.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I believe they transferred

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: the funding which grew from 1.98 million in FY26 to 6.7 million in the out years with headcount expanding from 44 to 84 positions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What specifically prompted the transfer?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Was it driven by performance issues, management issues?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Was it just better put under FDNY?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Did it make more sense to have it with EMS?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how are those being addressed?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So Be Heard is a very important program.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Obviously, it's one of these programs that really take a combination of people and they do a very difficult job for a community that needs specific help.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And we were happy to get it back.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: In the previous administration, the idea was to have this unit transferred to H&H Health and Hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And when I came back, we were able to have these conversations around the best fit for it, and we were able to have it sent back to the FDNY.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And we're excited about that.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We think it's a tremendous program, and it's got huge potential.

chapters.summary: Kristoff explains Be Heard budget numbers, noting the EMS mental health differential stayed in FDNY's budget during the transfer, and the current FY26 cost is about $4M for the FDNY piece, with social workers funded through H+H separately.
chapters.title: Kristoff details Be Heard budget components

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So as far as the budget pertaining to Be Heard, I'm going to pass this over to Commissioner Kristof to talk about the numbers for you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Yeah, you had the numbers right in terms of what was added back into our budget.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: I think earlier you referenced these larger numbers that had been in the budget before.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Part of that has to do with a differential that EMS members get for taking mental health training.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And so the numbers that you just stated in exec don't include that differential because that differential stayed in our budget even when that transfer had happened to H&H.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So currently all EMS members, once they've completed the training, are eligible for the differential.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, which I've actually attended the training before, and it's very, very comprehensive.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And it's great to see how the social workers, EMS, H&H staff are all sort of working together on that.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about service gaps during the Be Heard transition. Bonsignore says there were no disruptions, and Miano confirms they rehired members who had been sent to other positions back into Be Heard.
chapters.title: Lee and Bonsignore discuss Be Heard transition continuity

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Were there any service gaps or disruptions to be heard operations during the transition period, and how are those being addressed?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: There were no disruptions throughout.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: At one point, the unit was actually starting to disband, but when we came back in, we took it back and we were building it back to where it was before.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So we continued to run Be Heard units throughout this conversation about where it was going to land.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about Be Heard staffing from 44 to 84 positions. Miano explains it's an application process with passionate volunteers, and they don't anticipate filling issues. He reports 62 FDNY EMS members currently assigned: 28 sergeants/lieutenants and 34 EMTs.
chapters.title: Lee and Miano discuss Be Heard staffing and composition

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that in the beginning, especially when the program first started up, and as you alluded to, this is a very niche sort of program that deals with very underserved communities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wanting to know how has it been in terms of staffing up and getting enough teams that are able to appropriately meet the needs of the folks that need this help on the ground?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know that it was tough in the beginning to recruit folks to take on this work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I just wanted to hear if you had any updates on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in terms of also from 44 to 84 positions, do you anticipate any issues there in terms of staffing up?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll turn this over to our chief of EMS operations, Paul Miano, who will talk you through the operational portion.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: How are you?

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: As the commissioner previously stated, we were preparing to disband Be Heard without any service gaps.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: But that did mean we did start to send some units, some members out into the field, into other positions.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Since Commissioner coming back and taking back Be Heard to the fire department, we have rehired those members back into Be Heard, and our staffing remains consistent.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: And again, Be Heard, we don't just pick people to go to Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: It's an application process.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So whenever we put out a posting for it, we get people who want to be a part of the unit.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: It's something that our members feel very passionate about, so they apply to be a part of the unit.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: And as we have those postings and we're able to have those trainings that you attended, we're going to fill all of those spots that we had originally started to put people out into the field for.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so no anticipation of having issues filling that position.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hopefully, as you mentioned, I get that it's a volunteer application process.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No anticipation and, I mean, issues?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I don't believe so.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: We have a lot of people who are members of EMS who feel very passionately about that program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And can you break down the titles?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How many are clinicians, EMS co-responders, and how many are administrative or supervisory?

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So right now we have a total of 62 fire department EMS members assigned to be heard.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: The majority of them are EMTs, and we do have sergeants and lieutenants also assigned.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So that's going to be 28.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: 28 sergeants and combination of 28 sergeants and lieutenants that we have assigned to be heard and

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: 34 EMTs that are assigned to be heard.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, 34 EMTs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what's the total cost now of Be Heard program inclusive of all the funding sources, not just the transferred city funded portion shown in the executive budget?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So for the current year, fiscal 26, it's about $4 million.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: When you add the two components.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: That's just the fire department piece.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So the social workers, for example, are actually part of the health and hospital budget.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: H&H, right.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Yes, so you don't see that part in our budget numbers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And do you know what that total budget is overall, if you can remind?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: I don't know what the H&H budget piece is.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about Be Heard ambulances in FY32 capital plan and geographic coverage. Miano says current vehicles are fairly new, and the program currently covers four boroughs (not Staten Island). Kristoff notes conversations about expansion are ongoing.
chapters.title: Lee questions Be Heard geographic coverage and vehicle timeline

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the capital plan includes $1.1 million for 20 Type 2 ambulances for Be Heard in FY32.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Why is that vehicle acquisition so far out in the plan, and are there current Be Heard units adequate in the interim?

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: The B-Herd unit has received, the ambulances in the B-Herd unit, or the vehicles that they use, are custom for B-Herd, and they are fairly new.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So without knowing the exact cycle of replacement, the ones that they are in are not.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: They're not very old ambulances or vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, which is, you know, basically why you're saying it's probably that far out in FY32, because the current vehicles are adequate?

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then just in terms of the geographic coverage, does the funding in the executive budget bring the program to a point where it can operate in all five boroughs on a 24-7 basis?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So there is additional funding in the out years, as you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The funding does grow.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: We're currently in conversations with our interagency partners about potential expansion plans going forward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and how many boroughs does it currently cover?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is it all five right now?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Four.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: It's four right now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: What's the one?

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we've got to figure that one out, too.

chapters.summary: Lee asks for 911 mental health call numbers. Miano reports FY25: 9,638 Be Heard responses, 8,201 arrivals, 1,670 treated without transport. FY26 YTD: 7,297 responses, 6,233 arrivals, 1,400 treated without transport.
chapters.title: Lee and Miano review Be Heard response numbers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what percentage of mental health-related 911 calls?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We just had the NYPD commissioner right before this, and so just one of the questions I had asked was what...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are some of the numbers in terms of 911 that's been appropriately referred to the Be Heard program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you could give us some of those numbers, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So I have numbers for fiscal year 25 and year to date for 26.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: I don't have them in percentages, though.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: I just have them as numbers.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So Be Heard responses have been in fiscal year 25.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Be Heard responses were 9,638.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Be Heard arrived on scene to 8,201 of those.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: And Be Heard treated on scene with no transport to the hospital.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So before I get into fiscal year to date of 2026, I just want to, the reason why

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Beheard was called for 9,638 but only arrived on scene for 8,201 is because as we get, there's a very strict criteria that we send Beheard units to.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: And as our dispatchers talk to the caller more, as they get more information, they may no longer fall under that criteria.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So that's why those numbers are different.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Fiscal year 26 to date.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Beheard has had responses of 7,297.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: They've arrived on scene to 6,233, and they treated with no transport to 1,400.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I don't know if this is a question that folks know the answer to, because I know that the Mayor's Office of Community Safety is still sort of being fought through.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But I know that I saw that OCMH, which technically oversees Be Heard, was also in that department.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I'm just kind of curious if you know how that's going to look in terms of coordination of services.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I think this is going to develop out.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We're very excited to work with this new community of safety, Office of Community Safety, to scale up and move things around.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: But FDNY is going to be a key component to running Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how FDNY defines EMS pay parity and what benchmark is used. Bonsignore says parity is understood as comparison to firefighters, but the details are between OLR, OMB, and unions. She advocates for EMS as a sustainable profession.
chapters.title: Lee questions Bonsignore on EMS pay parity definition

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then before I pass it on to Chair Areola,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just wanted to ask a couple questions on EMS pay parity, which we know has been an unresolved issue for many, many years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so EMS pay parity, how does FDNY currently define pay parity for EMS personnel, and what is the benchmark being used for comparison?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So right now I think what you're going to hear a lot is pay parity in comparison to our firefighters.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So that is not something that we have defined.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: This is something that has just been a matter of conversation.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Between OLR, OMB, the EMS unions, but that's how we understand it to be defined.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And do you agree with that assessment, that it should be at the same level as firefighters, or are you all internally thinking of a different benchmark?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Yeah, I think at this point, you know, my position is this, and I've been pretty...

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Verbal about supporting EMS pay parity and I do think it's one of those things that need to happen sooner rather than later and I do hope that that is something that happens but

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: what I'm looking for is a way to have EMS be

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: an actual destination, a profession, you know, someplace they can come and stay, raise their families on the job that they're doing, and enjoy the life-saving work that they do.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And my position as the commissioner really surrounds us by, you know, being able to provide EMS services to New York City for the coming decades.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: That's what we want.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We want to have a robust, sustainable EMS system.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So as far as the details go of what that's going to look like, that I would have to defer to OLR.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I don't have a seat at that table.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The conversation that I'm driving really is about the safety of our city and being able to continue our services.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how NYC EMS compensation compares to other cities. Bonsignore says the entire country is experiencing EMS recruitment and retention problems, and NYC is no longer immune, having previously been able to attract enough candidates.
chapters.title: Lee and Bonsignore discuss EMS compensation nationally

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how does the EMS compensation in New York City compare to other large municipalities across the country?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are you seeing, and where do we fall in that?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: EMS across the country is experiencing this problem, and they're experiencing a very serious recruitment and retention problem.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: It used to be that New York City could kind of, you know, get away with it.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We had a lot of people who wanted to come to EMS, so we weren't...

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We weren't experiencing it the same way as the rest of the country was, but we are no longer there.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So now New York City is also experiencing difficulty in recruiting and retaining these incredibly talented and skilled professionals who save lives for a living.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So, you know, this is a conversation that...

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The entire country.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: is having and I hope that New York is going to lead the way in this conversation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, I agree.

chapters.summary: Lee asks for the total cost of full pay parity and whether any portion is in the executive budget. Bonsignore defers to OMB and OLR, saying she doesn't have a number. Lee asks about a timeline, and Bonsignore says union negotiations with OLR should be starting.
chapters.title: Lee presses Bonsignore on EMS pay parity cost and timeline

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if you were to estimate what's the total annual cost if we were to achieve full pay parity for EMS personnel, what would be the total cost and is there any portion that is included in the executive budget?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I think this is something that has to be worked out through OMB and OLR.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I don't have a number as to what that's going to cost because it hasn't been determined what it's going to look like.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So I would defer to them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if you do have any estimates that come up, if you could let us know, that'd be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is there a formal commitment from the administration in terms of a timeline for

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: when these conversations are going to happen, take place, and hopefully get settled?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: It's my understanding that the unions and OLR are starting their process of negotiation as far as that contract goes.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So I'm expecting that those conversations will be part of those conversations that should be happening now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Or very soon, hopefully.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Chair Ariola asks about increasing EMS headcount on ambulances to three people. Bonsignore says this is the first she's heard of it. Ariola also asks about ambulance fee increases being tied to EMS pay raises, with Kristoff explaining the 16.1% civilian pattern increase is reflected in rates.
chapters.title: Ariola questions Bonsignore on EMS staffing and fee increases

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to pause there and hand it off to Chair Areola.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, but quickly, we've been also joined by Deputy Speaker Williams, Councilmember Hudson, and Purina Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So just two points, just to piggyback off your questions.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We were talking about a rise in headcount, and there's been a rise in headcount with EMS.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is there a plan to increase headcount on ambulances?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I'm sorry, is there like increasing EMS?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is there a plan to put another, yeah, another EMS person on ambulances?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They've been asking for a third person.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Oh, we have not had that discussion, not with me anyway, so I'm certainly happy to have those discussions, but this is the first I'm hearing of it.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, and also when it was brought to our attention about that there would be an increase,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: in EMS charges when an ambulance goes to a call, that a portion of that additional money that would be gained would be considered

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: when the mayor's office was discussing

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: pay raises, if not parity, for EMS workers, that that was supposed to be incorporated into what, and I think you and I had that conversation, that that would be incorporated into why the ambulance fees were being raised.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: You want to talk about the 16th?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So the ambulance rate increases do reflect an increase in EMS salaries, but based on the civilian pattern.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we've incorporated a 16.1% increase in salaries based on what they would have gotten if they were following the civilian pattern.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So then there has been some discussion regarding an increase in their salaries.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: If you're saying 16.1%.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Yeah, that's the civilian pattern that all of the civilian unions had received.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And you're saying that that conversation is being started with their unions.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Ariola questions whether EMS transport is a net revenue generator, noting FY26 revenue of $254.5M against $400.8M in costs. Kristoff explains EMS is a net cost, with revenue historically covering about a third of all-in costs, and clarifies the $92M adjustment is actually a revenue decrease replaced by city funds.
chapters.title: Ariola and Kristoff clarify EMS revenue vs. costs

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So the executive budget projects significant new EMS revenue annually, beginning in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yet FY26, actual revenue came in well below the out-year projection against a substantial total department cost.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: This gap raises fundamental questions about whether EMS transport is a net revenue generator or a net cost to the city.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And whether the financial plan's revenues projections are realistic.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The committee wants to understand the state of the ambulance fleet and whether staffing is adequate to meet that demand.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That being said, the executive budget projects $92 million in new EMS revenue annually beginning in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yet FY26 actually came in at approximately $254.5 million against a total department cost of $400.8 million.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Does the department consider EMS ambulance transport to be a revenue generating operation or a net cost to the city?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And can you walk through the full revenue cost or the few revenue cost picture?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So EMS services are definitely a net cost to the city.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: They do not generate revenue beyond their costs.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: In fact, historically, EMS revenue has covered about a third of the all-in cost of operating EMS.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So whenever we look at setting rates, we're looking at the total cost.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: That's including not just the direct payroll for the EMTs and paramedics staffing the ambulances.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It's also including the fringe cost associated with that personnel.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It's including the OTPAS cost of the medical equipment, the supplies that they use, rent on any leased EMS stations.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It includes debt service for the purchase of ambulances, for the IT systems like the CAD system that are used to support 911 operations,

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: renovations at EMS stations, and it includes a proportional amount of support staff at the fire department that support EMS services.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So like the mechanics that help repair the ambulances, the IT personnel that work on those 911 systems, the HR people that help hire the EMTs.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Now, there are sort of three different adjustments to EMS revenue being made in the executive budget, one of which is the $92 million that's been referenced a few times.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: That $92 million is actually not an increase in EMS revenue.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It is a decrease in EMS revenue and an increase in city funds.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So that was related to a Medicaid supplemental payment program that the city had incorporated into our EMS revenue targets.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: You know, many of our transports are for Medicaid.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Medicaid patients, Medicaid pays according to their own fee schedule, which is much, much lower than what our actual costs are.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: There is a program that several other states have been able to get in place, referred to as certified public expenditures, where you can essentially show Medicaid

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: that your costs are much greater than their rates, and they will make a supplemental payment.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: That has to go through a state Medicaid plan amendment process, and we actually have two spas in coordination with New York State that have been pending for over five years now

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: in back and forth with the federal centers for Medicaid and Medicare services.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: That hasn't been approved yet.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And you've seen this in prior years where that revenue has been taken down one year at a time.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: This plan includes a significant adjustment to take down most of that revenue in the baseline.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The plans are still pending, so it's possible it may get approved, probably at a much lower amount than we thought initially.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: But that's why you see that adjustment.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And that's being replaced one for one with city tax levy dollars, so there's no negative impact to EMS operations from that program not going through.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The other two adjustments are our savings initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we have treatment in place, which is $10.1 million up, mostly because of Medicaid revenue.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Now that one, in contrast to the CPE that I was just talking about, that SPA, the state Medicaid plan amendment, has already

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: been approved by the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we feel much more confident with that.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: That was approved fairly recently, so back in February, and that is mostly what is supporting that 10.1.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Then we have another 24.6 million, and that is tied to the increase in EMS rates.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: But when you look at all of those adjustments and you compare what the EMS revenue targets were as of

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: the preliminary budget to now overall the EMS revenue target is going down because of that large $92 million adjustment.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Right, okay, so if the $92 million number is not correct, right, or not exactly correct.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It's the other way around.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Could be.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So it's not revenue going up, it's revenue going down.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: City dollars going up.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so then what is the department's total EMS revenue for FY27, and how does that compare to the total cost of running EMS?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: What are the actual numbers?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So the FY27 EMS revenue target is $343.7 million.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And that is still substantially short of the all-in cost of operating EMS, which is in excess of $900 million.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And so you're also saying, all right, so the executive budget projects $400.5 million in total EMS revenue in the out years.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So that number, $400.5, was what it was as of the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: As of the executive budget, it is now $343.7 million.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So it went down.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And so the FY26 came, that was actually $254.5 million.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So what accounts for the difference?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: What do you think is the major reason for the difference and why it decreased?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is it because the $92 million is not incremental and it was actually a negative?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So the 92 million was taken down.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So that helps explain part of the difference between the 400 and the 343.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And then the two things going up are the 10.1 for treatment in place and the 24.6 tied to increasing ambulance rates.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so we're still not working with real numbers and we're still not, we're working on what ifs and how are we supposed to really put a budget plan together when there's not any tangible numbers so far.

chapters.summary: Ariola asks about ambulance fleet size and out-of-service rates. Kristoff reports about 600 ambulances with 30% out of service at any time, average age two years, with replacements targeted at 5-6 years. Ariola asks if more ambulances would increase revenue; Kristoff says call volume would limit the impact.
chapters.title: Ariola and Kristoff discuss ambulance fleet capacity and revenue

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm gonna switch to fleet capacity.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: What is the current size of the FDNY ambulance fleet and how many units are out of service at any given time due to maintenance or age and what is the average?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Age of the fleet.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So there are about 600 ambulances in the field.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The number is a little bit higher than that if you include specialty ambulances and ambulances used in training.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The average out of service is about 30%, but our number of ambulances reflects the fact that we need that level of spares available.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The average age is about two years.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we aim to replace our ambulances when they're five to six years old.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And so they're fairly young.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And is that done regularly?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Yes, there was a delay around the pandemic because of manufacturing delays, but we have since caught up.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, and if the FDMY increased the number of active ambulances, would transport revenue increase proportionally, or are there just other constraints such as staffing or coal volume that would limit that impact?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So call volume would definitely limit the impact.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we think that the demand side of that equation is fairly constant.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The number of people calling 911 for a medical emergency isn't likely to change if we have more or fewer ambulances.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: How we estimate the revenue impact of adding a tour depends a little bit on the situation.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So in instances where we're replacing a tour that was operated by a voluntary hospital, which has certainly happened many times over the last few years.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: We can estimate the full revenue impact of that because basically supply is staying the same.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: We're just replacing who is operating that tour.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: If we were adding tours to the system to improve response time, say, the revenue impact would be less.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: There would still be one, but it would sort of redistribute where those patients are going.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: We're only two-thirds of the system.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The other one-third is the voluntary hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So if we were adding additional tours to increase the total number, we would anticipate that we would proportionally pick up more tours.

chapters.summary: Ariola asks about EMS headcount. Kristoff reports budgeted headcount of 4,540 with actual at 4,250, a shortfall of 290, with another 500+ expected to leave for firefighter promotions. Ariola asks about federal/state rate changes; Kristoff confirms treatment-in-place was approved in February and ambulance rate increases go through city rulemaking.
chapters.title: Ariola and Kristoff discuss EMS staffing shortfall and rate changes

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Do you believe that you have enough EMS headcount on staff available now for the units?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And are they fully operational at capacity?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And if not, what is the shortfall?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we're definitely not fully staffed.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we're under our budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Right now for EMS, our budgeted headcount is 4,540.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And we are at 4,250.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we're 290 under budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And we expect to lose another 500 plus EMS personnel over the next four firefighter classes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right, the executive budget projects savings beginning in FY27 through higher federal and state ambulance reimbursement rates, correct?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: What specific rate changes are driving this projection and how have those rates been formally adopted by the relevant state and federal agencies or are they still pending as well?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So there are the two.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So treatment in place, which supports 10.1 million of the revenue increase, has been approved.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So New York State had a Medicaid state plan amendment that they had submitted to federal CMS, and that was approved back in February.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: New York State has only recently distributed guidance on how to proceed with the technical details of billing for that.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: But that is all set, and we anticipate that proceeding shortly.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The second one is raising our EMS rates.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So EMS rates are something that we can set on our own that doesn't require state or federal approval.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It goes through the city's rulemaking process.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And so we have gone through that process.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: You know, we had our initial public notice, we had our public hearing, and the final rule was published in the city record just last week, which is well on time for the rates to be effective July 1st.

chapters.summary: Ariola asks about unpaid transports and billing improvement. Kristoff reports 18% of transports are self-pay, with charity care and settlement programs available. She explains documentation by EMS crews is the most critical factor for billing success, and Medicaid/Medicare rates remain well below costs.
chapters.title: Ariola and Kristoff discuss unpaid transports and billing improvements

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so a lot of it relies on a lot of like unpaid transports, billing, and collections.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Let's talk about that.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Approximately 20% of FDNY transport ports go unpaid.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Would that be correct?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: There are about 18% of our transports that are self-paid, folks who are uninsured.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And some of those folks do pay, but generally at much lower amounts than our bill rates.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And there would be roughly 10% where FDNY cannot submit a bill at all due to incomplete inpatient information, such as unknown addresses, John and Jane Doe cases, and returned mail.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: What steps is the department taking to improve billing documentation and reduce the unbillable share?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So there is regular training that is provided to EMS staff on documentation.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So names and addresses are very important to our follow-up options.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: We use an outside billing vendor additionally that can use that patient information to do automatic sweeps against insurance information.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And we share information with hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we have agreements with H&H and with Greater New York Hospital Association that allow for sharing of patient insurance information to facilitate our reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so the Medicaid fee-for-service transports that are not billed directly but are reimbursed through the health and hospital subsidy,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: how is that reimbursement calculated, and does it cover the full cost of those transports, or does it leave a structural gap that the city absorbs?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So it doesn't fully cover the cost.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: That calculation has historically been based on the health and hospital.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: hospitals inpatient Medicaid discharge rate.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It sort of harkens back to when we were a part of health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And so it was part of just their general Medicaid reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: What we were hoping would replace that is CPE, the certified public expenditure process I was referring to.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: If that Medicaid state plan amendment gets approved and we can move forward with CPE, it would replace that.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So instead of getting that additional payment through H&H, we would bill directly for Medicaid fee-for-service patients, and then we would get a supplemental payment based on our CPE analysis.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: That would come a lot closer to our costs than the current model.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So then if you take into account the rate increase, the unbillable share of transports, Medicaid reimbursement shortfalls, and the cost of EMS operations, do you think that EMS ambulance transport will be a revenue positive

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: with this new additional cost and fee in place?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So I think ultimately ambulance services are still going to be a net cost to the city.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Even if we were getting reimbursement on every single transport, fundamentally the vast majority of our transports are for Medicaid and Medicare patients, and Medicaid and Medicare pay according to their own fee schedule, not our rates,

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: and it is generally much lower.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right, and this is my last question.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Before I'll kick it to my members of the panel, is there any

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: plan to invest in improved building infrastructure, additional documentation staff, or outreach to uninsured patients?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And what would the return on that investment look like in terms of increased collections?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And has the department modeled that scenario?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So I think the most important piece of this is the documentation that's done by the EMS crew.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It's very difficult for anyone, you know, after that initial incident to replicate that sort of critical basic patient information gathering that needs to happen at that point.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: If we have identifying information for the patient and an address, we can follow up.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So, you know, we have insurance database information we can check.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: We have those information sharing agreements that I referenced with hospitals that allow us to follow up.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And if we don't get insurance information, we can send a bill to the patient.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The most common call back after receiving a bill is patients calling in to give us their insurance information.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So really it's that first step that is the most critical, which we continue to stress to ambulance crews, that documentation is really important.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: The other thing that would really have an impact is changes to Medicaid and Medicare rates that would come closer to covering our costs.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Williams asks about FDNY's racial equity plan, specifically the Deloitte study on recruitment strategies. Noonan details the study reviewing the recent firefighter campaign, attrition mitigation programs, and PRB processes, with approximately $7M in the FY26 recruitment budget.
chapters.title: Williams questions FDNY on racial equity plan and recruitment

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So we've discussed Be Heard and EMS, and I'm sure my colleagues have.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They have questions of their own.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So we're going to begin with Deputy Speaker Williams, then Councilmember Brewer, and Councilmember Banks.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I've been asking questions of all city agencies regarding the racial equity plans that you guys submitted.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So it says that the department is using internal data in a Deloitte study to identify recruitment strategies for EMT and firefighter candidates from underrepresented backgrounds.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: What funding is included in the FY27 exec budget to support that work?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Sorry, can you repeat the last part of that question?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yeah, I just want to know if any funding for this fiscal year is going towards identifying recruitment strategies for EMT and firefighter candidates.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: You all reported that you're using internal data in a Deloitte study.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Sure, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Definitely for the Deloitte study, there will be a review of our most recent firefighter campaign, which we will begin hiring for in October.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: So there's an after action analysis happening with that.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: They will review some of our attrition mitigation programs that we

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: run throughout the onboarding for firefighter candidates.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: They'll be looking at our PRB, personal review board.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: So there's quite a bit of analysis that will be going, that will be included in the Deloitte research.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, and is that reflected in the FY27 budget, like your recruitment efforts?

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Our recruitment?

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So the Deloitte study that we're talking about is reflected in our FY26 budget.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And so that was part of an OTPS adjustment that we got as part of the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: I'm confirming the exact dollar amount.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So there's no specific dollar amount.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: amount for this fiscal year for recruitment?

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
