utterances.text: There is absolutely what we have our budget for.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And I guess why you find that answer.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about disparities in hiring, promotion, and retention. Noonan explains they focused on reachable test takers for the firefighter exam, with preliminary findings showing outcomes exceeding blueprint goals. Williams also asks about the home division rule and gender-related facility needs.
chapters.title: Williams asks about hiring disparities and gender facility needs

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Another question I have is, does FDNY, will you assess factors contributing to hiring, promotion, and retention of EMTs and firefighters across applicant demographics?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: By the end of 2026, that was something that you stated that you would do.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So just wanted to know if you had any information on what disparities the department has already identified and what corrective actions are under consideration.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: In terms of corrective actions, we did quite a bit of an analysis for the 23-24 campaign.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: And we came up with a pretty sound blueprint that we used to work through the most recent campaign.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: UBS campaign for the 23-24 campaign, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: And a lot of what we found was that we needed to focus a bit more and not on just recruiting more people in general.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: We wanted to focus more on our reachable test takers, specifically for our firefighter titles.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: It's a very competitive title.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: And just recruiting blindly and not focusing on where we're finding people that are actually able to pass the exam at a score that's reachable was essential to us.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: And so what we did find in some of our preliminary findings is that

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: the outcome of the exam seems to be a bit more favorable than the goals that we set for the blueprint.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: So we're pretty happy about that.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: So our focus now is just making sure that we are providing attrition mitigation programs to make sure that the targeted audiences that we focused on reaching are able to pass the physical exam, make it through the hiring process, you know, pair with mentors and all the

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: programs that we offer to make sure that they make it through the process successfully.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Also in your plan, you stated that the department will continue assigning probationary firefighters who reside in New York City to divisions where they live whenever operationally feasible.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: What is the rationale behind this initiative and how does FDNY believe it improves community trust or service delivery?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So that is what we consider the home division rule, where probies that live in the city are given the option to be assigned to a fire company in the division in which they reside.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And that was part of our settlement with the court more than 10 years ago.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: There are times when due to staffing levels that it's not operationally feasible, and then they would be placed in a division, one of the divisions closer, which does not happen commonly.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Most of the time there, if they request the home division, they are granted the home division.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, just quickly two more quick questions.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just I want to talk about gender-related facility needs.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So how frequently are borough commanders reviewing gender-related facility needs, and is there a centralized tracking system for unresolved issues?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I know in some previous hearings that was a big complaint.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Just making sure you have facilities for women.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: I'm sorry, what was the second part of that?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: How frequently are borough commanders reviewing gender-related facility needs, and is there a centralized tracking system for unresolved issues?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So twice a year, the borough commanders have the mixed gender meetings where all the female firefighters are invited to come in and sit down with the borough commander.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And bring up any issues.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And for any facility,

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: These related problems, there is a resource center, the central reporting system, to track them.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So, yes, they are tracked.

chapters.summary: Williams asks whether FDNY has identified racial disparities in EMS response times by neighborhood. Miano says they monitor call volume across the city and redeploy units to high-demand areas, but doesn't directly answer whether disparities have been identified.
chapters.title: Williams questions FDNY on EMS response time disparities by neighborhood

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, last question.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Long-term racial equity goal included analyzing EMS response times alongside neighborhood demographics to identify racial disparities in emergency response.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Has the department already identified disparities in medical emergency response times across neighborhoods?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So you all said that you were going to do that, so I'm just following up on what you guys said.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: I would say that we consistently and routinely monitor call volume in all areas of the city.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: And as call volume is increasing in certain areas, in those areas, we redeploy units into those areas to be able to address the call volume that we see.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: That could include running extra units in that area.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: It could also, as we look at the city as a whole, it could include just taking, in that moment, units from other parts of the city which are not as busy and relocating them for a period of time into that area until we're able to get a handle

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: on the amount of calls that are happening in that specific area.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, just to clarify though, the question was like, have you identified any disparities based off of geographical location, but we can continue later.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Oh, did you want to?

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Just to answer, I'll give you a little bit of more clarity on the budget you asked for, the equipment budget.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: For fiscal year 26, our budget was about $7,000, and I think we are on par.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Sorry, $7 million.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Sorry, $7 million.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: We spent $14 million the previous campaign and $12 million the fiscal year before that.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: And a lot of that is tied to the campaign that we're in.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: So for the fire campaign, we do ramp up quite a bit more.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: There's a lot of overtime spend.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: And there's also a lot of money that we spend on advertising.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: So it does fluctuate, but $7 million for fiscal year 26.

utterances.speaker: Nafisa Noonan
utterances.text: And we anticipate around that same amount for fiscal year 27.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Brewer asks about fire prevention funding increases. Chief Esposito reports significant fire increases in the Bronx and Queens, with electrical systems as the predominant cause, and details education outreach including smoke detector distribution and door-closing messaging.
chapters.title: Brewer and Esposito discuss fire prevention and Bronx fire trends

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Banks.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and thanks, Jason, for all his.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He's always 24-7, seven days a week, even when he's on vacation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I just want to say thank you to Jason.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Fire prevention has always been something that I can see in the executive budget you got more of.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In the past, it's always been underfunded.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I just want to know, with more funding, more staffing, what do you expect to do?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, the Bronx is of concern, but in general, a concern.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't quite understand why there are so many more fires.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Maybe you do.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know that I was instrumental in the beginning of the lithium-ion batteries, trying to work with your previous commissioners to get the stores to stop selling them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Of course, Amazon continued to sell them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I don't know if they're lithium ion or something else, but how are you going to approach this prevention?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You have great officers out at every street fair, et cetera, but it doesn't seem to be sinking in.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So we do know that there has been a significant increase in serious fires in the Bronx this year.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But elsewhere too, I think, not just the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: In the Bronx and Queens, we've seen a significant increase over last year.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And we're looking at and reviewing all of the fires.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Especially not just the serious ones, but the fatal fires, and trying to find what is in common with them.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Aside from that the majority of them are in buildings with a single stairway, we haven't found a common denominator yet.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: The causes of the fires are separate, although the predominant cause of fires in the Bronx and throughout the city is related to the electrical system, either in the building

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: or extension cords or power strips or an appliance cord.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We continue with the education outreach, the messaging we're getting out there about closing the door.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We know that was very effective with the lithium ion battery issues, and we thank you for your support and championing that effort.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And we saw great results with that.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So we're going to keep pushing that and getting the word out.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: When there is a fatal fire, we do send education teams to that neighborhood, to that building, to get the word out.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We continue with the education.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: The messaging we're getting out there about closing the door.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Smoke detector, distribution.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Anybody that needs it, we will go out and install it.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We know that was very effective with the lithium ion battery issues, and we thank you for your support and championing that effort.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just think that seems to be the number one concern.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about hydrant blocking enforcement. Esposito says they work with NYPD for enforcement and will issue criminal court summonses and tow vehicles that impede fire operations, resulting in thousands of dollars in fines.
chapters.title: Brewer and Esposito discuss hydrant blocking enforcement

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Hydrants being blocked.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that is improving?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And we saw great results with that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I noticed on my blocks, I can't believe that people actually leave their car unattended at a hydrant.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So we're going to keep pushing that and getting the word out.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: When there is a fatal fire, we do send education teams to that neighborhood, to that building, to get the word out.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We continue to see that.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We work with the police department for enforcement.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We continue with the messaging.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: When it does impede our operations at a serious fire, we'll have our fire prevention folks issue a criminal court summons and have the car towed, which results in thousands of dollars of fine.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: The messaging.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: But I agree, it's an issue that we're seeing across the city.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: That we did not historically see.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, so I guess, I mean, I'm just saying that should be a major, not just by you, but by the mayor, an education campaign, like, you know, do social media on that as you do on other topics, because I see it all the time.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't think people understand that it's illegal or they're just not paying any attention.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about ambulance billing for uninsured patients and response times. Kristoff explains the charity care program, and Bonsignore addresses response times, attributing increases to higher demand, staffing challenges, and hospital closures requiring longer transport distances.
chapters.title: Brewer and Bonsignore discuss ambulance billing and EMS response times

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The other thing I had a question about was just, again, I hear what you're saying about the ambulance transportation fee.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So what you're saying is if somebody doesn't have insurance and they're self-pay, but they can't pay, then they can get...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: support.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that what you're trying to say?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do people do that?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: People do.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So there's a phone number on every ambulance bill for our billing vendors call center and they can call

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: and talk through the options there.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: But there is a charity care program that's based on the same household size and income guidelines that health and hospitals uses.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then response time for ambulances.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What is it generally, up, down, in between?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How is it going?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I appreciate your support for parity strongly.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And we appreciate your support as well.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And just to your training comment, I've met with many of you.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We've talked about strategies on how to get messaging out.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Because the real success is not having a fire to begin with.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The real success is not going into cardiac arrest from the beginning.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So when we prevent those things, that's where the real life-saving work is.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So I appreciate all of you spending time with me talking about different ways to get your constituency on message as to what they can do to keep themselves safe.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Response times, as we've seen, they can fluctuate, but we have seen that response times are going up.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And this is quite honestly why I'm coming out so loudly for EMS parity.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: When we have an increased year after year demand for 911 services and we have staffing that is going in the other direction due to recruitment and retention

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: challenges, that equation will lead to increased response times.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Now, there are other things that cause...

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: increased response times too.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: That's not the only thing that we can point to.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: There are things like, I know when I started, there were 20 additional hospitals within the five boroughs than there are right now.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So as hospitals close, our EMTs, our paramedics need to go further.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: They need to wait longer in the emergency rooms that need to absorb that additional call volume.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: You know, there are lots of different things that go into this problem,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: things that we are taking very seriously, and any possible solutions that we can come up with to reduce response times, including, as Chief Miano was talking about a minute ago,

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: you know, moving units into areas of higher demand.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And we watch the demand across the five boroughs all day, every day.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So we see where there are areas that are really getting inundated with calls or response times are higher.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And we're able to shift some, but that doesn't mean that response times automatically go down all together, right?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So it may go down in that area and we'll have to shift some resources around.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: But it is something that we watch very closely because there are so many different factors that go into it.

chapters.summary: Brewer raises concerns about siren noise, particularly rumblers versus loud sirens, noting she lives between two hospitals. Bonsignore expresses willingness to discuss siren options, acknowledging it's a quality of life issue for residents near hospitals.
chapters.title: Brewer raises siren noise concerns with Bonsignore

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Maybe second round I want to ask about rumbler sirens versus

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Loud sirens.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So do you have a preference?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: There are some people that really like the rumblers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yes, I like the rumblers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I live in between St.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Luke's and Roosevelt.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And it's all day and all night.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I have complaints all day and all night.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering, when I talk to the union, they like the loud sound.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I think we should think about sirens.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, I feel like bringing them all to City Hall Park and just blasting them all day long.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because then somebody might do something about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have bills to do it, but I could use some help in thinking about sirens.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because, you know, we've got a quality of life issue that is, if you don't live near a hospital, you don't hear it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've got two hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Right next, in the middle.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I would love siren discussion.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We would love to have siren discussion.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Banks, and then we're going to go back to Chair.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Banks asks about fire and EMS coverage gaps in the southern portion of his district. Bonsignore explains EMS stations are fewer but ambulances are deployed citywide, while firehouses are more evenly distributed with over 200 locations.
chapters.title: Banks questions Bonsignore on fire and EMS coverage gaps

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, to the Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you for coming today.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: One of the concerns I frequently hear from residents in my district, particularly those living in the southern portion of the district, is the perception that the fire and EMS resources are located too far away from where they live.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I want to know, how does the FDNY evaluate potential coverage gaps and determine whether a neighborhood is being adequately served?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And what role do community feedback, call volume, response times, and pollution growth play in that analysis?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: There are definitely some areas in the city that have stations that are further away.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: EMS stations we're talking about, right?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: But with the call volume that we're experiencing, those stations aren't really having a significant impact on where they are because our ambulances are out serving the public 24 hours, seven days a week.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So what generally happens is when an ambulance has a run and they take that person to the hospital, they become available.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So they automatically now become available for the area that they're in as the closest resource.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And what about fire trucks or firehouses?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So firehouses are more distributed across the city.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: There are far more firehouses than there are EMS stations.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: EMS stations, I think we have about 40 EMS stations and over 200 firehouses.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So there tend to be a more equal distribution of firehouses than there are EMS.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Particularly...

chapters.summary: Banks asks about NYCHA elevator outages affecting emergency response. Esposito details an elevator task force and pilot program with NYCHA's elevator team, but acknowledges outages can create barriers and delays for emergency responses in NYCHA buildings.
chapters.title: Banks and Esposito discuss NYCHA elevator outages and emergency response

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Let me go to some other questions.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: When it comes to NYCHA emergency response challenges, there was a recent study done by the elevator advocates,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: and this was a relegated, particularly dealing with the 42nd Council District, which stated that there were close to

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: five buildings in my district which had chronic elevator outages.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So I want to know, particularly with these elevator outages that have taken place, has that been a barrier or has that played in the response time, particularly when it comes to NYCHA developments?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So the fire department, we have an elevator task force, and we work closely with NYCHA and the NYCHA elevator team.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So we have a pilot program that we've expanded that when the fire department receives a call for a defective elevator or somebody stuck in an elevator,

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: At the same time, we will also notify the NYCHA elevator team, and at times we can have an elevator mechanic meet us at the scene and resolve that problem sooner.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What about just general response times to someone who may be calling 911 for some type of help?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What is the, has that been a barrier?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Has there been situations where there's been an elevator outage that has blocked or prevented access to that particular call?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: I see you're asking about emergency operations in a building when there's an elevator on the service.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And that's entirely something that can happen and does on occasion.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: I don't have the specific stats on that.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So that goes to my broader point on response time, particularly.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: when it comes to NITRA residents, when it's been reported in my district that we have five buildings that have chronic

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: elevator outages and I just want to hone in on what's being done as far as any is there any coordination with NYCHA and with some of these private developers that are now managing these developments.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So specifically with NYCHA we do have coordination about the elevators we report when we operate there and have an elevator out of service we do report that to NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And it becomes their responsibility to repair it.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: So has the FDNY observed situations where elevator outages have created barriers, delays of responding to emergencies within NYCHA, to be more specific?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Yes, that happens occasionally, but I don't have the stats for how many.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And if so, what coordination?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Exists between the FDNY and NYCHA to address these challenges to ensure that residents can receive timely emergency assistance, particularly seniors or individuals with disabilities and residents living on the upper floors.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We would notify NYCHA when we discover an out-of-service elevator.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And when it's not per se NYCHA, what's the coordination when it comes to a RADPAC converted development?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We would deal with the managing agent then.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Has the FDNY identified any developments that consistently present operational challenges?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: due to chronic elevator outages?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And if so, are there any proactive measures being taken to mitigate those risks before the emergency occurs?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: In those new complexes or with the new managing agents, we have not identified that at this time.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Chair Ariola.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Ariola asks about fifth firefighter data on fire suppression outcomes. Esposito says they don't have specific data differentiating four versus five firefighter engine operations, but acknowledges five firefighters enable faster hose line stretches and quicker extinguishment.
chapters.title: Ariola questions Esposito on fifth firefighter impact data

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I'd like to discuss the fifth firefighter and the deployment of the fifth firefighter on engine companies has long been a priority for labor and a point of ongoing discussion between the council and the administration.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The operational case is straightforward.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: More personnel on scene means faster knockdown times, fewer injuries, and better outcomes for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So what does the data show about the fifth firefighters'effects on fire suppression outcomes, including time to knock down a fire, firefighter injuries, and civilian casualties?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We do not have specific data that shows the difference of operations with four firefighter engines and five firefighter engines.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: When we break down our fire reports that talk about civilian injuries and firefighter injuries,

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We don't have the ability to differentiate between the two.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: While we certainly welcome additional resources, the ability to stretch and operate hose lines is increased and better and more effective with five firefighters.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Than with four.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so would it be safe to say that if a fire happens and the first due arrives and they have only four members on that rig and that fire is out of control, the

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They have to wait for the next second due to come, which would have the fifth firefighter on it, before they get water on a fire.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is that correct to say?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: The fifth firefighter speeds up the stretching of the hose lines and putting water on the fire.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Water doesn't get on that fire until that second rig arrives.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is that true?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Not necessarily, but it's...

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Now, now, is it true?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Not, not, if that first engine stretches the hose...

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: line and they're ready to operate, they're going to operate.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: It happens faster with the fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So if it happens faster, what does that mean?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Does that mean that the fire doesn't spread more quickly?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We would theoretically put the fire out quicker, yes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It would mean that lives would be in less danger.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Yes, we'd put the fire out quicker.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right, good.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So I think that's a pretty good analysis.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So have you conducted any internal analysis or pilot of the fifth firefighter deployment?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And if so, what were the results internally?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Have you discussed?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We don't have any formal studies done with it, but anecdotally it would lead to quicker hose line stretches and quicker extinguishment of the fire.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And what is the current average response time by borough, and does the data show meaningful disparities between boroughs that a targeted fifth firefighter deployment could address?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So we do have the average response time to structural fires so far this year.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: For Manhattan, 4 minutes, 39 seconds.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Bronx, 4 minutes, 58 seconds.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Brooklyn, 4 minutes, 10 seconds.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Queens, 5 minutes, 2 seconds.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And Staten Island, 5 minutes, 5 seconds.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And what was the second part of that question?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It was, was the current average response time per borough and does the data show meaningful disparity between boroughs that a targeted fifth firefighter deployment could address?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: That's one of our points of discussion about the fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We don't know.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We don't know what impact it would have on response times because it's putting another firefighter on a seat of an engine that's already responding.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: But it does put water on a fire sooner if the first responding rig has a fifth firefighter, correct?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Correct, but that apparatus would theoretically arrive at the same time.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So the more apparatuses that have a fifth firefighter on it, the quicker water gets on a fire and the ability to cause that fire to not spread reduces and lives can be saved.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: I would agree with that.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Geographical need, is there a targeted deployment?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I think you just gave us kind of what the percentages were per borough.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: The discussion about the fifth firefighter is in the Bronx, is in engine companies in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, and can you provide a breakdown of fire incidences by borough and by engine company over the past three fiscal years?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Yes, I have for all engine companies throughout the city, we have the breakdown for fiscal years 23, 24, and 25

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: of the number of structural and non-structural fires they've responded to.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And, you know, as you can imagine, it's not evenly distributed.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We have some very, very busy companies, and we have some not-so-busy companies.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So right now, the fifth firefighter is in what areas?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So we have, currently we have 20 engine companies that have five firefighters, and that is distributed throughout the city

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: in areas that have the types of buildings that generally require longer hose line stretches.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so what are the specific concentrated areas right now?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Well, they're not with the fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: They're spread throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Every borough has some engines with five firefighters.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So do you think that the fifth firefighter should be concentrated?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Do you think it works better?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Does that model work better than having them sporadically placed unevenly throughout the boroughs?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: But they're placed in areas where their building stock requires those longer hose line stretches.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Ariola asks about response times by borough. Esposito reports: Manhattan 4:39, Bronx 4:58, Brooklyn 4:10, Queens 5:02, Staten Island 5:05. Ariola presses on whether the fifth firefighter should be concentrated in high-need areas like the Bronx.
chapters.title: Ariola and Esposito discuss response times and fifth firefighter targeting

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So let's just talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The Bronx consistently has among the highest rates of structural fires and fire-related fatalities in the city.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Does the FDNY's internal data support a targeted fifth firefighter deployment in high incident boroughs like the Bronx and secondly Queens as you mentioned?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We would certainly welcome any additional resources and it could have an impact on putting out those fires quicker.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: But, you know, the fires that we're seeing in the Bronx, putting them out quicker is, or the fifth firefighter is only not necessarily a silver bullet to stop, to solve all of those problems, right?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Stopping and preventing the fire in the first place, sharing the message about closing the door so that the fire doesn't spread before we get there.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: It's a comprehensive approach to deal with this fire problem.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And isn't the fifth firefighter very, very important, especially in vertical fires and getting the hose up and buildings and more and more into the outer boroughs, not just Manhattan or in the Bronx, but in the outer boroughs.

chapters.summary: Ariola asks about fire incidence by borough and current fifth firefighter locations. Esposito reports 20 engine companies currently have five firefighters, distributed citywide based on building stock requiring longer hose stretches, with four in the Bronx.
chapters.title: Ariola and Esposito discuss fifth firefighter distribution and Bronx fires

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: boroughs we're having more and more vertical buildings that are being built doesn't that help in putting fire

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: the taller the building the longer the hose line stretch the more impactful that the more we build and the more we have you know we have

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: larger buildings in areas that didn't necessarily have them the more we need the fifth firefighter and that's why this council is fighting for that are there

chapters.summary: Ariola asks whether FDNY would support a Bronx pilot with fifth firefighters in 20 busiest firehouses. Esposito says they would welcome additional resources. Ariola asks about coverage gaps when companies are committed to active calls, with Esposito explaining dispatchers manage resource relocations.
chapters.title: Ariola presses Esposito on Bronx fifth firefighter pilot

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Specific engine companies that are statistically overburdened relative to their current staffing based on call volume, run frequency, or simultaneous incident rates, and can you provide data to the committee?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And those are the engine companies that historically, in those 20, that we provide the fifth firefighter to, the ones that have heavier fire duty in those areas that have the bigger buildings.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Would you support a pilot program in the Bronx with a fifth firefighter in 20 of their busiest firehouses?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Would you support that as an agency?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We would welcome additional resources.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is that something that you're willing to fight with the council for with the Mamdani administration to fund?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We would welcome additional resources to deal with this fire problem that we have.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Rather than a binary choice between full citywide development and no deployment and no deployment, has the FDNY evaluated a tiered

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: or phased approach that prioritizes the highest need companies first?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And if you have, you know, how would you prioritize that and the deployment of the firefighter?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Those fires in the Bronx that we're seeing generally in six and seven story buildings that are over 100 years old, old building stock,

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: The potential for spread in those buildings is high.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Some of these fires, the fire raced up through the stairway, so it's long hose line stretches.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: And you can compare that to Queens that in some parts have smaller buildings, shorter hose line stretches.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So that fifth firefighter would be...

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Much better off in areas with larger buildings, longer hose line stretches.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And does the fire department track instances where a company is committed to an active call and a second call comes in requiring a delayed or cross-district response?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: That's something that we don't currently track.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So we know, though, that in those areas, those busier areas with the companies that are doing,

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: responding to more incidents, that there are times they're on one, and then another company has to come over and respond to an incident because that original company is not available.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And when that other company comes over, doesn't that leave a gap in coverage for a large portion of that particular area that the second apparatus would be covering?

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: I would dispass.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: Watchers in the communications office are able to manage and move resources around the city.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: So if they do see a gap in that coverage, then they'll move another company over as well.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So kind of someone is always without coverage.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Like if you keep moving them, somebody's always without coverage.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: If you keep moving them along.

utterances.speaker: John Esposito
utterances.text: We continually assess and move resources around.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: At this point, I'd like to go over to my panel for questions on Fifth Firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Oh, your panel.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yeah, well, we have anybody who's the next up.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Oh, okay, yes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Sorry, yes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And if anybody wants to talk about Fifth Firefighter and you already spoke, just please sign up for second round.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Got it.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Wong asks about the headcount gap, with Kristoff reporting 706 total positions under budget: 316 uniform, 290 EMS, 100 civilian. Wong also asks about the 30% ambulance out-of-service rate, with Miano explaining the 600-ambulance fleet supports 340 active units with spares.
chapters.title: Wong and Kristoff discuss FDNY headcount gap and ambulance fleet

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Councilmember Palladino.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I read the testimony here that the headcount is under budgeted by 190 people.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My question is, how many of them are EMS positions?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And what does the FY27 budget doing to close that gap?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So overall, the department is under budgeted headcount total agency by 706 positions.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: 316 of that is on the uniform side, driven by firefighters and lieutenants.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: 290 of that is EMS, and about 100 of that is civilian, largely because we just got positions added.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So on the EMS side, unfortunately, because of the ongoing promotions to firefighter, although we are trying to staff up as much as possible and are aiming to hire large classes and continue

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: to recruit folks to fill those vacancies, we do anticipate that it will take us quite some time to achieve budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Regarding 600 ambulances in the field with 30% of them out of service,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That is a very high number.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you talk about what's being done?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What's going on?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You're waiting for parts?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is it a budget problem?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's 180 ambulances out of 600.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's out of service.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: So the maximum amount of ambulances that we have in the field at any given time during the two tours that we run during the day is 340.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: That is distributed throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: Although we have a fleet of 600, that's in case any ambulances like the ones that are off service.

utterances.speaker: Paul Miano
utterances.text: are off service, we can replace those ambulances with spares.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I see.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about lithium-ion battery fire funding. Kristoff says there's no separate funding pot, and Chief Correo details a comprehensive approach including Local Law 40 enforcement, post-fire investigation, and public education on UL-rated batteries and safe charging practices.
chapters.title: Wong and Correo discuss lithium-ion battery fire prevention

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My final question is on lithium ion battery fires.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I've never seen so many homes, five alarm fires in homes involving lithium batteries.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How much funding is dedicated specifically to lithium battery enforcement, inspections, public education, and specialized firefighting equipment?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you please talk about it?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: There isn't really a separate funding pot tied to lithium ion batteries.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: It's just something that's incorporated into everything.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So when, say, our public safety or fire safety education teams go out and do public education, this is one of the topics they cover.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Similarly, on the fire prevention side, there are a number of staff that work on that.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: Chief Correa may have more to add.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everybody.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: So as Commissioner Kristof had said, it's really a comprehensive approach.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: It's fire safety.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: It is tied to Local Law 40, working with other agencies to make sure that we don't have unregulated batteries that are being sold out there.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: It is addressing it as part of a post-fire investigation.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: We work very, very closely with the Bureau of Fire Investigation.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: So it's a really comprehensive approach to be able to deal with that.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: And, of course, the most important is education, right, and which has made a marked difference

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: in, I think, raising the level of awareness that folks understand that they have to–

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: it's really important to make sure that they have batteries that are UL rated, that it's a complete system, the battery, the battery charger, not, you know, basic safety things, not charging it unattended, not charging it by means of egress.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: So it's really a holistic approach to get the...

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: to the best safety outcome.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Do you think these vehicles should be stored indoors or they're safer for them to be stored outside?

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: Are you talking about electric vehicles?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, electric bikes or e-scooters, yes.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: So what we do is we make sure, in terms of electric vehicles, like cars, we don't regulate that.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: In terms of micro-mobility devices, what we do is make sure that the charging devices, the bike itself, the battery is up to the UL standards.

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: We find that's the safest way to approach to make sure that when

utterances.speaker: Thomas Correo
utterances.text: those are implemented, those are charged, whether they're outside or inside, or the battery charging cabinets, we make sure that they are at a very high level of safety standard.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Councilmember Palladino, followed by Ressler.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Palladino expresses surprise at the laid-back approach to the fifth firefighter and asks about capital funding for vehicles she allocated. DeRacus confirms the generator project for Engine 290/25 is in design with construction starting FY27. Palladino also questions the overall FDNY budget of $4.6B against the city's $124.7B budget.
chapters.title: Palladino questions fifth firefighter enthusiasm and capital funding

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I want to go back, if I could, please, just for a moment, and then I'll move on.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: The fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I expected to see and hear a lot more enthusiasm about the fifth firefighter, because there was such an uproar when Bloomberg let that fifth firefighter, when that fifth firefighter would let go.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I thought tonight, today, I would hear a lot more enthusiasm.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And as the chair asked about it, I saw a very laid-back approach.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: As

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: to the importance of the fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I was a little surprised by that.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So, thank you, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: The fifth firefighter and the staffing is currently part of the UFA contract with the city that talks about the number and a medical leave level.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: But what we are trying to do, the priority in the fire department, is we're trying to stabilize EMS.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And the fifth firefighter is an important issue, but we're really focused more on stabilizing EMS, the staffing levels, the ambulance availability, the response times.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Making sure that we're able to fill our EMS recruit classes so that we can have ambulances arrive on scene in a timely manner.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Okay, good enough.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Also, I'm going to circle back to a question I had asked a while back when we first started these hearings.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I'm curious, I'm just one council member.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And as for one council member, I've given close to a million dollars of our money to the fire department.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And that went for generators and other very important vehicles and such.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I'm wondering, where is that money?

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And when is the fire department and EMS going to see the two vehicles?

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: That I did fund fully.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And the

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: generators that I'm sure just not myself, like I said, I'm just one of 50.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I'm sure other council members have done the same.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: We can follow up on those specific projects and get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: That's the third time I've heard that.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: It's okay.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: But please get back to me because it's a lot of money.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And like I said, I'm one of 50.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: So I'm sure there's a lot of money floating out there in the millions of dollars that we need to know.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Also, your budget.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: It says that we have achieved savings without harming our ability to carry out our mission of protecting life and property, and we strive to provide the best possible service to the people of New York.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And you certainly, most certainly have done that, and I thank you for all of that.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I understand that we're all working under somewhat of a tight shoestring budget this year.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I want to see, I want to know how much of a shoestring budget is our FDNY, who should not be working with a shoestring budget at all, how much are they, how much is your budget so far for this year?

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Overall budget.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Do you know?

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Paul.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: I just wanted to...

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Hi, Paul.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Hi, how are you?

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Oh, can I turn it on?

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: No, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: I just wanted to double back on your generator question.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Sure, go right ahead.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: So we have that generator that generates for Engine Company 290.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: 25 specifically.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Oh, good.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Yes, yes.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I heard from them, yes.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: That project is currently, the design is currently complete, and we are rolling right into construction at the beginning of FI 27.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Oh, good.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: FI 27, which means July 1 is FI 27.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: So we can expect to see movement.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I'll be paying attention.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Right after July 1.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Yeah, so we've already reached out to all of our vendors and obtained their proposals and everything.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Paul DeRacus
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: As far as the overall budget goes, I mean, we are in the process of signing a budget on June the 30th of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And anybody who knows me knows I'm in a bit of an uproar about that.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Because I like to see where the money's going.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: And you guys are certainly entitled to major upgrades to your firehouses, structurally your vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: All around, you guys are entitled to pretty big...

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: If we're going to increase the budget by $100...

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: $14 billion total.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: If we're going to be increasing the budget to that kind of money, I just am curious, before I vote on the 30th, where's our money going?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So our FY27 expense budget is about $2.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: And our capital budget going from 26 through 35 is about $1.9 billion.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Okay, so let's add up those two numbers, shall we?

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Plus seven is 16.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Carry it over.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Three, four.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: So that's $4.6 billion for the fire department out of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: I think that's outrageous.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: That's all I'll say about that.

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Wrestler followed by Jay Sanchez.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Restler asks about the cost of a citywide fifth firefighter. Kristoff confirms the Council's estimate of $92M for 86 companies, roughly $173M citywide. Restler also asks about EMS pay parity costs, with Kristoff noting the average EMT salary is $57,494 versus $109,930 for firefighters.
chapters.title: Restler questions Kristoff on fifth firefighter and EMS parity costs

utterances.speaker: Vickie Paladino
utterances.text: Not in a good way.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Tough act to follow.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'll do my best.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you to the chairs and congratulations, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Really excited to have you in this role and somebody who brings EMS expertise to the agency right now, I think, is urgently needed.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I also just want to take a second to congratulate the promotion of my friend and most importantly, constituent, Jason Shelley, Deputy Commissioner, very well deserved.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: He's been such an anchor in the fire department now for a decade plus.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're really lucky to have him there.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So just a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I've been deeply concerned by the fires that we've been seeing across our city.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I know we've had a concentration of them in the Bronx that have been fatal, and it's terribly scary.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And grateful for all the work that the members of FDNY do every day to keep us safe.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But also want to make sure that we're smartly investing resources that can make the greatest difference to prevent fires from taking lives and ruining property.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So just to confirm, Chief, I believe...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I heard in your testimony that we've got 20 engines right now that have a fifth firefighter that are deployed.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Yes, there are 20 engines.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And there's no data that we have whatsoever to show that a fifth firefighter improves or doesn't improve outcomes one way or the other, response times?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Just to confirm, this is what you testified to earlier, I just want to get this clear.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Not response times, but we know that they stretch and operate hose lines.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They stretch and operate hose lines quicker, but no impact on getting fires under control faster with the fifth person or not.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have no data to back this up.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I mean, other than your anecdotal expertise.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Because we don't have that many five firefighter engines to see that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have 20, but understood, if there's no data, there's no data.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What would the cost be, Deputy Commissioner Kristof, to adding a fifth firefighter citywide?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: So we are currently in conversations with OMB on that point.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But it's an estimate that you should be able to come up with pretty directly.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Could you give us a ballpark?

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: We would defer to the budget director, who I believe is testifying.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You can't give us an estimate of what it would cost?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We know how many firehouses there are.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We know how many engines there are.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There's nobody, right, adding a fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What would that cost us?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Ballpark.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: I think that the council has cost estimates that are not out of line with the order of magnitude.

utterances.speaker: Lizette Kristoff
utterances.text: What are those estimates that the council has said?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Could you get those to us just for the purpose of the conversation?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What are the council's cost estimates?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We can follow up.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: No, come on.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're at a hearing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're asking basic questions.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know the answers to them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You should share them on the record.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What are the council's cost estimates?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm not even asking you to give the cost estimate.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What's the cost estimate?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: A council member has asked a direct question, and he deserves an answer.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So if you don't have it now, we have plenty of time to sit and wait, and then you can get that answer for him.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It is rude to sit there and not answer a direct question from an elected official

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: who came here today to represent his constituency, whether you like the question or not.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So let's work on getting that information for Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I know you've signed up for a second round, Councilmember Ressler, but if you have other questions, please proceed.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Councilmember Jay Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I have lots of other questions to ask.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I just am waiting because I really do want an answer here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But we'll come back to it on the second round.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Oh, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I don't mean to.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Go for it.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Sanchez asks about fifth firefighter locations in the Bronx, with Bonsignore confirming four Bronx engine companies have five firefighters. Sanchez asks for fire data at those stations and whether firefighters could be redistributed from lower-fire areas to the Bronx.
chapters.title: Sanchez questions Bonsignore on Bronx fifth firefighter deployment

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'll shift gears and I'll come back to my questions on the fifth firefighter later.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Commissioner, we've had some challenges with FDNY and DOT coordination on bike lanes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Does the fire department support protected bike lanes?

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: So we are a life-saving agency.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: That's our mission, our core mission, save lives.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: And we appreciate the fact that bike lanes put people in a safer place and they save lives.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: So, yeah, the fire department has no problem with bike lanes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: There are going to be these situations where maneuvering around them are an issue.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: And in those circumstances, as we have already started having conversations with the DOT to resolve those issues together.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we sent you a letter to this effect, and I don't think we got a commitment on a time frame, but Councilmember Areola passed legislation requiring coordination between DOT and FDNY on bike lanes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There's been some litigation around this as well.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can we get a commitment from the fire department that barring extraordinary circumstances, the feedback can be provided to DOT within 30 days?

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We are going to try our very, very best.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: Having these additional heads are definitely going to help speed things along.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: But sometimes they're just, you know, it's not only the process.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: It's not only the fire department, I would say, that kind of slows things down.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: It may be a more complex thing.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: That needs to be reviewed.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: It takes a little longer.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: But we certainly will commit that we'll do our best to get them a timely response.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: And we're working very well together.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: I mean, we are talking together and solving problems together.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: And we actually have seen, I can have Chief Correa talk to you about it if you wish, but we have seen some reduction in turnaround times.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The chair asked me to be brief in this round, and I've already gone over it, but I'll just say I appreciate the improved coordination.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think having a timeframe commitment that, barring extraordinary circumstances or a really complex project, that FDNY is getting back to DOT in a certain timeframe really matters for planning and execution because there's a set timeframe in the year,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: about six months, that DOT can install these projects, and we lose the windows and lose a year of safety that we can otherwise get if we're getting tight responsiveness from the fire department.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I hope we can work together to have some real accountability there.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: Yeah, I can assure you we're working well together.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And Council Member Ruster, I have you on for round two, so don't worry.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so just quickly, we can agree that it is your job and your mission to put out fires, right?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And you acknowledge that fires go out faster with the additional staffing.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Do we agree on that?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yes or no?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, as a general statement, yes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Jay Sanchez, followed by P.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: First and foremost, I want to say thank you to all the firefighters for all the work that you do day in and day out.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Also, happy Pride Month.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I know nothing has to deal with sexuality, but representation matters.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And there are people all over the city and this country that get to see someone like you and feel represented.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So thank you for the work that you do as well.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Aside from that, back to the fifth firefighters.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
