utterances.text: Representing the South Bronx and a proud co-chair of the Bronx delegation, this is truly important to us.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: We sent over a letter on February 10th in partnership with Councilmember Areola and the Bronx Borough President.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: But I want to dive deeper.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You said there are already 20 fire stations that have this fifth firefighter, but they are dependent on the higher the buildings, the more firefighters.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So is it safe to say that these 20 fire stations are located in downtown Brooklyn and midtown Manhattan?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Some of them are, but it's not necessarily high-rise buildings.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: It's buildings that have longer hose line stretches.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: In high-rise buildings, we operate off a standpipe, which is generally a shorter stretch than stretching off the apparatus.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Is there any way to identify where these 20...

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: 20 firehouses are and yes, we have them we can provide that for you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: How many of those are currently in the Bronx?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I'm not...

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Do we have that, Kevin?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Currently, four of the engine companies in the Bronx have five firefighters.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And how many engine companies are in the Bronx in total?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I think we have 30.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So how many fires have happened in these areas that have the fifth fire already during the course of this past year?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I'll have to get that for you.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So you're asking the number of fires that the engines where there are five firefighters, the number of fires they've gone to?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: Okay, we'll have to get that for you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Can we have that by the second round?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We need that information.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We need the information answered.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We asked you to come prepared.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So saying we'll get the information is not going to be tolerated.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Find the information, answer in the second round.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We have the breakdown of the engines and the fires they've had the last three fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Basically, what I'm trying to get to with my line of questioning is, have there been enough fires in where these fifth firefighters are to justify them being there?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You said earlier that based on gaps in coverage that you would move the firefighters over to cover different things.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: We would move the fire companies around.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So based on the data.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: that you already have based on the fires, has there been any indication that would suggest that moving

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: some of the stations that have a fifth firefighter to other stations in the Bronx that are experiencing higher fires, would there be any type of movement from the stations that are not experiencing fires versus the ones that are?

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: I guess we could have that discussion, but they're all going to fires.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: But it's just if the fifth firefighter is reducing times and reducing the ability, I completely understand that, you know, the cause for the fire and the prevention is not a fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: That's not what we're saying.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: The housing stock and the infrastructure, but we're not going to change the housing stock overnight.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Physically and literally impossible, but what we can do is cut down response times to what we actually do with the fires.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And I understand that question, but then you also have to see the reality that there's other council members here that if we take one of the five firefighter engines from their area, we're going to get the same issue.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Well, I think just showing from Staten Island to Brooklyn to the Bronx, we're seeing a concerned, unifying voice about the fact of everyone's recognizing the number of fires that the Bronx is going through.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And we have been on this since the very beginning.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I mean, I know this fight has been happening now for 15 years.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: From when we started.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: started as the Bronx delegation co-chair.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This has been a major fight.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So understanding that combating fire times in the Bronx is our top priority, making sure

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: that if there are other places that are not having a single fire or minimal fires compared to what we're experiencing every day, I get the all hand, the notices every day on my email, multiple times a day.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: If we're able to move those firefighters around, that also does tremendous things for the ability to take care of our fellow Bronxites.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Peace and joy.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Pierina Sanchez highlights the relentless fires in the Bronx, noting nearly 50 fatalities this year, and asks for updated fire data. She advocates a three-pronged approach: faster response, public education, and holding building owners accountable for failing systems.
chapters.title: Pierina Sanchez raises Bronx fire crisis and interagency coordination

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, cousin.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It won't get old, maybe in a couple years.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But thank you, Chair, for the unrelenting questions on the fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I have to join Chief.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I have to join Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But before I do that, I just want to echo my colleagues in thanking the FDNY, the firefighters who jump at the flames, who are there.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I feel like I live right next to a battalion, and I feel like the sirens are just nonstop, nonstop.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I thank you to our firefighters.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I thank you to our EMS.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But it does feel like the Bronx is burning.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It feels like the Bronx is burning again.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And I...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I uplift the 50 New Yorkers who we've had, almost 50 New Yorkers who we've already lost this year, and highlighting, Chief, and I'd love some clarity

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: here during this hearing, that in a private briefing with the Bronx delegation some weeks ago, you shared that the fires have nearly doubled over

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: for the last three years in the Bronx and Queens for the time period that we were in.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I'd love to just get updated numbers on that.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And then specifically, just wanna, and I should highlight for you, I'm gonna do the thing where I ask all my questions and then hopefully leave you five seconds to respond.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But I just wanna make sure to get them all in and I'm sure you'll be able to capture them.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We had a fatal fire at 226 West Tremont last weekend.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: A one-year-old passed away on Bainbridge Avenue, on the block where I grew up.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And so it really feels in the Bronx, and it feels like in my neighborhood, that it's just relentless.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It's just relentless.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In my view, I want to complicate our conversation also on the fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: My view is that we need a three-pronged approach.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We need to first make sure, yes, that our first responders have the resources for the fastest response, put out the fires as fast as possible.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We have to address things like the blocked fire hydrants that you keep raising with us in conversations.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But second, we need to also expand.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Public education immediately, right?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The things that you raised during the press conferences, the doors that weren't closed behind, we need to make sure we're highlighting that and reaching the audiences in the Bronx, in Queens, in the neighborhoods that are really impacted.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But third, and this is where I am the chair of the housing committee on the council, we have to make sure we're having a conversation that also puts the onus on the owners of these old properties to address the failing systems leading to the

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: these fires.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: At 226 West Tremont, it's an old building.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: That owner is the fourth worst in the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And in the last year, the building had 13 violations for defective and missing smoke detectors, which means that the residents were calling it in.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And in the last two weeks, there were violations for self-closing doors.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So the second question, the first question was about data.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The second question is about the interagency coordination.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: How is FDNY sharing

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: everything in real time that you're seeing about fires and causes, how are you talking about that with the Department of Buildings, with HPD, and crafting new policies and new interventions to address the root causes?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And finally, you know, as we talk about the infrastructure in these buildings, I just want to make sure that

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: That is also a part of the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And I think I mentioned this to you, Commissioner and Chief, in some of the Zooms we had.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: When FDNY has all the press in front of you, because there's been a fatal fire and there's been a tragedy,

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: just making sure that the agency does not then only talk about the self-closing doors and the smoke detectors, the interventions that the tenants are responsible for, because that feels like blaming the victims.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It feels like you're blaming...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: By only focusing on those causes, it feels like a blame on our people in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And I want to make sure that, one, we are doing, right?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We are HPD, DOB, FDNY are collaborating on solutions, but we're also

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: talking about what owners can do.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So one, data.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Two, now I am forgetting what my second question was, but I'm sure you captured it and I'll bring it back around if you didn't.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Tucker and staff detail collaboration with DOB and HPD on fire prevention, including joint PSAs with the mayor and DOB commissioner, smoke alarm distribution, and post-fire inspections that issue violations to building owners. Bonsignore reports 27 multiple-alarm fires in the Bronx as of May 13, up from 16 in 2025 and 12 in 2024.
chapters.title: Tucker and Bonsignore detail fire prevention collaboration and Bronx fire data

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: So I'll just take a couple and then the chief could jump in here.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: So one, the collaboration, right, between DOB, HPD, FDNY, we are collaborating and there is no strategy that we won't

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: you know, try to get this message out there.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We have done collaborative PSAs.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: I've done PSAs with the Commissioner of Aging.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: I've done PSAs with the DOB Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: The mayor himself put out PSAs for us.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We had him do a PSA because he has a large following.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: So he's done a PSA for us.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We have life safety.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: Pamphlets and different things out there, we're working together to try to solve a problem because we know that lives are attached to this, right?

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We know that people are going to die if we don't get this message out, if they don't understand how to behave, if they actually get into a fire situation.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: Now, we don't want them to get into a fire situation, which is why we talk about things like overloading outlets, overloading power strips.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We talk about not putting your space heater too close to things that are combustible.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: And, you know, I can appreciate what you're saying as far as how do we...

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We talk about this when we're in the press.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: When we're in the press, there are a couple of things we have to hit, right?

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We have to give them strategies, give the people strategies, so that they can share that information amongst themselves if they get into a place where they're in an emergent situation.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: And it's not intended to put blame on anybody, but if...

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: If we have these situations where these violations exist, and our fire prevention folks go in there and they write those violations too, right?

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: But if those places exist and those violations exist already, telling the owners is not necessarily going to solve the problem in the press.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: Telling the people what they should be getting, informing them of how your door should work, how your apartment, how you're supposed to have fire

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: smoke alarms, that is the stuff that gets them calling 311, gets them reporting the building, reporting their issues to the supers and the landlords.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We want them to be as educated as possible, and we will do anything we can to try to keep these folks safe.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We give smoke alarms.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We install smoke alarms.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We want people to know that, that if you need smoke alarms, we can do that for you.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: You can call 311 and say, I need smoke alarms, and we can come and make that happen.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We want people to know that their doors are supposed to close behind them.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: And a lot of people rig it so it doesn't, because they don't want to get...

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: locked out if they walk out into the hallway.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: We want them to know these things because these are the things that keep them safe.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: So we certainly don't want to put blame on the victim, but we want to educate the victim and give them as much information so that they can share it with their families and friends

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: and be safe if, God forbid, they end up in a position where they're trying to survive in a fire situation.

utterances.speaker: Robert S. Tucker
utterances.text: As far as the data, you can give the data.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So for multiple alarm fires in the Bronx, at this time, as of May 13th this year, we've had 27 multiple fires, multiple alarm fires.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: On May 13th, 2025, we were at 16.

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: And May 13th, 2024, we were at 12.

chapters.summary: Pierina Sanchez asks what information FDNY shares with HPD and DOB about electrical fire causes, and whether FDNY advocates for building preservation grants to address electrical systems. Staff confirm recent meetings with DOB commissioner and work on developing new technologies to detect electrical fire risks.
chapters.title: Pierina Sanchez presses FDNY on interagency data sharing for fire prevention

utterances.speaker: Lillian Bonsignore
utterances.text: So a significant increase, as we've said before.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Chair, may I just ask a quick follow-up?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We're on the same team here.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We want New Yorkers to be safe and avoid these situations.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: On that vein, FDNY, you folks throw yourselves at the hardship.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: yourself at the emergency.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So let me take it to the to a different place, which is what information does FDNY share with buildings and HPD?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And are you actively, at the FDNY executive level, are you actively talking about, hey,

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Buildings are having electrical shorts all over the place.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: You all should really have this in your scopes of work when you're putting out preservation grants or tax incentives.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you having that conversation with your sister agencies?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Because as the housing chair, it does not feel like what we're getting on the council, what we're approving, the scopes of work that we're approving for tax incentives are not covering these electrical causes of fires.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I just want to understand what the conversation is.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We recently met with the Commissioner of DOB and discussed those issues.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Electrical fires have been consistently the leading cause of fire within the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And our buildings continue to get older and older, and we continue to see these electrical fires.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We are working on developing new technologies that may potentially alert us to these fires prior to them occurring.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There are some...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You know, hopeful items out there that we're going to work on and work with some national laboratories.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've already consulted with Underwriters Laboratory to try to develop some of these products for use within the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Obviously, we are a huge city with a complicated

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: complicated.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: set of buildings here.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're one of the oldest cities in the nation.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we do work with other agencies as well and make sure that we address these problems prior to them starting.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And to the Commissioner's point, we never want to blame anybody for any actions that cause these fires, but we do want to make sure

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We take that risk with the idea that somebody else won't die.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And fire prevention does a tremendous job to issue violations prior to the fire as well as after the fire.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we do do a post-fire inspection, and all those violations do go to the owner of the building.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And a lot of those issues that we addressed at the press conference, those are directly related to the fire that just occurred right before that press conference occurred.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we want to strike while the iron's hot.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And some of those things we don't know at the time of the press conference either.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have our marshals go out and they investigate whether the smoke detectors were installed properly.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's not information we may have at the time of those press conferences.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we want to make sure that we get the information out to the building residents immediately so that doesn't occur again.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs, for the time.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Maloney asks about FDNY capacity to handle supertall buildings. Staff explain newer buildings have auxiliary fire protection systems and are generally more fire-safe than older buildings, though EMS response times to upper floors can be longer.
chapters.title: Maloney questions FDNY on supertall building safety

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, we have Councilmember Maloney followed by Councilmember Gennaro.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: My question is just high-rise buildings because there are a lot.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm not a big fan.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Oh, wait, Maloney, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Go ahead.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Gennaro asks about EMS retention, with staff reporting 10.9% attrition last year and 14.3% annualized this year, with about 25% of EMS workforce shifting to fire during promotion cycles. Staff detail training costs: $41,000 per EMT, $130,000 per paramedic, and explain how pay parity would reduce costly turnover.
chapters.title: Gennaro and staff discuss EMS retention, training costs, and pay parity

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I wanted to return to the question about EMS retention and the career pipeline there.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: One of the challenges you've already alluded to is significant workforce turnover and difficulties with both recruitment and retention for EMS.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: What percent of EMS are leaving each year and what percent of those leaving are heading to the FDM or to FIRE?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the attrition rate last year was about 10.9%.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the annualized attrition rate for this year is about 14.3%.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's much higher than it would be in a year in which there weren't firefighter promos.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So in years without firefighter promos, the attrition rate is closer to 7% or 8%.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Okay, so you roughly what percent would you say is converting to FDNY roles?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Seven?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Converting to FDNY to firefighter?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: During a promotion cycle, which is multiple classes, they're all coming from a promo list so they go consecutively.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We will shift about 25% of the EMS workforce from EMS into fire.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And what percent of the incoming firefighter academy class then would be from EMT or EMS?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It'll be a promotion class for the most part.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So that entire group of people who left EMS will end up being the probationary class in the fire academy.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And is the training different or the same?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: For those two roles?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, there are different focuses, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So, you know, we're the...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: People coming from EMS have been certified as EMTs or paramedics, and they're required to recertify their EMT and paramedics every four years in order to maintain their positions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Once they go into the fire academy, then they're taught firematics, and they're taught firefighting, you know,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You can talk a little bit more about what you're teaching them, but it is specific to the job that they're doing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But when it comes to an operational perspective, once they've moved over from EMS to fire, outside of the CFR engine,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They are no longer doing EMT level or paramedic level work.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're doing firefighting work, which is the stuff that they learned in the fire academy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The current PROVY class that we have in the fire academy is all EMS promotions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Later in the year, we'll hire another class that

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 60 to 70% will be EMS promotions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And then what they learn in the fire academy, whether you came from EMS or not, the curriculum is the same, and it's all about firefighting operations that EMS does not currently have and do.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And just so we're clear, once those folks become firefighters, they're no longer working on an ambulance in any way.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So they're not able to go across.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Cross from fire to EMS.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So asking both questions together, one is have you considered if it'd be more cost effective to close that promotional pathway if the training is different?

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And then the other is what role EMS compensation and increasing that compensation plays and if that would be more cost effective than continuously investing in recruiting and

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: trying to retrain or train new people if you're cycling out 10 to 14 percent.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Right, so there has been lots of discussion about the promotion pathway from EMS to fire because it does take such a large chunk of EMS at one

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: one date range and put them into another area of the department.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: However, because of that promotion process, people come to EMS.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So in order to be eligible for a promotion, you have to work at EMS for four years.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So what generally happens is somebody who wants to be a firefighter will come in through EMS, spend their four years at EMS, and then look to promote into fire.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So if I were to close that promotion process, that...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That segment of people that come to EMS to become firefighters.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We know law

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: longer have a reason to come to EMS to become firefighters.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So without there being some significant compensation so that if you were an EMT or a paramedic, you can make the choice as to whether you want to stay in EMS or become a firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's the pay parity part, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If the money was in place so that people didn't have to leave EMS to go to fire to make more money,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: then the expectation is those people would stay in EMS and we would have to train far fewer people

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: because right now we're training constantly and it's pretty costly to continue to train, to move people from one area of the department to the other area of the department.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: And just to close out, I know I'm out of time, but do you have an estimate of what those costs are on recruiting and training?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, so for EMT, it costs $41,000 to train a coming in the door, a new employee, EMT.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For a paramedic, it costs $130,000.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we train our EMTs to be paramedics.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So there are people that work for us who are being upgraded to paramedic.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For refresher training, which is what happens every four years,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For an EMT, it's $6,300.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And for a medic, it's $9,000.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Essentially, we would save money if we were able to keep people longer than having to keep bringing them in at the entry level.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about supertall building safety in round two. Staff reiterate that newer supertall buildings are more fire-safe than older buildings due to auxiliary fire protection systems, though response times to upper floors can be longer.
chapters.title: Maloney follows up on supertall building fire safety

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: So round two, we have Councilmember Brewer followed by Councilmember Wong.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Sorry for the mistake earlier.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: My question is just tall buildings.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Some of your previous commissioners would bring to my attention, because on the Upper West Side, there are developers who just want to build to the sky.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And the comment was, we just don't have enough capacity to deal with these supertalls.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And obviously, we want development, or some people want a lot of development.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I want lower development.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And yet, I really have no concept whether or not what was said in the past is true.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Is this something you're thinking about?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Is there something that you're concerned about with these supertalls, which seem to be...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: proliferating.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering about your opinion on that.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And then I didn't say earlier, I don't think that in participatory budgeting, we got a new HVAC system on ladder 25 on 77th Street.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So Westsiders just love your members.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Just want to let you know.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Well, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: But in terms of the supertalls.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You want to talk about supertolls?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So since the turn of this century, the building materials and building technology has allowed us to build smaller buildings, but much taller than historically, than we've been able to.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And one of the issues that we face with that is it could take us longer to get from the sidewalk upstairs than it does to get from the EMS station or the firehouse to the scene.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we do look at response times, but these are newer built buildings.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They have auxiliary fire protection systems.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're generally more fire safe than the older buildings that we're talking about and generally have less fire instances in them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You might have more emergencies, and the same with EMS and their response time to be able to get upstairs to provide life-saving care.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So you're saying in terms of the fire, not the emergency heart attack or whatever, the fire that you're feeling that they're somewhat safe is what you're saying?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, they're more fire safe than some of the older buildings that we have.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Well, I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I wish you had said the opposite because I can't stand them.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: But thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Ressler.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Won asks about EMT-to-firefighter versus EMT-to-paramedic transitions and retention. Staff explain 506 EMS members left for firefighter promotions over three classes, with about 70 paramedic graduates per class. Won presses on fixing the retention rate within the 30-day budget process, with staff noting the 30-year unresolved pay parity issue.
chapters.title: Won questions FDNY on EMS retention and pay parity timeline

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Do you have figures on how many EMTs left to become EMTs?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: firefighter versus how many EMTs left to become paramedics for the past year?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And is pay gap the reason or what can we do to

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: to improve the retention rate.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think I have those numbers, although I might have to look up some of them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So just in terms of how many EMS members have left to become firefighters, you know, over the last three firefighter classes, we've lost 506 members.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I just want to, EMTs don't leave the job to become paramedics.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They promote to paramedic after going through rigorous training.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So they still stay on EMS.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Each paramedic class, we run approximately two a year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We graduate about 70 from each one of those classes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And we've been talking about this since the last hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: How do we fix this retention rate?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Is that being considered in the current budget year or is still something that's not solved?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think the commissioner has said it multiple times that one of the things that would help us with retention is

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: the EMS pay being increased, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Like we have members who are out there dedicated every single day, every single day.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: that deserve to be compensated for the work that they do.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Right here in the room, just looking around, we have an incentive program every single month.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have what we call a unit of the month program.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That unit of the month, for the first time ever this month, the same unit from the Bronx,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: has won that title two months in a row.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And just two of those members happen to be sitting right here.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I wish your counterpart was here because they actually are from his district.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think we need to recognize the work that our EMTs and our paramedics do, and they should be compensated for it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: But in the current budget, the EMS pay levels remain the same.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So as of this point, these conversations are still happening between OLR, OMB, and the FDNY unions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're in the contract negotiations now, and I don't have a seat at that table.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So, you know, it's an ongoing conversation.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's a conversation we continue to bring light to because we do believe it's step one to stabilizing the EMS system, which, you know, as the chief spoke about and Chief Miano has spoken about, is a real thing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Like, I think at this point, we need to stabilize our EMS system, and the pay is going to be one of those things that set us on the right path to get that done.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And possibly then we can...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: look at promotion processes, right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So if there is an option to stay in EMS, somebody may take that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But right now, we have a 17,000 person department who are willing to do just about anything to keep New Yorkers safe.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They'll do it in good weather and bad weather.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They'll do it in any time of year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And as you've seen over the last, you know, the last winter we had, which is extraordinarily cold, which caused a lot of the fires we're seeing now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Or you see it during a pandemic, which was incredibly challenging.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The fire department never turns their back on New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So at this point, we're saying we have to prepare ourselves and build this department in a way that provides these services for the next five decades for New York.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's what this conversation is about, trying to make sure that we can have a robust and sustained EMS system to provide those cares.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Yeah, but we have a budget process that's due in 30 days, and what you're saying is that we don't know how to fix this pay gap until the contract negotiations have definite terms.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And that's going to take a lot longer than the 30 days that I'm putting together the budget.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, I will say that those conversations are ongoing, and EMS has been part of the fire department for 30 years, and this has been unresolved.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I'm guessing in the next 30 days it probably will not be resolved, but I do hope that we get to a point where we resolve it sooner rather than later.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Cheers.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Restler asks again for the citywide fifth firefighter cost. Kristoff confirms approximately $173M citywide based on Council estimates. Restler asks if given $180M, would EMS pay parity or fifth firefighter be the priority. Bonsignore states EMS pay would be at the top of the list, and Restler agrees.
chapters.title: Restler and Bonsignore debate fifth firefighter vs. EMS pay priority

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thanks so much.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So let's try again.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Fresh take, take two.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Approximately how much would a fifth firefighter cost if we were to be implemented citywide?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the city council has released an estimated number of 92 million for 86 companies.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's about half of the companies.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It would be 173 if you were to expand it citywide.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And you think those estimates were roughly accurate based on your assessment?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For order of magnitude purposes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So roughly we're talking about $180 million to go citywide.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That would be the city council estimate.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And no independent perspective from the fire department?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You think that that's roughly broad strokes?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That seems like a reasonable estimate.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think order of magnitude, that's about right.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, we're in conversation with OMB about the estimate.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have some differences and we're working.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, I've really appreciated, and I don't mean to cut you off.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You're one of the best finance directors at any agency in the city, so I know you know the answers to all these questions, and I will push because there are some folks that could sit up on this panel and wouldn't be able to answer questions.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You are not one of them, so I appreciate you coming back to us.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I really appreciate, Commissioner, you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: coming in with a very forceful position on supporting EMS workers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Any orders of magnitude?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There have been a range of approaches on pay equity.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I know that it's subject to negotiations, but can you speak to the range of costs that have been discussed to improve compensation for EMS?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So this is not speaking to the range, but I know that we have deferred to OLR on what it would cost, maybe just to put some context around what the scale is.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The average EMT salary, looking at base salary and additions to gross, but not including overtime, is $57,494.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The average firefighter salary, base salary, additions to gross, no overtime, is $109,930.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So it's almost double.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's 91% higher.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so if we were to double the salary of every EMS worker, which I don't think is the likely outcome, but something along those, how much would that cost us?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have a broad sense of, I mean, I've seen estimates that have ranged that EMS is advocating, EMTs are advocating for increases in wages from $80 million up to $200 million or even more, potentially over a multi-year period.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Comparable number, I mean, numbers that are in a similar ballpark to what we could be talking about for a fifth firefighter.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Considering how dire the situation is with EMT worker retention in a world of limited resources, I really do hope that we focus on fixing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: this crisis that we have at EMT, the fact that after five years an EMT can't make more than $59,000 is disgraceful.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I never, the starting salary in my office is north of that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And frankly, these are kids coming out of college who are great and bright and amazing, and I'm lucky to have them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Person who's spent five years saving lives across the city of New York deserves more and deserves better.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I really, and then coming back, if we were to add a fifth firefighter at a cost of approximately $180 million citywide, I'll ask Commissioner or Chief, if you were handed

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: a bucket of $180 million in cash and we're told that you could do anything in your power to reduce fire fatalities in the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is hiring a fifth firefighter at the top of your list, do you think that would have the greatest impact?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's what we're looking at.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're trying to figure out what would have the greatest impact.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It would have an impact.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't know if that would be the greatest impact in every company around the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I imagine that we could look at a lot.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If I had that bucket of money, right, certainly EMS pay would be the top of the list.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I certainly appreciate that, too.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I share that priority.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: we're on the same page.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: For the narrower question of how do we address what we have as a crisis of fires in the Bronx and situations that need to be resolved, fire prevention investments,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: investments to address the most concerning housing stock, there are a variety of different targeted ways that we could probably

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: smartly spend a whole lot less resources that could have a bigger impact in reducing fatalities from fire than a fifth firefighter and an extraordinary cost of $180 million citywide.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that fair?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think that's fair.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So recognizing that and also recognizing that you said that the fifth firefighter, the thing where they have the greatest impact is getting the hose out and getting water out.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Fire stock in the Bronx, six, seven-story buildings.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that the height of buildings that we need to get that fifth firefighter in place?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Are those the areas that you would prioritize it having the greatest impact?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As a general statement, yes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's a longer hose line stretch, 75 feet, 70, 75 feet tall is the height of a building.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Above that, you need a standpipe in it, which is different from stretching off the apparatus.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And then just to make sure I got this right on the record, you said that the response times in Queens and Staten Island are slower than in the Bronx?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You had five minutes and ten seconds in Queens and five minutes and two seconds, five minutes and ten seconds in Queens, five minutes and five seconds in Queens, five minutes and two seconds in Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Did I have those right?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The Bronx was ranked third in terms of response time citywide.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So Bronx was 4 minutes, 58 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Queens was five minutes, two seconds.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Staten Island, five minutes, five seconds.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, so all about the same.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's year-to-date average response time to structural fires.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I really appreciate the responses.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm sorry to get frustrated earlier.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I just think in a world where we have limited resources, we want to invest them in the places they're going to make the greatest difference in saving lives.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we do have a crisis in the Bronx that I'm very committed to advocating for with my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I have seen Councilmember Pierina Sanchez in particular out on site with the men and women of the FDNY just in dire situations.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We do need to do more up there.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I just want to make sure we're spending our money smartly, and that's an extraordinary expense that we don't have data to back up the difference that it would make.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so I hope we can be smart about how we prioritize things from here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Ariola cites independent studies showing the fifth firefighter eliminates kinks, enables hose shuffle, puts water on fire faster, and reduces firefighter fatigue, with Esposito agreeing. Ariola then asks about facilities tracking systems, with staff confirming they use an EAM asset management program and Niedermann systems, all currently operable.
chapters.title: Ariola cites fifth firefighter studies and questions facilities tracking

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So I know that you said that you don't have any real data about the fifth firefighter and whether or not the fifth firefighter helps.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We have opinions that we've been sharing back and forth.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: But there have been independent studies done, and I do have one here where it notes that

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The fifth firefighter makes a difference by eliminating kinks and drag, which you guys know the lingo, enables

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: the hose shuffle, freeze the pump operator, puts water faster on the fire, and reduces firefighter fatigue.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Would you agree with that?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think that's an accurate statement.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So getting more staffing in the busiest companies will put a line in a place quicker,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: limit the spread of a fire and the loss of life and the displacement of residents at the same time, reduce the strain on firefighters operating on that fire.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Would you agree with that?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As a general statement, yes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Ariola raises concerns about deferred maintenance and capital projects pushed to out-years. Kristoff reports $994M for facilities across FY26-35, with $553M in the first five years, noting capital funding has nearly doubled over five years. Ariola presses on why firehouses remain in disrepair despite funding.
chapters.title: Ariola and Kristoff debate capital project backlog and deferred maintenance

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We're going to move on to, well, does the fire department have a platform that tracks and prioritizes work order requests, assessments, and resolution?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, we do.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have an asset management program.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I didn't hear you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, we do.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have an EAM.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's an asset management program.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, and you use the Niedermann systems as well, or?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, we have Niedermann systems.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Niedermann systems.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And how many systems are inoperable right now?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Zero.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Zero.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They're all operable?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Let's see.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The FDNY has publicly cited a substantial backlog of capital projects.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: facilities funding to the out years.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Meanwhile, we have identified a list of firehouses with severely deteriorating roofs, of which only six projects have been funded to date.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We have serious concerns about deferred maintenance.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The adequacy of near-term capital funding, and the administration's reliance on council member allocations to fund what could be core infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Do you have an amount needed to repair all capital projects, and have you requested that from the administration?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So to talk about facilities funding in general, so our current capital plan includes $994 million for facilities across FY26 through FY35.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The funding is split pretty evenly between the first five years and the last five years.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's actually slightly front-loaded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So FY26 through FY30 is $553 million, whereas FY31 through FY35 is $441 million.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But even with that, I mean, we have many facilities.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have over 400 buildings, and they are old.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the average age of our firehouses is approaching 100 years old.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so our state of good repair needs have exceeded our budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think we are seeing the budget move in the right direction.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So this plan includes an additional $24 million for roofs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And, you know, we have also seen that our capital funding for facilities over the last five years has nearly doubled.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've seen significant adds to our budget over the last five years.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yet many firehouses remain in disrepair, a few of which, and a number of you, our commissioner, our chief, and certainly Paul, we were out there looking at them.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So how can you...

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: take this pool of funding that is pushed into the out years and encourage the administration to bring that funding forward.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we do have conversations with OMB about project accelerations, and the executive budget did include some funding accelerations on the facility side.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It included accelerating $11.7 million into next year for renovations at one of our fleet maintenance buildings, which will expand capacity.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It included accelerations of $1.7 million into 27 and $10.1 million into 28 for multi-component renovations at EMS Station 4.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It included $10.5 million into FY27 to replace the apparatus for Engine Company 163, and $6.8 million into FY27 to replace air compressors at our mask services unit.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think there are lots of projects that we're looking at to see if there's a potential for acceleration.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Bandwidth is a part of this, too.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the additional civilian headcount that was added in this plan will be incredibly beneficial towards getting more projects done.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So that will allow us to hire more trade staff and facilities to make repairs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We'll help with our project management capability.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We'll help with procurement.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we've lost a lot of procurement staff, and adding back some of those folks will help get those design and construction.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Construction contracts that we need to get that capital work moving done faster.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, so.

chapters.summary: Ariola questions why capital projects rely on council member allocations rather than administration funding, noting many firehouses were built between 1895-1929. Kristoff notes several roofs were funded by council members and explains the six funded priority projects, with DeRacus detailing completed and ongoing roof projects.
chapters.title: Ariola presses on council member reliance for firehouse repairs

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So realistically, we're not going to see any of this money anytime soon, because once it goes into the far out years, like 35, it oftentimes evaporates, as you know, because we have a lot of firehouses, you said, are nearing 100 years old, and there hasn't been

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: a nickel put into them in 100 years, and we've seen

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: the result of that, which is complete deterioration.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We have work orders in for 25 years that have not been fulfilled, and we'll get into that later on.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We've identified a list of firehouses with severely deteriorating roofs, totaling approximately 80.9 million in estimated need as of 2024.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: With costs now escalating further.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So that's the other problem.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: As we push further into the out years, the cost of fixing that fire

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That issue, that EMS station is much larger, so we don't have the money to cover it fully.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And a lot of it gets eaten up in the design process.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And that's what we're trying to stop.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Six of those projects have since been funded, right?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, what is the status of the remaining underfunded roof projects by the list that my office gave?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: To the fire department, and when does the department expect to address them?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So first we just want to express our appreciation because one of those roofs was funded by you, Councilmember Areola.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Actually, several of those roofs were funded by council members.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So there was funding provided by Councilmember Riley, Farias, and Carr.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And you were also instrumental in getting some of that roof funding added for the big house, which was another one of the roofs on the list.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So for the remaining roofs, that's what the funding that was added for the six roofs will go towards.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It will help us prioritize the next six roofs that are unfunded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We do have some roof projects already underway, which I will defer to Commissioner Dorakis to talk about.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We definitely do have some roofs that are on the way.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Some are already completed or nearing completion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That would be Engine 325, Engine 161, Engine Company 54, Engine 74, and Engine 301.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we are constantly assessing the conditions of our roofs with our trade staff.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we do as much of the repairs as we possibly can while we are constantly working with the home base to accelerate that funding forwards, if possible.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And as you mentioned, a lot of those roofs that are already funded came from council members that were funding in their own districts and the big house.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Council member Selvena Brooks Powers advocated for that.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I advocated for that.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And we saw the dish repair there, but the funding is fully there.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: But why is it always on the city council?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Why doesn't the administration take care of their buildings?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: When we visited buildings throughout the five boroughs, the condition of those buildings, if visited by the fire department or the DOB, no one would be able to inhabit them.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And that's a problem.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So many of the buildings on this list that we provided for you, which I'm looking to see here.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, none of which made your list, but okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: You know, were constructed between 1895 and 1929.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That, you know, the fire department has to advocate for these projects, not just rely on the city council.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So how many of these projects were sent

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: by the fire department to the admin asking for funding to complete them.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Was it just the six?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we have had conversations with OMB and City Hall on all of our capital needs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so they are certainly aware of the condition of our facilities and the broad needs that we've identified for them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we do operate under funding constraints.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so the six are sort of the priority ones that have been identified and funded.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Right, but they won't be even addressed until 2035, is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The funding is currently in fiscal 31, and we're currently assessing to see if it's possible to accelerate.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right, and of the list that the office provided you, how many of those projects did you send to council members?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: When you were asking for budget priority requests?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We can double check, but probably quite a few of them.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Anybody here?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: You got a request?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, none of us.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I see that Councilmember Hudson has come back into the room, and I'm going to defer to her right now for her to ask her questions.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Hudson asks what percentage of total capital needs the current funding represents. Kristoff estimates 25-30%, noting the department has over 400 buildings with an average age approaching 100 years, and capital needs are a rotating challenge as buildings age.
chapters.title: Hudson questions Kristoff on percentage of capital needs funded

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chairs, and I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I had to run to another meeting, so apologies.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And please do tell me if these questions have been asked.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: But I do want to just...

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Jump in on the capital piece.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I don't know all of the questions that Chair Areola has already asked, but just want to say that in your testimony you referenced some capital dollars in this fiscal year as well as the out years.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And by my count, the funding would go towards the renovations of about eight buildings or so.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I was just curious to know what percentage

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: that represents of your entire physical infrastructure that needs repair, stations, firehouses, any other properties?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think the things that were mentioned in the testimony were probably the roofs that were just funded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, six roofs, I think.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And then there were a few projects that were accelerated.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But those are not the only projects funded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There are many more projects actually funded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Those were just the ones that were changed.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Many more that are...

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Are funded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, many more.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Many more capital improvements that are funded already?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So of your total capital needs, how many of the projects would you say are funded?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, our capital needs are significant.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Are great, I know, that's what I'm getting at.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm trying to help you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we do have, as I said, $994 million across the 10-year plan for capital, and $553 million of that is in the first five years of the plan.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So almost half.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Roughly?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: About half.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So if that 994 was a number?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: 994 million is completed, then all your capital needs are taken care of?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, as I said before, given the number of our facilities and their current state of repair, our capital needs go beyond our existing funding.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm just trying to figure out what of the existing funding that's been allocated to address capital needs, what percentage of that, what percentage is that of the total capital needs you have?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would have to say it's about 25 to 30%.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm not going to attempt to do quick math in my head, but $100 million, roughly speaking, or $994 million, you said, is about 25% to 30% of your total needs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Or is it the 500 million?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I believe it's the $500 million.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: The $500 million is 25% to 30% of your total capital needs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't think we have it quantified that way.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think, you know, we have 400 or so buildings.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Many of these buildings are a century year old.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Those capital projects will continue to pop up as we go forward.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't think there's any...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Any place where we could say all our capital needs will be secured and done if you give us this amount of money because it's a it's a rotating thing it's you know as the buildings age yeah more and more things pop up

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: so that's i think that's what you see and like why we're struggling to try to give you an actual percentage um the the money that we have

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: is the money that's going to go towards the capital, the most needed capital projects.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, I was just going to add for context, I mentioned this earlier, but I don't know if it was when you were out.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we have seen our capital funding for facilities increase pretty substantially in the last five years.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If you looked five years ago, so in 2021, our facilities funding was only about 37% of our capital budget, and now it's more than half.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's 52% of our capital budget.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
