utterances.text: It's grown by nearly $500 million.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, that's great.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There's still a lot of state of good repair needs that we have to address, but we are.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: are seeing the budget move in the right direction towards funding those state of good repair needs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And another component of this is really having the bandwidth in terms of civilian staff to actually execute those projects.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so the 84 civilian positions that we got in this budget are instrumental towards doing that, both staff for facilities and for procurement to execute on those design and construction contracts.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I'm, you know, not trying to be difficult, just I'm trying to get down to the fact that I would argue you could use far more capital dollars to address the needs at all of your buildings.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And so I think you could probably agree with that sentiment, perhaps.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: More money would solve more problems.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: There you go.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks about cold weather EMS call volume increases and dedicated budget allocations. Staff confirm significant call volume increases during extreme weather but no specific budget adjustments. Hudson also asks about firefighter applicant demographics, with Noonan reporting the most recent exam passers as 22% Black, 28% Hispanic, 40% white, 6% Asian, and 7% women.
chapters.title: Hudson questions cold weather budget and firefighter demographics

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And then I just want, did anyone ask about cold weather events yet?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, if I can just ask a couple questions.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: What percentage increase in EMS calls occurs during extreme cold weather events compared to normal conditions?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't know if we have the exact number.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Do you have the exact number?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't think we have the exact number of what percentage, but certainly this last winter, that was way colder than any other winter we remember, and for a longer period of time, we saw a significant number increase in coal volume.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As part of the city's engagement with the folks that were out in the field, we were right front center stage on that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So anytime that we see stretches of bad weather, whether it be cold or hot, the call volume goes up for us.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And it goes up both on fire and EMS.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Maybe you can follow up with the specifics.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, we can get back to you with that.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And then what additional budget allocations have been designated for extreme cold weather operations?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We haven't had any specific budget adjustments tied to extreme cold weather.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, to the extent that, you know, we've incurred additional overtime costs, that's been part of the adjustments that were made in the last budget for our PS budget.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I mean, I would suggest that just given where we're going globally in terms of climate change and all of that, and like you mentioned, Commissioner, the winters are going to get colder and longer, the summers are going to get hotter and longer.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I would strongly advocate for more funding or adjusted funding to accommodate, you know, over time and the needs as they grow during those extreme weather conditions.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And then just one last question, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: What's the demographic breakdown of applicants versus hires as far as percentages by race and gender?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We'll let Commissioner Newman talk to you about that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Are you referring to our firefighter applicants?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Both, if you have it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would have it for the fire applicants.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, I'll take what you have.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So our most recent exam, we hired, we had about 20,000, over 20,607 passers.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 22% of those passers were black, 28% Hispanic, 40% white, and 6% Asian.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 7% were women.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Are passers hired?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're not hired.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We haven't actually hired any of these candidates yet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're just now going through the first step, which is passing the physical exam.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So that will continue through the end of June, and then they'll start the background process.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Do you have any of those statistics for previous fiscal years?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So for our previous firefighter exam,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Give me a moment.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Where did I put it?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If you give me a second, I'll...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So for the last exam, we had a...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In terms of our test takers, we had 28%

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Black, 27% Hispanic, and 40, no, I'm sorry, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I need glasses.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 25% black.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Our previous exam, 7001, which was held in 2016-2017, 25% black, 27% Hispanic, and 44% white.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Those are for the applicants?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Those are for the test takers.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: The test takers, and then how many were hired?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Was the demographic breakdown similar?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The demographic breakdown is similar for those that were reachable.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: 18% black.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Sorry, what do you mean by reachable?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Reachable meaning they scored high enough on exam to be reached, to be processed.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So does that mean hired?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Not.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Specifically, not necessarily.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The hiring numbers would be different.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Do you have the hiring numbers?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't have that on me.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't have the numbers of those that were hired from the last exam.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we can very easily get that for you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And also just want to...

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Express my gratitude for the entire department for everything that you

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: you do to keep New Yorkers safe and we greatly appreciate it and I would love to see more resources and more money and pay parity for EMS division.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you chairs.

chapters.summary: Ariola notes the fire department's own priority list from 2025 doesn't match the projects being addressed, and questions why. Staff explain funded projects move off the unfunded priority list. Ariola then begins presenting facility photos.
chapters.title: Ariola questions FDNY priority list discrepancies

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I just want to note that I was just given another list that actually the fire department gave our office in 2025 that were priority engine companies and EMS stations that needed,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: they were in terrible disrepair.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And again,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The engine companies that you gave, 325, 161, 54, 74, and 301, they're not even on your own priority list.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: You didn't take anyone off your own priority list.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is there a reason for that?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Because those projects that were referred to were already funded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So they would have been higher priorities, but because we were able to allocate funding to them, they weren't on the unfunded list.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And so as projects get funded, they will move off that unfunded priority list.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So the list that I have here, are they in priority?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: FT4,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: S or I number 7 and S228.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Are they in priority order in this chart?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: When the funding is available.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't think that they are in priority order, but we could follow up.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, we're going to move on.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I'm so sorry.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Councilmember Ressler has one follow-up question.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Just one question.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm really sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Commissioner, remind me, how many years you worked in EMS?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I worked 31 years before I retired.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So one of the things that we hear a lot about, just like Councilmember Brewer, is the extreme impacts of the noise from ambulances.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We, of course, want ambulances to get where they need to go as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But I have long worried about the impact it has on workers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Have you ever thought about the impact the sirens has on the hearing of EMS workers?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is this something that you're concerned about at all?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, that's a common concern for all first responders because we spend our lives underneath the sirens.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If you ask my family, they will probably tell you that I've suffered from that problem.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But yeah, it's definitely something we look at, and it's something that the entire EMS community looks at.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's where these conversations about rumblers versus sirens versus wailing versus intermittent, like all those conversations come out of that concern.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But that's...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That would be the same for all first responders.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I think it's just worth remembering.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We banned smoking in restaurants because of the safety of, thank you, Gail Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Yes, good.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We should just premise most statements.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Gail Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But preface most statements, excuse me.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But we banned smoking in restaurants because of the safety of the workers in the restaurants and the health impacts they suffered from secondhand smoke.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think it's worth thinking about, do we need to devise a safer siren for the workers that are dealing with that noise, you know, throughout all day long, every single day?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, I appreciate you bringing that up.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, we're happy to continue those conversations.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think, you know, these sirens.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Need to be safer for the people who sit under them all day long.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're exposed to so many things.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The siren is one aspect of that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But they also need to be functional in a way that people in a city this size can hear us.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Recently, we've added an EMS division chief to our safety battalion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And just last year during the summer, we actually did do a decibel pilot in which we monitored or we tested the levels of the siren inside the cab.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we expanded that to our Gator and ASAP units, which some of the council members have been so gracious to be able to purchase for us.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So these are the units that operate on the beach during the summer because they're in an open compartment rather than a closed compartment.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And we had those decibel levels tested, and we tried out different types of hearing protection that also allowed them to be able to hear the radio transmissions and pedestrians.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And it wasn't very conclusive that the hearing protection changed.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The decibel level wasn't above the OSHA amount inside, and the hearing protection was more of a hindrance to the members than it was through their own...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Through what they stated, it was more of a hindrance to them hearing the radio, hearing pedestrians.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we are, but we are looking at that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you both for the response.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And thank you, chairs, for the latitude.

chapters.summary: Ariola presents a chart of fire apparatus restricted from relocation due to union contract provisions about apparatus age. Esposito explains the Seagrave manufacturing backlog, with 51 outdated apparatus in the field, and Kristoff confirms all are funded and ordered but delayed by manufacturing.
chapters.title: Ariola and Esposito discuss restricted fire apparatus and replacement delays

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, I'd like to ask you to take a look at the screen.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The chart on the screen shows a list of fire apparatus.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The ones highlighted in yellow are restricted from relocation under any circumstances until further notice.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Two questions.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Are the replacement rigs funded?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And what happens when one of those rigs goes out of service for repair?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is the first due area left unprotected?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Are you able to make out the screen or?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Maybe make it bigger.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: You know what?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yeah, we're going to bring you a copy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So you see the highlighted in yellow shows that these rigs are restricted from relocation under any circumstances.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So this is...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This deals with a contract provision with the fire officers union about the age of first frontline apparatus.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And due to delays, the global supply chain and delays during COVID, our manufacturer, Seagrave, which is in Wisconsin,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: is still backlogged on some of these.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I had visited the factory in November.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They had to lay off a significant number of their workers during COVID.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They went to hire them back, and most of them did not come back.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They are now up and running, and they have been for

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For a couple of years, they continue to produce and deliver our apparatus, but they are custom-made.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Nobody else in the country, no other fire department in the country, orders apparatus to our specifications because of the heavy-duty use we have, the roads that we have.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And they are all

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: effectively custom made.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We are continuing to receive them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They are being delivered.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On this list you have ladder 111, which we have, which will be going into service soon.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So in accordance with the contract, when

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: and we went through a grievance with the union on this, the grievance and the arbitration, the arbitrator decided that a relocation was not an emergency.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The department's position was opposite that, that relocations are emergencies.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So these apparatus

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: are not able to be used to relocate to cover other areas.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That does handicap the department at times because there are times we would like to use these to relocate.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So it's fair to say that the first due area is left unprotected if one of these rigs that are unable to be used are in that engine company?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: No, it's other areas.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They have to stay in their area.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They cannot be sent to relocate somewhere else.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So if they're operating at a fire, we can send another company to cover them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We cannot use any of these to be sent to relocate somewhere else.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And besides Ladder 111...

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: How many others are funded?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Are they all fully funded?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They're all funded and they've all been ordered on schedule.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All of the highlighted.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So just to add to that, this hasn't been a funding issue.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've put in the orders for the replacement apparatus on what would have been on time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In fact, we've been putting in the orders earlier and earlier, but because of the manufacturing delays, it has been taking longer for us to receive the vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But we do track the companies that are in outdated apparatus and in terms of when they're going to be resolved.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So there's about 51 outdated apparatus in the field right now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There are four pumpers.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're expecting that we'll have received those and they'll be in service later this calendar year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There are seven squads, seven high pressure pumpers, and four 75 foot tower ladders that we expect to be delivered and in service by the end of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There are two 95 foot tower ladders that we expect to be in service by the end of summer.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: There are eight tractor drawn aerials that we expect to be in service by early 2027.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the most backlogged are 23 rear mounts that we anticipate are about a year out.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Ariola presents photos of deteriorating conditions at multiple firehouses including Engine 59/Ladder 30 (water damage), Engine 262 (roof leak), Engine 4/Ladder 15 (crumbled wall), and Engine 229 (roof sinking). DeRacus confirms most repairs have been completed by trade staff or vendors.
chapters.title: Ariola presents firehouse deterioration photos: Engine 59, 262, 4/15, 229

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Now, again, on the screen, we're going to put up a series of photos, many of which you have seen before, and a few new ones.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The photos show water damage at Engine 59, Ladder 30 in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The picture shows water damage that has corroded the ceilings and walls of the firehouse.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: There is a pipe that clearly shows the water has been leaking out of it.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The water has damaged the steps inside the firehouse from the constant leak, and we see steps with stains and buildup.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Were these conditions remedied?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That's engine 59, ladder 30.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, so we've been working with our trade staff to address all these issues and to try and work on correcting them until we could get funding later on.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So you're working with the firefighters that are in there?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: No, no, no, with our trade staff.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Oh, the trade, okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Next, we have a roof leak at engine 262 in Astoria, Queens that has at least five work orders reported,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: In the pictures, we see a drop ceiling that is missing tiles from what looks like a leak.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The tiles are filled with heavy water stains.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: There is another picture of a ceiling covered with tiles that seem to be missing.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: There is a picture of a blue wall surrounding a window air conditioning unit with what appears to be the outlet of the unit left out of it.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: What is the status of these repairs?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, our trade staff has responded and repaired the roof on building 262, as well as replaced the ceiling tiles, and I believe they're currently working on the walls as we speak.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Next photo is of the first floor of engine four, ladder 15.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The first picture shows the crumbled support wall of the firehouse between the two apparatus doors.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Was that repaired?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, following the last budget hearing, we quickly went out there to assess it to determine that it's not a structural wall.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Our vendor was there and we completed that wall restoration, I believe, on May 15th.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The picture we're looking at now is the rear wall of the apparatus floor, which we also see crumbling.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: This is the same location.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: There are also many areas with ceiling tiles missing around the light fixtures with either water stain tiles surrounding the missing tiles, so we can assume the tiles were missing due to water damage from the leak.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Were these conditions corrected?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The same vendor who fixed the wall in between the apparatus doors also corrected that wall there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the ceiling tiles have been repaired.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And all replaced.

chapters.summary: Ariola presents photos of Ladder 78's unstable parapet wall with netting installed in 2023 but no repairs since, and Squad 288's buckling apparatus floor held up by floor jacks from 2001. DeRacus explains the parapet is stabilized and Squad 288 has window funding with apparatus floor funding in out-years being pursued for acceleration.
chapters.title: Ariola presents photos: Ladder 78 parapet and Squad 288 apparatus floor

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That is not my photo.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That's the previous photo, and he said it was corrected.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It should be engine 229.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: This is the roof of engine 229 ladder 146 in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The photo shows ceiling damage and the roof has sunk from its proper place.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The roof should be in line with the metal rail on the exterior wall.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The picture shows the roof about one foot below the metal rail.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: This was reported on 10-22 of 25.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The next picture shows water damage happening inside the firehouse above a cooling and heating unit.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The last time we were here, we also discussed the various work orders submitted for the Niedermann system.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: For those who do not know, the Niedermann is the vacuum they attach to the apparatus to exhaust

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: to the exhaust system to capture exhaust and diesel fumes.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The orders were submitted on 2-11-26, 2-18-26, and 2-26-26.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: You said earlier that all Niedermann systems have been repaired.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Is this one repaired?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That is correct.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I would also like to talk about the roof.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Yes, please.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: If you could show it again.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the roof has also been repaired.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The interior damage to ceilings has been repaired, and we are working on the walls.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I would like to explain a little bit about what you refer to as the roof sinking and that metal rail.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That detail is the proper detail.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It does not show that the roof is sinking.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That piece of aluminum flashing that you see right there exactly, that's called through wall flashing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And right below that, you see kind of like the roof member kind of curves up.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's an entire roof.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Parapet waterproofing detail that we try to mimic.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Actually, we install at every one of our new roofing projects.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That roof never sank, although the roof did need repair, and it has been repaired.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I believe I showed a very similar situation, the roof of Engine 329 on Friday to fill us when we were up on the roof.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So that is our standard condition that we try to

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, actually that we do build at all of our facilities that have masonry parapet walls similar to that.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, for the record, Phyllis Insurillo is my chief of staff and senior advisor.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Now we're going to our next photo, ladder 78.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Ladder 78 has put in for an unstable front parapet wall of the firehouse numerous times.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: In June of 2023, buildings units subcontracted out a company to put a protective netting over the unstable parapet wall.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: No repairs have been done since the netting has been put on the parapet.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Previous work orders were submitted on 6-21 of 22, 3-11 of 22, 12-6 of 21, and 1-25 of 2020.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Was this repair completed?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So that is engine company 155, and yes, we did stabilize the condition with that netting there.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This facility was also submitted as part of the roofing package that we had submitted.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We do believe that six of these projects have been funded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're not sure exactly which six.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We can prioritize which six.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we could definitely prioritize which of these six have funding.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And while we could prioritize that, we also have an outside vendor who is our engineering team working with us on the best approach.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The next picture is from Squad 288 in Maspeth.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The apparatus floor is buckling, patched with concrete.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The following picture shows how it is being held up by hoists that were installed in 2001, which are now also buckling and unsteady.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The apparatus floor holds rigs that weigh several tons.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: There is sometimes flooding in the basement, which also compromises the hoists.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: What is the status of the repairs here?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So for Squad 288, those are what we refer to as floor jacks.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: They are supporting the apparatus floor because the apparatus floor is in need of a replacement and an overall multi-component project.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Currently, we have funding to do windows at Squad 288, which we are moving forwards with once the funding is registered.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And the funding for the multi-component aspect of it, which would be the apparatus floor, that funding is in the outer years, which we are working with OWNB while we're doing the windows to try to accelerate that funding forward so we could get them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As close to simultaneous as possible.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Good.

chapters.summary: Ariola presents photos of Engine 290/Ladder 103's rest area with a gaping hole in the wall exposing brick and pipes, and a video showing water pouring from a ceiling pipe directly onto an oil burner. DeRacus says trade staff responded to address the leak, and the building needs complete roof restoration.
chapters.title: Ariola presents Engine 290 deterioration photos and leak video

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: This slide is the rest area for firefighters at Engine 290, Ladder 103 in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We see a red wall with a large gaping hole that exposes the brick of the building structure.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: There are pipes exposed as well.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: There is also water damage all along the wall, and there is a bed to the side.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: How do you think that this damage should be repaired?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That's how it was repaired.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: How do you think it should be repaired?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, I believe that that hole was made for us to access some of the piping behind the wall to address a leak.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Although Engine Company 290 is definitely one of those buildings that needs a complete roof restoration and building envelope restoration.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Again, this is one of those that is on the list where we received six of the...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Funding for and we will continue to prioritize this and hopefully we could get this one in there too.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Right, I just for the record have to note that the slide we're looking at is the current condition of the same red wall with the hole in it, but we now see a metal plate that has been placed by facilities over the hole.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The water is now pooling to the left of the metal plates and that is evident in the photo and you can see the paint bubbling directly above the powered outlet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, our video.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We'll get there.

chapters.summary: Ariola shows a video of water pouring from a pipe onto an oil burner at Engine 290 and asks why it's unrepaired. DeRacus says work orders are called in immediately and trade staff respond, but with 300 work orders per week he can't confirm this specific one. Ariola expresses frustration that facilities staff didn't bring a computer as requested.
chapters.title: Ariola confronts FDNY about unrepaired leak at Engine 290

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Okay, in this video, we see a pipe along the ceiling of Engine 290, Ladder 103 in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The pipe has water pouring from it, and when the camera pans down, you can see the water is leaking directly onto the oil burner.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Why has this gone unrepaired?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's hard to say that it's gone unrepaired.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Whenever something like that happens, a work order is called in immediately to our resource center.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And depending on what the issue is, on an order like that, we would have our plumbers respond, as well as maybe our oilers, which would be the HVAC guys, to make sure that that boiler is operational.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I wouldn't say it's going unrepaired if it's an active leak like that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm not exactly sure what type of pipe that specifically is, but the water does look pretty clean.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I'm not going to say it's a sewer pipe.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I just don't know from that picture.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Have you received a work order for that particular engine and ladder company and that particular leak?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: With 300 work orders a week, it's pretty difficult for me to answer that question specifically.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: You were asked to bring a computer today so that you could look up.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Do not have it.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Why would you not have it when a specific request was made for facilities to bring a computer so that you could look up the work orders?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I just do not have it with me.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I could definitely let you know about this work order.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Commissioner, we let Jason know very pointedly that we needed for facilities to have a computer with them so that they could look it up so that we could know

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: what's been done and if they had received a work order.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And the fact that that request from this committee was completely disregarded is unacceptable and disrespectful.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Can I just say it?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The request was not disregarded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I just didn't have the computer readily available to bring today.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Come on now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: No, I'm not going to accept that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I do have a staffer back at the office who unfortunately is not there at the moment.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This might be her texting me right now.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: No, she unfortunately had to leave the office for a family emergency.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I do not have that specific information for you yet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're happy to get you the information that you need.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We can't change, unfortunately, we can't change the fact we don't have the computer.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We did have somebody at the office that could have answered it, but they're gone.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Well, that was the reason for the computer.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That's why the S was made in advance.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And it was made...

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: To Jason Shelley and Jason, you know, you've been around long enough to understand that when I ask for something for this committee, I expect it to be provided.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And if it cannot be provided, I need to know ahead of time.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: But in situations like this where it's expected,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: You know, I don't have it because there was not a computer available to me is not an answer.

chapters.summary: Ariola delivers closing remarks criticizing the administration for allowing firehouses to deteriorate, noting 100-year-old buildings haven't had investment unless council members provided it, and calling conditions unacceptable. She states the committee will check on all presented facilities and raise the issue at the OMB hearing.
chapters.title: Ariola delivers impassioned closing on firehouse deterioration

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: On that note, we'll be checking on each of the slides that we saw to make sure that the work is being done.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: But I think anybody with eyes can see that the work being done

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: is just a band-aid.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It's just a band-aid and the facilities are falling apart and our firefighters and our EMS workers are working in substandard conditions.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They are living there.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: The better part of their days and nights are spent at the EMS stations and at the fire stations and they are living in worse conditions than we would allow

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: anyone to live in.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And as I said earlier, if you as the fire department were called to inspect those buildings or the DOB went down to inspect those buildings, they would be closed.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So shame on this administration.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Commissioner, I know that you are new and you are doing everything you can to try and remedy this, but shame on anyone who's been here for a long time and has let this go by year after year after year without fixing

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: the locations where your firefighters, you as firefighters, have let it go this far, that 100-year-old buildings have not had a nickel put into it unless a council member provided it.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And then even then, money is held up with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It's totally unacceptable and we will bring that up at the OMB hearing.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: If no one, if you have anything to add, Council Chair Lee?

chapters.summary: Chair Lee thanks FDNY for extending their time, acknowledges Chair Ariola's passion, and expresses understanding of the difficult capital process while committing to partnership. She closes the hearing for the day.
chapters.title: Chair Lee closes FDNY hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, I just wanted to thank you all for extending your time because I know that we are past the time and thank you for sticking around and answering all of our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I admire Chair Areola's passion, and I hope you understand that it's, as someone who's been through the capital process before with the city on the nonprofit side, I understand it's a horrible process that's very slow.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There's a lot of regulations, tons of regulations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But, you know, Chair Areola, myself, and I think all the other council members, we do want to make sure that

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: we understand your priorities, that we're able to support it where we can, and that we are partners with you because we do care, as you obviously just heard from Chair Areola, about the firefighters that are serving our city and keeping us safe.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wanted to thank you for being here today and for the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think if there's nothing else, I think we are done for the day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee, for your partnership.
